Renal biopsy demonstrates granular deposition of immunoglobulin G (IgG) along the glomerular basement membranes, as observed by immunofluorescence microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate with numerous plasma cells within a fibrotic interstitium, as demonstrated by H&E staining. These findings are diagnostic of IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis, in the context of membranous nephropathy.
Diffuse glomerular deposition of fibrillary, comma-shaped, electron-dense organized deposits observed on electron microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis.

Light microscopy demonstrates collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis characterized by segmental collapse of glomerular capillary loops and associated podocyte hyperplasia. Adjacent segments show mesangial hypercellularity, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Acute granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by aggregates of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells within the renal interstitium, in the absence of necrosis. The morphologic findings are diagnostic of a drug-induced etiology, specifically attributable to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agent exposure.

Electron microscopy reveals collagen fiber hyperplasia and haphazardly arranged fibrous tissue within the renal interstitium, consistent with advanced interstitial fibrosis. These interstitial alterations are accompanied by a prior diagnosis of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, which is likely the primary driver of the observed fibrotic changes.

MUC1 immunoreactivity is observed in collecting tubules, renal vesicles, comma-shaped bodies, and S-shaped bodies in the developing human kidney. Glomeruli are distinctly negative for MUC1 immunostaining at 40 hours post-fertilization (hpf), confirming the absence of MUC1 expression in mature glomerular structures at this stage.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a crescentic glomerulonephritis pattern, characterized by the presence of cellular or fibrocellular crescents within Bowman's space, as indicated by arrows on the histopathological slide. The findings are diagnostic of necrotizing glomerulonephritis secondary to ANCA-associated vasculitis, given the clinical context of pulmonary-renal syndrome.

Hematoxylin and eosin staining reveals a morphologically normal renal glomerulus in a rat kidney specimen. The glomerular capillary architecture, cellular composition, and surrounding Bowman’s space appear unremarkable, with no evidence of cellular proliferation, inflammatory infiltration, or structural disruption; the absence of mesangial hypercellularity effectively rules out early diabetic glomerulopathy.
Renal pathology revealed crescentic glomerulonephritis. Approximately 25% of glomeruli were globally sclerosed, consistent with chronic irreversible damage secondary to the active crescentic process.

Renal tissue examination revealed no histopathological evidence of toxicity. The glomeruli, tubules, interstitium, and vasculature exhibited normal morphology without signs of acute tubular injury, interstitial inflammation, glomerular basement membrane thickening, or vascular abnormalities.

Expansive renal tumor infiltrating the spinal region with compression of adjacent parenchyma. Histologic examination reveals multiple cellular nests composed of blastemal cells, surrounded by a neoplastic stroma of spindled to stellate cells, consistent with Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma).

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following early exposure to oral cadmium reveals initial tubular epithelial injury predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Light microscopy demonstrates cytoplasmic vacuolization, loss of brush border integrity, and focal areas of tubular epithelial cell degeneration with minimal interstitial inflammation, consistent with early-stage acute tubular necrosis.

Renal biopsy findings: Single glomerulus demonstrates focal segmental hypercellularity, associated with glomerular basement membrane thickening and presence of hyaline deposits. These findings are diagnostic of early diabetic glomerulosclerosis; however, immune complex-mediated glomerulopathy remains a less likely possibility that would require further clinical correlation.

Tongue: Foreign body granuloma involving the lamina propria and skeletal muscle layer, characterized by a chronic inflammatory infiltrate with multinucleated giant cells surrounding a refractile foreign material consistent with a hair fragment. The presence of granulomatous inflammation within the muscular layer suggests possible extension from an underlying systemic granulomatous disorder, although no additional clinical evidence supports this.

Photomicroscopic examination reveals a vascular lesion composed of variably sized blood vessels exhibiting heterogeneous wall thickness, consistent with arteriovenous malformation. Arterial components are identified by the presence of an internal elastic lamina, highlighted by Elastica-van Gieson stain (arrowheads), confirming the diagnosis of a high-flow arteriovenous malformation.
Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular abnormalities characterized by mesangial hypercellularity and expansion of the mesangial matrix. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis.

Chronic kidney disease is characterized by advanced stage at initial clinical presentation. Patients frequently present with established renal impairment, consistent with delayed referral patterns and often indicative of irreversible glomerulosclerosis.

Kidney biopsy: A slightly enlarged glomerulus demonstrates diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane and mesangial interposition (solid arrow), diagnostic of membranous lupus nephropathy. Focal and segmental mesangial expansion is present, with mild glomerular hypercellularity (open arrow) on PAS-silver stain.

Membranous glomerulonephropathy is identified in a Hatano low-avoidance rat. Indirect immunofluorescence using serum from the affected rat on normal Sprague-Dawley rat pancreatic tissue demonstrates weak cytoplasmic reactivity within islet cells, consistent with autoantibody-mediated beta-cell injury characteristic of early autoimmune diabetes.
Multilocular cystic renal cell carcinoma is identified. Adjacent renal parenchyma demonstrates tubular degenerative changes, thyroidization of glomeruli, and interstitial fibrosis.

Kidney biopsy, hematoxylin and eosin staining (×200):  
The renal parenchyma shows diffuse interstitial infiltration by atypical myeloid and monocytic cells, consistent with leukemic involvement. The architecture of the glomeruli is preserved, with no morphological evidence of glomerular injury.

Not applicable.

The provided text describes an experimental imaging setup for studying neural odor responses in honeybees and does not contain any pathological findings or tissue-based diagnostic information. Therefore, no renal pathology description can be generated from this content; however, the absence of glomerular structures in the preparation confirms it is non-renal tissue.

Electron microscopy demonstrates ultrastructural findings consistent with early focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), characterized by segmental podocyte foot process effacement and subepithelial electron-dense deposits. These features are diagnostic of recurrent FSGS in the renal allograft.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates an essentially normal glomerulus with open capillary lumens and minimal hypercellularity, without significant glomerular basement membrane thickening, endocapillary proliferation, crescent formation, or necrosis. The absence of immune complex deposits confirms the lack of immune-mediated glomerulonephritis, and no thrombi are identified.

Leukocyte-derived chemotaxin 2 (LECT2) amyloidosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposits involving the glomeruli, with deposition observed in the mesangial matrix, glomerular capillary walls, and renal interstitium. The pattern of involvement is characteristic of LECT2 amyloidosis and confirms the diagnosis without the need for additional immunohistochemical or mass spectrometry studies.

Kidney section from a dog with visceral leishmaniosis demonstrates marked lymphoplasmacytic interstitial nephritis. The glomerulus, along with both afferent and efferent arterioles, is effaced by granulomatous inflammatory infiltration, consistent with leishmanial granulomas.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular basement membrane thickening and mesangial proliferation. These findings are characteristic of immune-mediated glomerulonephritis and are most consistent with active systemic lupus erythematosus, particularly in the appropriate clinical context.

Kidney tissue section demonstrates tubular epithelial swelling and cytoplasmic vacuolization. Glomeruli exhibit increased cellularity and marked reduction or obliteration of Bowman’s space, consistent with early crescent formation.

Photomicrograph demonstrates diffuse, high-grade interstitial positivity for alpha-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA), indicative of myofibroblast activation within the renal interstitium. This finding confirms the presence of established interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy, which are diagnostic of advanced IgA nephropathy.
Photomicrograph of a glomerulus from a control non-diabetic rat, demonstrating normal glomerular architecture. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain.

Microscopic examination reveals normal glomeruli in renal parenchyma adjacent to a focus of invasive squamous cell carcinoma. The neoplasm is composed of well-differentiated squamous epithelial cells exhibiting keratinization and intercellular bridges, arranged in solid nests and trabeculae, consistent with primary renal squamous cell carcinoma arising from metaplastic urothelium.

Zymography SDS-PAGE analysis was performed to evaluate vesicle-associated gelatinolytic activity in urinary extracellular vesicles. One microgram of protein, quantified by Bradford assay, was loaded per sample lane, confirming the presence of active matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) based on the observed lytic bands.

Uterus: Adenomyosis (endometriosis interna) is present, characterized by the presence of ectopic endometrial glands and stromal cells within the myometrial layer. This finding is consistent with active endometriosis and is observed incidentally in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*) undergoing toxicity studies.

Section of glandular stomach demonstrating a gastric ulcer. The lesion is characterized by disruption of the mucosal epithelium, loss of surface integrity, and associated inflammatory infiltrate within the underlying lamina propria and submucosa, consistent with active *Helicobacter pylori* infection.

Immunoperoxidase labeling of liver tissue sections using antibody F59 demonstrates specific expression of sarcomeric myosin heavy chain (MyHC) in perisinusoidal cells, consistent with hepatic stellate cells. No immunoreactivity is observed in hepatocytes or other parenchymal cell types, confirming that MyHC expression is exclusive to the hepatic stellate cell lineage in the liver.

Microscopic examination of the renal cortex reveals intraepithelial lymphocytic infiltration within tubules, accompanied by an erythrocytic cast associated with attenuated tubular epithelium. Glomeruli appear morphologically normal, ruling out glomerulonephritis as a contributing factor.
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with lymphocytic tubulitis

answer_text: Spleen: Hyalinization of germinal centers characterized by deposition of eosinophilic material within follicles. This finding is commonly observed in cynomolgus monkeys and is considered a non-specific histological change indicative of chronic antigenic stimulation.

Membranoproliferative glomerulonephropathy associated with cardiac angiosarcoma. Liver biopsy, H&E stain: demonstrates metastatic angiosarcoma characterized by infiltrates of hyperchromatic spindle cells within hepatic parenchyma, consistent with hematogenous spread from the primary cardiac tumor.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly describes angiosarcoma cells in the liver without specifying whether this represents metastasis.

Glomeruli contain bright fuchsinophilic, round to ovoid crystalline inclusions within endothelial cell cytoplasm, surrounded by basement membranes highlighted in blue on Masson’s trichrome stain (×100). These crystalline deposits are consistent with amyloidosis despite being negative for amyloid on Congo red staining (not shown).

Kidney tissue in green tea-treated Wistar rats showed near-normal histological architecture by day 45 of the experiment, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at 400× magnification. This finding indicates complete reversal of gamma-hexachlorocyclohexane (lindane)-induced renal tubular necrosis, with full structural and functional recovery evident in the treatment group.

Diffuse hemorrhages involving the splenic parenchyma are present, as demonstrated by histopathological examination of splenic tissue stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The hemorrhagic changes are widespread and disrupt the normal architecture of the red pulp, consistent with acute splenic infarction.

Renal biopsy findings:  
The renal interstitium is mildly edematous and infiltrated by mature lymphocytes with occasional eosinophils, consistent with acute interstitial nephritis. Glomeruli appear morphologically normal.

Irregular and markedly thickened glomerular basement membranes are evident on light microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Silver stain highlights prominent vacuolar changes within the glomerular basement membranes, indicative of early diabetic nephropathy.

Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates heterogeneous downregulation of angiotensin II AT1-A and AT1-B receptors in renal arterioles of streptozotocin (STZ)-induced diabetic rats. Specifically, AT1-A receptor expression is reduced on renin-positive smooth muscle cells of the afferent arteriole compared to normal controls, with decreased immunoreactivity observed at the cell surface, suggesting impaired renin-angiotensin system signaling as a primary driver of early diabetic nephropathy in this model.

Renal biopsy demonstrates pathological deposition of free κ light chains within the mesangial matrix and tubular basement membranes, as demonstrated by immunoperoxidase staining using a sheep polyclonal antibody specific for free κ light chains. The absence of λ light chain staining confirms a diagnosis of light chain deposition disease restricted to κ isotype.

Lupus nephritis, class IV-G (global proliferative), with diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity, prominent neutrophilic infiltration, and numerous wire-loop lesions. Immunofluorescence reveals full-house immune complex deposition (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q) along capillary loops and mesangium, consistent with active systemic lupus erythematosus–related glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates circumferential extracapillary crescents with associated glomerular tuft collapse (floccular crushing), as evidenced by hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×400 magnification. Crescents are composed of cellular proliferation and fibrinoid material, consistent with active pauci-immune glomerulonephritis; the presence of fibrinoid necrosis confirms this diagnosis.

Light microscopy reveals granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by a poorly formed granuloma containing foreign body-type multinucleated giant cells. The lesion is identified within the renal interstitium and is consistent with sarcoidosis.

Collagenous nodules are identified within the renal interstitium, exhibiting a peritubular distribution and prominent sclerosis. These structures are highlighted by Masson trichrome stain, which demonstrates abundant blue-staining collagenous matrix consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Glomerulus markedly enlarged with endocapillary and mesangial hypercellularity, resulting in occlusion of capillary lumina. Neutrophilic exudate is present within glomerular tufts, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting cellular crescent formation. Crescents are composed of proliferating parietal epithelial cells and infiltrating mononuclear inflammatory cells, with evidence of circumferential involvement of Bowman’s space, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate marked global thickening of the glomerular basement membranes, which is homogeneous and eosinophilic, with segmental accentuation. Occasional glomerular capillary lumina contain homogeneous eosinophilic globules, consistent with immune complex deposition.

Spleen parenchyma exhibits a large thrombus within a splenic blood vessel, associated with vascular congestion and multifocal hemosiderin deposition. Multifocal aggregates of macrophages are present throughout the parenchyma, consistent with chronic infarction secondary to the thrombus.

Light microscopy reveals eosinophilic deposits within the mesangial and subendothelial regions of the glomeruli. These findings are diagnostic of C3 glomerulonephritis. The observed glomerular pathology is directly caused by underlying multiple myeloma.

Electron microscopy reveals marked thickening of the renal arteriolar and glomerular capillary walls due to deposition of non-branching, randomly oriented fibrils measuring 8–10 nm in diameter—diagnostic of amyloidosis. These deposits are Congo red–positive with apple-green birefringence under polarized light (not shown).

Prussian blue stain demonstrates strong, diffuse deposition of coarse blue granules within the renal tubular epithelial cells, consistent with hemosiderin accumulation. This finding is diagnostic of renal hemosiderosis.

Immunofluorescence staining for nephritis-associated plasmin receptor (NAPlr) in renal tissue from a patient with acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis demonstrates granular deposition of NAPlr antigen primarily within the mesangium and along the entire length of the glomerular basement membrane. A ring-like granular staining pattern is also observed in association with infiltrating leukocytes, consistent with direct immune complex–mediated injury.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates recurrent immunoglobulin A nephropathy with crescentic glomerulonephritis, evident in two consecutive episodes. Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals intense granular deposition of immunoglobulin A within the mesangial areas and capillary walls of glomeruli, consistent with active immune complex–mediated injury driving crescent formation.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately links IgA nephropathy with crescentic transformation but stops short of asserting a direct mechanistic relationship.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is present, characterized by segmental glomerular sclerosis involving a subset of glomeruli. The affected glomerulus demonstrates segmental collapse of the capillary tuft with increased mesangial matrix and hyalinosis, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy (PAS stain, ×200).

Light microscopy and immunohistochemical staining for type IV collagen reveal epithelial hyperplasia and fibrosis in the gingival tissue. Focal discontinuities of the basement membrane underlying the squamous epithelium are identified at several sites (arrows), consistent with a diagnosis of Alport syndrome.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse necrotizing and crescentic glomerulonephritis with widespread disruption of glomerular tufts. Cellular crescents and fibrinoid necrosis are present in multiple glomeruli, consistent with severe immune-mediated glomerular injury, most likely due to anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.
Pulmonary-renal syndrome with absence of electron-dense immune complex deposits on electron microscopy. Findings are diagnostic of pauci-immune glomerulonephritis.

Immunofluorescence assay on HEp-2 cells demonstrates a speckled nuclear staining pattern of variable intensity at a dilution of 1:40, consistent with anti-proliferating cell nuclear antigen (anti-PCNA) antibodies. This finding is diagnostic of systemic lupus erythematosus and warrants immediate clinical correlation to confirm disease activity.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence demonstrated diffuse, 3+ fine granular mesangial deposits of immunoglobulin M (IgM), consistent with primary IgM nephropathy. No other immunoreactants were mentioned in the provided description.

Foci of squamous cell carcinoma are present within the renal parenchyma, demonstrating well-defined keratinizing squamous differentiation with formation of characteristic squamous pearls. Normal renal tubular structures are entrapped within the tumor focus, confirming primary origin of the carcinoma within the kidney parenchyma.

Gallbladder: Mild lymphoplasmacytic infiltration is present within the lamina propria, consistent with early chronic cholecystitis. Focal inflammatory cell infiltration is observed, with occasional development of well-formed lymphoid follicles.

Electron microscopy demonstrates coarsely granular, electron-dense deposits localized to the outer aspect of the tubular basement membranes. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of light chain deposition disease.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis with ultrastructural evidence of immune complex deposition. Electron microscopy reveals electron-dense deposits corresponding to silver granules scattered within the mesangial matrix and glomerular basement membranes (arrows), consistent with argyria.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly identifies the deposits as silver granules related to argyria—a non-immune, exogenous pigment deposition.

Immunofluorescence assay on HEp-2 cells demonstrates a speckled pattern of nuclear and nucleolar staining, consistent with the presence of anti-SSA/Ro antibodies at a dilution of 1:40, which is diagnostic of Sjögren’s syndrome.

Renal biopsy specimen examined by electron microscopy demonstrates abundant subendothelial deposits composed of large fibrillar structures measuring approximately 97 nm in diameter. These fibers exhibit long-spacing striations and morphological characteristics consistent with collagen, predominantly involving the glomerular capillary walls, and are diagnostic of collagenofibrotic glomerulopathy.

Abdominal radiograph demonstrates multiple calcifications consistent with bladder calculi. The location and morphology of the calcifications are diagnostic of urolithiasis originating from the renal pelvis, with subsequent migration into the bladder.

Proliferative glomerulonephritis with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition in a renal allograft. Glomerular capillary walls are markedly thickened and distorted due to extensive subepithelial electron-dense deposits.
lesion_type: proliferative glomerulonephritis with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition

answer_text: Renal tubular dysfunction with associated abnormalities in calcium metabolism observed in individuals exposed to cadmium. Findings are consistent with acquired proximal tubular injury, which directly causes impaired renal calcium reabsorption and consequent systemic hypocalcemia.

Immunoperoxidase staining for fibulin-1 was performed on laser capture microdissected glomeruli to characterize glomerular disease patterns.

- Normal glomerulus from a baseline allograft biopsy demonstrates absence of mesangial staining.  
- In fibronectin glomerulopathy, an enlarged glomerulus exhibits mild but diffuse mesangial staining.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates marked arterial disease with associated chronic ischemic glomerular changes and background tubulointerstitial fibrosis. Arterial lesions are characterized by significant intimal thickening and luminal narrowing, consistent with chronic vascular injury due to long-standing hypertension.

Electron microscopy revealed electron-dense deposits located both epimembranously and intramembranously along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with immune complex deposition typical of membranous nephropathy. Podocyte foot processes were diffusely effaced.

Photomicrographs of renal tissue sections from Wistar rats exposed to nickel sulfate and maintained on a 10% Moringa oleifera-supplemented diet, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at 400× magnification, demonstrate preservation of glomerular and tubular architecture. No significant histopathological alterations, such as tubular necrosis, glomerular congestion, or interstitial inflammation, are observed, indicating that Moringa oleifera supplementation fully prevents nickel sulfate–induced nephrotoxicity.

Electron microscopy demonstrates endothelial tubuloreticular inclusions and a slightly thickened glomerular basement membrane. Podocyte foot processes show diffuse effacement, consistent with lupus nephritis.

Renal biopsy specimen shows glomerular abnormalities characterized by mesangial expansion with increased mesangial matrix and hyalinization of the glomerular basement membranes, consistent with diabetic nephropathy. Capillary loops are thickened.
Microphotograph (×400) reveals mild tubular epithelial desquamation and focal necrosis in the renal cortex of a rat from group II, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Necrotizing vasculitis involving the renal parenchyma is present, characterized by fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls, transmural inflammatory infiltration, and disruption of vascular architecture. The lesion is associated with crescentic glomerulonephritis, showing circumferential cellular crescents with evidence of glomerular necrosis, consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis presenting as atypical post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates dominant IgG staining in a granular pattern within glomerular capillary walls, consistent with immune complex deposition and diagnostic of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.

Acute renal allograft dysfunction associated with cecal volvulus. Abdominal and pelvic CT imaging revealed marked cecal dilatation and concurrent proximal ureteral dilatation of the renal allograft, consistent with extrinsic compression by the distended cecum causing mechanical obstruction of the ureter.

Differentially expressed long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) were identified in kidney tissue from male low birth weight rats, induced by maternal protein restriction, compared to control rats at postnatal days 1 and 10. Hierarchical clustering analysis was performed on lncRNA expression profiles using log2-transformed data, with differential expression defined as an absolute fold-change ≥2 and p ≤ 0.05, indicating a causative role of these lncRNAs in the development of renal dysfunction associated with low birth weight.

Renal tissue shows granular deposits within the glomerular capillary lumina, mesangial areas, and cytoplasm of renal tubular epithelial cells, consistent with immune complex deposition. Focal patchy necrosis of the renal tubular epithelium is present, indicative of acute tubular injury secondary to immune-mediated damage.
lesion_type: immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis with acute tubular injury

answer_text: Histologic examination of the kidney reveals deposition of gouty crystals within the renal tubules, associated with tubular inflammatory cell infiltration. Additional findings include interstitial edema, fibrin deposition, and fibroplasia, consistent with acute urate nephropathy.

Renal cortical necrosis is present, characterized by coagulative necrosis of glomeruli and tubules. Glomerular capillary loops contain intraluminal brown pigment deposition, consistent with hemosiderin-laden macrophages indicative of prior hemorrhage.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, diagnostic of ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Histopathological findings include mild tubular atrophy.

Histopathological examination reveals globally sclerosed glomerular tufts with segmental endocapillary hypercellularity. Tubular atrophy is present, accompanied by loss of tubular brush borders. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy with advanced chronic changes.

Tongue: Focal regenerative changes are present in skeletal muscle fibers of the lateral tongue. These findings are characterized by muscle fiber regeneration, including features such as enlarged nuclei, eosinophilic cytoplasm, and increased nuclear density, consistent with prior minor injury and subsequent repair.

Lichen planus: A band-like lymphocytic infiltrate is present at the dermo-epidermal junction, associated with vacuolar degeneration of the basal layer and pigment incontinence. The epidermis exhibits a saw-toothed rete ridge pattern, consistent with chronic lichenoid interface dermatitis.

Parathyroid: Cyst (Kürsteiner’s cyst). The cyst is lined by cuboidal epithelium, occasionally ciliated, and contains proteinaceous material within the lumen, consistent with a developmental remnant of the third pharyngeal pouch.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description correctly identifies a Kürsteiner’s cyst—a benign parathyroid cyst—without speculating on its embryologic origin.

Peripheral blood smear demonstrates multiple cytoplasmic lipid vacuoles within neutrophils, consistent with neutrophilic lipid inclusions. This finding is diagnostic of a lipid storage disorder, such as Chanarin-Dorfman syndrome, particularly given the presence of these inclusions in circulating neutrophils.
Histopathological examination of the renal mass demonstrates abortive tubule formation and glomerular differentiation, diagnostic of Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma).

Kidney biopsy shows segmental glomerulosclerosis and karyomegaly in tubular epithelial cells. The glomerular lesion is characterized by focal segmental sclerosis involving a subset of glomeruli, consistent with primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

The accessory olfactory bulb (AOB) is located on the dorsomedial aspect of the caudal main olfactory bulb. It receives sensory afferents directly from the main olfactory epithelium via the vomeronasal nerve.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates medullary-predominant inflammatory infiltrate composed predominantly of neutrophils with a minority of mononuclear cells, including occasional plasma cells. There is associated congestion of peritubular capillaries and red blood cell extravasation, consistent with acute pyelonephritis.

The specimen demonstrates extensive calcification involving the posterior aspect of the mitral annulus, extending into the adjacent left ventricular wall and papillary muscles. The calcific deposits are dense and involve the structural support apparatus of the posterior mitral leaflet, consistent with severe mitral annular calcification leading to secondary mitral regurgitation.

The specimen demonstrates immunohistochemical expression of cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1), cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), microsomal prostaglandin E synthase-1 (mPGES-1), and microsomal prostaglandin E synthase-2 (mPGES-2) within renal, pulmonary, and cardiac tissues in the context of experimental hypertension. Expression is observed in glomerular epithelial cells, vascular endothelium, tubular epithelium, cardiomyocytes, and alveolar macrophages.

Fibrous gingival enlargement with anterior tooth migration, suggestive of underlying periodontitis despite the absence of visible alveolar bone loss.

Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates enlarged podocytes with prominent cytoplasmic vacuolation, consistent with lipid-laden epithelial cells diagnostic of minimal change disease. Vacuolated changes are also present in a subset of tubular epithelial cells.

Light microscopy reveals glomerular capillary basement membranes of relatively normal thickness, without significant hypercellularity or matrix expansion (silver methenamine stain; magnification ×400). The findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Renal biopsy shows acute tubular injury characterized by degenerative and regenerative changes in the tubular epithelium. Intratubular amorphous deposits with morphological features consistent with calcium oxalate crystal deposition are present.
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with calcium oxalate crystal deposition

answer_text: Eye: Cataract involving the lens. Histopathological examination reveals swelling and disorganized arrangement of lens fibers, with focal disruption of intercellular junctions at the posterior pole, consistent with posterior subcapsular cataract formation.

Electron microscopy reveals glomerular capillary loops exhibiting endothelial cell swelling (thick arrow) and luminal occlusion by cellular elements, cell debris, and electron-dense material, consistent with fibrin deposition. Focal duplication of the glomerular basement membrane is present (double thin arrow), indicative of chronic thrombotic microangiopathy.

Photomicrograph (PAS stain, ×40) demonstrates PAS-positive material within dilated renal tubules. The absence of glomerular structures in the field precludes evaluation for collapsing glomerulopathy; however, given the tubular changes observed, a diagnosis of early or incipient collapsing glomerulopathy cannot be excluded.

Nephrotic-range proteinuria is associated with crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by c-ANCA positivity and linear immunoglobulin deposition along the glomerular basement membranes. Light microscopy reveals fibrocellular crescents involving at least one glomerulus, consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Histopathology: Spinal cord – Melanin pigment deposition is present within the meninges and perivascular tissues of the vascular wall. This finding is consistent with melanosis and is considered a pathological process that may contribute to neuroinflammation in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*), particularly when observed in the central nervous system.

Testis: Immature (Grade 1). There is a markedly reduced number of spermatozoa; however, spermatogenesis is present and progresses to the spermatid stage in nearly all seminiferous tubules, indicating intact Sertoli cell function and likely normal Leydig cell activity.

Acute interstitial nephritis characterized by a mononuclear cell infiltrate within the renal interstitium. Oxalate crystals are present within renal tubules and are likely the primary cause of the interstitial inflammation.

Ultrastructural examination reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis characterized by diffuse podocyte foot process effacement (indicated by thin black arrows) and intracytoplasmic protein reabsorption droplets within podocytes (white arrow). Endothelial cells demonstrate tubuloreticular inclusions, as seen in the upper right-hand field (star), consistent with lupus nephritis.
Renal injury secondary to atheroembolic disease is often indistinguishable from contrast-induced nephropathy and should be diagnosed based on the presence of cholesterol crystals in renal arterioles.

Liver: Scattered hepatocytes contain intracytoplasmic eosinophilic inclusion bodies, appearing as rounded droplets. These inclusions are interpreted as intracellular accumulation of blood plasma, consistent with early-stage alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency.

Kidney: Mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis, characterized by diffuse and marked enlargement of glomeruli, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin (HE) staining. Glomerular changes are associated with mesangial hypercellularity and expansion, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy specimen shows global glomerular sclerosis characterized by marked expansion of the mesangial and capillary luminal spaces by acellular, eosinophilic, hyaline extracellular matrix. Capillary tuft collapse is associated with synechiae formation between the glomerular tuft and Bowman’s capsule, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates coarse granular IgA deposition with strong intensity (+3) localized to the glomerular capillary walls. The pattern is consistent with immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis, and the dominant IgA staining is diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

Light microscopy (PAS stain, 250×) demonstrates a circumferential cellular crescent with marked compression of the glomerular capillary tuft. The finding is diagnostic of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis due to active anti-glomerular basement membrane disease, confirming a relapse of Goodpasture’s disease.

Renal biopsy reveals intramembranous electron-dense deposits on ultrastructural examination, consistent with membranous nephropathy. The patient presents with nephrotic syndrome and a clinical history of mixed connective tissue disease, which is likely the underlying cause of the observed glomerular pathology.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate collapsing glomerulopathy characterized by collapse of the glomerular capillary loops with hyperplasia of visceral epithelial cells (podocytes). Associated tubular abnormalities include microcystic dilatation of tubules, particularly in the cortex, consistent with advanced chronic kidney disease.

Photomicrograph of renal glomerulus in a diabetic rat following four months of disease duration with two months of insulin treatment demonstrates partial recovery of glomerular structural changes. Glomerular enlargement is reduced, vascular dilation is improved, and the glomerular basement membrane shows near-normal architecture, consistent with early reversal of diabetic nephropathy.

Kidney, rat – histological examination (H&E stain, 100× magnification): Normal renal architecture with preserved glomerular and tubulointerstitial structures. No evidence of acute tubular injury, glomerular abnormalities, or interstitial inflammation; findings are consistent with early diabetic nephropathy given the absence of overt pathology.

Renal cortex preparations demonstrate successful Cre-recombinase-mediated deletion of floxed PDGF-B and PDGFR-β alleles, as confirmed by PCR genotyping. Deletion fragments (Δ) are detected at 540 bp for PDGF-B (lane 3) and 350 bp for PDGFR-β (lane 5), consistent with complete recombination in all renal cell types.

Liver section demonstrates fatty change, intrahepatic cholestasis, and bile duct proliferation. Mild to moderate lymphocytic infiltration is present within the portal tracts, consistent with chronic biliary obstruction.

Histopathological examination reveals mild glomerular abnormalities in the majority of glomeruli, as demonstrated by Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. Findings include features consistent with nephroangiosclerosis and definitive focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Renal biopsy demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting circumferential cellular crescent formation and diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity, characterized by proliferation of endothelial and mesangial cells with infiltration of occasional leukocytes and red blood cells. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Glomerulus exhibiting extensive deposition of birefringent amyloid material within the mesangium and glomerular basement membranes, as demonstrated by Congo red staining with polarization. The amyloid deposits are associated with architectural distortion of the glomerular tuft and are diagnostic of systemic amyloidosis.

Photomicrograph of a gastric section from a control Wistar rat exposed to distilled water, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). No significant histopathological abnormalities are observed in the mucosal epithelium; the surface lining appears intact with no evidence of inflammation or metaplasia, consistent with normal gastric mucosa.

Increased interstitial inflammation is evident in the kidneys of double transgenic rats, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical labeling for ED-1, a marker of monocyte/macrophage infiltration. Prominent ED-1-positive cells are present within the renal interstitium, confirming angiotensin II–mediated inflammatory cellular recruitment as the primary driver of tubulointerstitial injury in this model.

Kidney section from the group receiving 5,500 shock waves over three sessions demonstrates focal, minimal tubular atrophy. One atrophic tubule contains a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive hyaline cast, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Photomicrograph of pancreatic tissue from a rat co-exposed to zinc and glyphosate (375 mg/kg) shows hyperplastic acinar cells and distension of the interlobular septa by transudate, as demonstrated on H&E staining. These findings are consistent with early-stage pancreatitis, although no additional specific inflammatory or degenerative changes are described.

The provided text does not describe a human renal pathology specimen or biopsy and contains no findings related to kidney morphology, glomerular structure, tubulointerstitial pathology, or vascular abnormalities. The material referenced involves experimental observations of silver deposits in the spleen of mice within a non-clinical, pharmacological study; however, the presence of such deposits raises concern for potential nephrotoxic effects that may indirectly manifest as early glomerular injury, despite the absence of direct renal tissue examination.

Renal cortical necrosis is present, characterized by ischemic necrosis involving the renal cortex with preservation of medullary structures. The lesion affects cortical arterioles and arcuate arteries, with associated thrombotic microangiopathy, consistent with a diagnosis of hemolytic uremic syndrome.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates a shrunken (collapsed) glomerulus with segmental or global collapse of the capillary tuft, accompanied by overlying podocyte hyperplasia and hypertrophy, diagnostic of collapsing glomerulopathy. Adjacent renal parenchyma shows marked tubular dilation in proximity to the affected glomerulus, likely representing early cystic change secondary to the glomerular pathology.

Photomicrograph demonstrates a collapsed glomerulus with segmental or global capillary loop collapse and associated podocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia, diagnostic of collapsing glomerulopathy. Adjacent renal parenchyma shows severe interstitial injury characterized by tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and mononuclear inflammatory infiltrate.

Renal biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposition within the glomeruli. Immunoperoxidase staining using a rabbit antibody to κ light chains, performed following automated antigen retrieval, reveals extensive immunoreactivity that colocalizes with the amyloid deposits, supporting a diagnosis of AL amyloidosis of κ light chain type.
lesion_type: al amyloidosis, κ light chain type

answer_text: Renal biopsy demonstrates mesangial granular IgA deposition by immunofluorescence microscopy. This finding is diagnostic of IgA nephropathy; however, the temporal association with severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and the development of nephrotic syndrome raise consideration of a secondary or reactive glomerulopathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates transformation of membranous glomerulopathy into anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease. Histologic examination reveals a cellular crescent involving one glomerulus, identified on methenamine silver (Jones) stain, which is characteristic of active anti-GBM nephritis.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates perihilar variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) in association with glomerulomegaly. Glomerular enlargement is diffusely present, and segments of glomeruli show sclerosis involving the perihilar region, characterized by collapse of capillary loops and hyalinosis, consistent with secondary FSGS due to hyperfiltration injury.

Kidney: Focal atypical hyperplasia of the tubular epithelium is present, characterized by stratification of tubular epithelial cells with cytological atypia. These findings are diagnostic of early tubular neoplasia, consistent with the pathogenetic mechanism observed in previously described tubular hyperplastic lesions.

Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates heterogeneous downregulation of angiotensin II AT1-A and AT1-B receptors in renal arterioles of streptozotocin (STZ)-induced diabetic rats. In normal rats, AT1-A receptor immunoreactivity is prominently localized to the cell membrane of renin-positive smooth muscle cells (SMCs) in the afferent arteriole, consistent with a direct role in renin secretion regulation.

Histopathologic examination of the parathyroid gland reveals a circumscribed, non-encapsulated neoplasm composed predominantly of chief cells with uniform nuclei, scant eosinophilic cytoplasm, and absence of significant mitotic activity or necrosis—consistent with a parathyroid adenoma. The lack of capsular invasion definitively excludes parathyroid carcinoma, and no features of hyperplasia are identified.

Eosinophilic deposits are present within the renal parenchyma in a diabetic rat kidney. The deposits are observed in glomerular and/or tubulointerstitial regions and are consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at 400× magnification.

Kidney: Tubular epithelial cells show degenerative changes. There is cystic dilatation of renal tubules and presence of hyaline casts within tubular lumina. Findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis.

Mild segmental mesangial thickening identified in renal parenchyma, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff staining at ×400 magnification. Findings are consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. No additional pathological findings reported.

Schistosoma hematobium-associated glomerulopathy.

Cytomegalovirus-associated collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular tuft collapse, podocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia, microcystic tubular dilatation, and prominent interstitial fibrosis consistent with chronic viral injury.

Kidney: Focal interstitial lymphocytic infiltrate and a sclerotic glomerulus are present. The interstitial inflammation is predominantly mononuclear, with lymphocytes being the principal cell type, consistent with early-stage chronic interstitial nephritis.

Thymus: Mixed thymoma, WHO type AB. A well-circumscribed, 1.5 cm neoplastic mass was identified within the thymic parenchyma, consistent with a biphasic proliferation of spindle and epithelioid cells characteristic of type AB thymoma.

Transmission electron microscopy demonstrates intact hemidesmosomes (arrows), as observed in gingival basement membrane. The ultrastructural preservation of hemidesmosomes supports a diagnosis of Alport syndrome, given their characteristic stability in this condition.

Renal biopsy histopathology demonstrates glomerular capillary basement membrane ischemic wrinkling, endothelial cell swelling, and capillary luminal obliteration. Adjacent arterioles exhibit fibrinoid necrosis with luminal occlusion, consistent with malignant hypertension.

C4d deposition is present in the walls of glomerular capillaries of the renal allograft, as demonstrated by immunofluorescence microscopy (magnification ×400). This finding confirms acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Glomerular capillary with open lumen and segmental podocyte foot process effacement, as demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy. Findings are diagnostic of early focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, particularly in the context of familial glomerulonephropathy.

Electron micrograph demonstrating cytochemical localization of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) using the cerium-based method in a human neutrophil following phagocytic stimulation. answer_text: The liver exhibits a well-preserved histological architecture with maintained lobular organization, intact hepatocytes, and regular sinusoidal patterns in the control group animals. No pathological alterations are observed in the hepatic parenchyma or portal tracts, confirming the absence of underlying chronic liver disease.

Histopathological examination of renal tissue, stained with Periodic Acid-Schiff (PAS), reveals the following findings:

Glomeruli exhibit widespread and intense mononuclear cell infiltration, moderate mesangial matrix expansion, and focal pericapsular fibrosis, consistent with active lupus nephritis. Renal arteries display marked intimal fibrosis with fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina, indicative of hypertensive arteriosclerosis.

Photomicrograph of a splenic section from a rat co-exposed to zinc and glyphosate (375 mg/kg) reveals hemosiderosis, evident as pigment deposition within both the red pulp and white pulp. The presence of pigment in the white pulp is consistent with active erythrophagocytosis by lymphoid macrophages, indicating ongoing hemolysis.
Microphotograph (×400) showing mild tubular epithelial desquamation and absence of necrosis in the kidney of a rat from group II, consistent with early-stage acute tubular injury.

The electron micrograph demonstrates neuronal cell bodies within the alpha component of the anterior olfactory nucleus. Neuronal somata are in close apposition (indicated by arrows), consistent with synaptic connectivity.

Renal biopsy demonstrates prominent mesangial deposition of C1q by immunofluorescence microscopy, with intense and global mesangial staining. Immunofluorescence also reveals co-deposition of immunoglobulins and complement components consistent with a pattern of immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis, strongly suggestive of lupus nephritis.

Fontana-Masson stain demonstrates melanin pigment within corneocytes, dendritic melanocytes, and dermal melanophages; pigment is sparsely present in keratinocytes. These findings confirm a diagnosis of pigmented Bowen’s disease.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates fibrinoid necrosis within glomeruli, identified in 19 glomeruli examined. Cellular crescents are present in 26% of glomeruli, consistent with active pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates glomerular crescent formation on light microscopy. Crescents involve a subset of glomeruli and are composed of cellular proliferation within Bowman’s space, accompanied by segmental fibrinoid necrosis in some glomeruli, consistent with active anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Expression of transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) is observed in renal tissue from a patient with idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (IRPGN) who demonstrated a poor clinical response to therapy. Immunohistochemical or molecular analysis confirms TGF-β1 upregulation within glomerular and/or tubulointerstitial compartments, indicating that TGF-β1 overexpression directly causes treatment resistance and drives irreversible fibrosis in IRPGN.

Selective left renal artery angiography following coil embolization demonstrates a compact coil mass with complete occlusion of the wide-neck renal artery aneurysm. The adjacent downstream vascular architecture is preserved, and there is no evidence of parenchymal ischemia or perfusion defects; thus, the embolization has successfully prevented rupture without compromising renal function.

Renal biopsy demonstrates interstitial granulomatous inflammation associated with interstitial lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and tubular damage characterized by loss of normal tubular architecture. Granulomas are present within the renal interstitium and are consistent with sarcoidosis.

Metanephric stromal tumor: A rare benign pediatric renal neoplasm characterized by a specific stromal phenotype. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates diffuse positivity for CD34 within the tumor stromal cells (magnification ×200), supporting a diagnosis of metanephric stromal tumor over other pediatric renal tumors.
Peripheral blood film demonstrates eosinophilia, consistent with an underlying parasitic infection. No additional hematologic abnormalities are described in the provided material.

Histopathological examination of a lesion from the ankle reveals marked irregular acanthosis, papillomatosis, and a parakeratotic stratum corneum. Within the papillary and upper reticular dermis, there is a dense inflammatory infiltrate composed predominantly of lymphocytes and plasma cells, consistent with early mycosis fungoides.

Renal biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury. Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals linear staining positive for kappa light chains along the tubular basement membranes; lambda light chain staining is negative, consistent with light chain proximal tubulopathy due to monoclonal kappa light chain deposition.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is present, characterized by segmental sclerosis involving a subset of glomeruli. Scattered tubules exhibit focal minimal atrophy with associated foamy epithelial cells, diagnostic of lipid accumulation.
Electron microscopy of renal tissue demonstrates attenuation and loss of nuclei within tubular epithelial cells. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis.

Photomicrograph of hepatic tissue from broiler chickens fed a diet containing 25% hydrolyzed cassava peel meal as the primary carbohydrate source. The liver exhibits diffuse hepatocellular vacuolar degeneration, with marked cytoplasmic clearing diagnostic of microvesicular steatosis.

Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) was assessed using multiple methodologies, including radionuclide plasma sampling and serum creatinine-based measurements. The results from each method were compiled and reviewed for consistency and accuracy, with radionuclide plasma sampling considered the definitive gold standard for confirming chronic kidney disease staging.

Ovary: Hyperplasia of the rete ovarii is identified. Remnants of the rete ovarii are commonly present within the ovarian hilum and may exhibit hyperplastic changes, which can be associated with underlying hormonal stimulation or early neoplastic transformation.

Renal biopsy demonstrates segmental glomerular lesions characterized by mesangial matrix expansion, epithelial cell proliferation, and focal necrotizing changes. These findings are diagnostic of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, consistent with an ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Histopathological findings include the presence of cellular crescents within glomeruli. Crescents are composed of proliferating podocytes and infiltrating mononuclear inflammatory cells, occupying the Bowman’s space.

Section from the carotid body (intercarotid gland) of an adult cat, 25 days following surgical removal of the vertebral sympathetic chain. Histological examination reveals preserved fascicles of myelinated nerve fibers and parenchymal cells with normal innervation patterns, indicating successful reinnervation by parasympathetic fibers.

Renal pathology associated with inborn errors of metabolism, specifically primary hyperoxaluria type 1. Bone biopsy specimen from an adolescent patient reveals birefringent calcium oxalate crystals observed under polarized light microscopy, consistent with systemic oxalosis and confirming the diagnosis of primary hyperoxaluria type 1.

Fusiform endothelial cells are present in the vascular tumor tissue from a patient with kaposiform hemangioendothelioma. These cells exhibit characteristic morphological features consistent with this rare vascular neoplasm, involving localized proliferation of spindle-shaped endothelial cells arranged in a fascicular or storiform pattern, which is diagnostic of kaposiform hemangioendothelioma in the absence of further immunohistochemical confirmation.
Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by global fibrinoid necrosis of glomerular capillary loops. Crescents are present, involving a subset of glomeruli.

Immunoperoxidase staining for podocin was performed on laser capture microdissected glomeruli to characterize glomerular diseases.

- Normal allograft glomerulus demonstrates intact podocin expression localized to podocytes along the glomerular capillary walls.  
- In fibronectin glomerulopathy, the enlarged glomerulus shows absence of mesangial staining and diminished podocin immunoreactivity in the capillary wall, consistent with podocyte injury.

Necrolytic acral erythema: Histopathologic examination reveals epidermal pallor involving the stratum corneum, hyperkeratosis, dyskeratotic keratinocytes, and spongiosis. A superficial perivascular mixed inflammatory infiltrate is present in the dermis, consistent with a lymphocytic vasculitis.

Perpendicular section of renal parenchyma examined at 10× magnification demonstrates a superficial zone of thermal injury measuring less than 1 mm in thickness. Immediately subjacent to this is a region of viable renal tissue, including glomeruli and tubular structures, which appear histologically unremarkable and confirm the absence of underlying parenchymal disease.

Photomicrograph demonstrates strong interstitial immunoreactivity for collagen type IV, with prominent periglomerular distribution. This finding is diagnostic of advanced extracellular matrix deposition in the renal interstitium and directly indicates progressive renal dysfunction in patients with IgA nephropathy.

Pancreas: Focal interstitial inflammatory cell infiltration, predominantly composed of lymphocytes and plasma cells, is present, particularly in the periductal regions. Such findings are characteristic of early chronic pancreatitis and indicate ongoing autoimmune-mediated injury.

Photomicrograph of renal cortex from a rat treated with Arabic gum, showing cortical tubules and peritubular capillaries without morphological evidence of pathological change; the absence of tubular injury confirms the nephroprotective effect of Arabic gum. Magnification: ×200.

Picrosirius red staining of a fresh autologous living tissue biotube demonstrates a predominant and widely distributed network of type III collagen fibers exhibiting weak birefringence under polarized light, along with a sparse and scattered distribution of type I collagen fibers displaying strong birefringence (magnification: ×400). The collagen composition reflects a matrix rich in reticulin-type fibers with focal areas of denser, fibrillar collagen deposition, consistent with early fibrotic remodeling.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “consistent with early fibrotic remodeling” is an unwarranted diagnostic inference.

Kidney, dog: Non-sclerotic glomerulus exhibiting intracapillary neutrophilic infiltration and periglomerular plasma-lymphocytic inflammatory cell accumulation. These findings are diagnostic of immune-complex–mediated glomerulonephritis secondary to pyometra.

Scanning electron microscopy of the urinary aspect of a normal glomerular capillary demonstrates intact podocyte architecture, including: (a) podocyte cell bodies, (b) primary cytoplasmic processes, and (c) interdigitating secondary foot processes forming filtration slits, which exhibit a regular, zipper-like configuration. These findings confirm the absence of minimal change disease and represent the normal ultrastructural morphology of podocytes.

Liver, rat: Histological examination reveals hepatic congestion consistent with early-stage centrilobular necrosis. Findings are based on a study assessing the effects of chromium and vitamin E (α-tocopherol) treatment.

Ultrastructural examination of renal tissue reveals the presence of intratubular crystals characterized by radiating spicules, observed at high magnification (×5800). The morphological features are diagnostic of exogenous crystal deposition.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrating recurrent fibronectin glomerulopathy.

Histopathological examination reveals enlarged glomeruli with acellular expansion of the mesangial matrix. Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining highlights mesangial deposits, consistent with immune complex deposition characteristic of fibronectin glomerulopathy.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates mild acute tubular injury with interstitial edema and minimal interstitial inflammation (H&E, ×400). The findings are observed in the context of a severely ruptured graft, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Renal biopsy demonstrates membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by lobulation of the glomerular capillary tuft, mesangial hypercellularity, and thickening of the capillary walls. Within the glomerular capillary lumens, intravascular amastigotes consistent with *Leishmania* species are identified, confirming active visceral leishmaniasis as the underlying etiology of the glomerulopathy.

Amyloid deposits are present within the small vessels of the pancreas, as demonstrated by Congo red staining. The deposits exhibit characteristic apple-green birefringence under polarized light, confirming systemic amyloidosis.

Diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane is demonstrated on silver methenamine stain, consistent with membranous glomerulonephritis. These findings are diagnostic of membranous lupus nephritis in the clinical context of systemic lupus erythematosus.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate a segmental necrotizing glomerulonephritis with an associated cellular crescent causing disruption of Bowman’s capsule. The crescent exhibits features of active inflammation, including nuclear fragmentation and cellular proliferation, consistent with early fibrocellular transformation.

Renal biopsy electron microscopy demonstrates fibrillary glomerular deposition characterized by randomly arranged microfibrils measuring 15–20 nm in diameter. These deposits are present within the glomerular basement membranes and mesangial regions, consistent with a diagnosis of amyloidosis.

Histological examination of the postnatal day 11 rat kidney demonstrates glomerular immaturity, particularly in the outer cortex. Most glomeruli exhibit an immature appearance, characterized by cellular paucity and incomplete differentiation of the glomerular tuft, consistent with delayed nephrogenesis.
The tumor demonstrated strong and diffuse membranous immunoreactivity for smooth muscle actin on immunohistochemical staining, consistent with a diagnosis of leiomyosarcoma.

Section of kidney tissue from a rat in the gout control group (Group II) demonstrates the presence of proteinaceous casts within the lumina of renal tubules, as observed on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections at 240× magnification. These casts are consistent with early-stage acute tubular necrosis, although no additional pathological features such as tubular epithelial cell degeneration or loss are evident.

Adrenal gland: Focal mineralization is present at the corticomedullary junction, with involvement of the inner cortex. This finding is commonly observed in cynomolgus monkeys (Macaca fascicularis) and is consistent with metastatic mineralization.

Recurrence of ANCA-negative, renal-limited pauci-immune glomerulonephritis in the renal allograft. One glomerulus exhibits a cellular crescent containing fibrin, consistent with active anti-GBM disease.
Testis: Spermatocele. There is focal accumulation of spermatozoa within the seminiferous tubules, occasionally resulting in tubular occlusion.

Renal biopsy demonstrates features consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody disease, including linear IgG deposition along the glomerular basement membranes, in the absence of glomerular crescents, accompanied by thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA).  

Light microscopy reveals prominent myxoid intimal thickening of an interlobular artery, suggestive of chronic endothelial injury typical of anti-GBM disease.

Diffuse interstitial inflammation with associated tubular degeneration and tubular dilation observed on hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×200 magnification, consistent with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Photomicrograph of kidney tissue from a pouch young thylacine (Thylacinus cynocephalus). The histological section demonstrates glomeruli (G) and renal tubules (T), with preserved architecture of the nephron components, consistent with normal renal development for this developmental stage.

Electron microscopic analysis reveals endothelial cell swelling, disruption of the lamina rara interna, and electron-dense deposits localized to the subepithelial space. These findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

The superficial cortex of the kidney demonstrates normal glomerular architecture and preservation of proximal tubular segments. Light microscopy of toluidin blue-stained semi-thin sections from plastic-embedded tissue reveals distinct morphological features of the proximal tubule: S1 segment shows a narrower lumen and lower epithelial cell height, while the S2 segment exhibits a wider lumen, taller cells, and markedly dilated basolateral intercellular spaces, consistent with early acute tubular injury.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description is purely morphological and does not assign any pathological significance to the observed features.

Electron microscopy reveals electron-dense, laminated lipid inclusions—characteristic of zebra bodies—within the cytoplasm of a podocyte. These findings are diagnostic of Fabry disease, as zebra bodies are pathognomonic for this condition and do not occur in other disorders.

Liver – Pooled fraction: Microscopic examination reveals hepatic fatty change and focal hepatocellular necrosis, consistent with acute viral hepatitis, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining at ×400 magnification.

Thyroid gland: Extensive lymphocytic infiltration with germinal center formation is present. Follicular epithelial cells show mild enlargement and partial nuclear clearing, diagnostic of Hashimoto thyroiditis.

Photomicrograph of the brain (cerebrum) from a Wistar rat exposed to glyphosate (375 mg/kg) demonstrates neuronal degeneration, characterized by shrunken, hypereosinophilic neurons with pyknotic nuclei and loss of Nissl substance, as identified on hematoxylin and eosin staining. These findings are consistent with acute neurotoxic injury directly attributable to glyphosate exposure.

Electron microscopy of an affected glomerulus from a 20-month-old mouse reveals intracapillary luminal accumulation of optically lucent areas consistent with lipid vacuoles. The deposits are located within the glomerular capillary lumina (indicated by closed arrow) and are suggestive of early-stage lipoprotein thrombi formation.

Acute kidney injury with features suggestive of irreversible renal damage following pemetrexed therapy. Light microscopy reveals tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by diffuse tubular injury, including tubular epithelial cell degeneration, loss of brush border, and focal tubular atrophy, consistent with chronic interstitial fibrosis.
lesion_type: tubulointerstitial nephritis with chronic interstitial fibrosis

answer_text: Renal cortex demonstrates histological features consistent with diabetic nephropathy, including glomerular hypertrophy and increased mesangial matrix expansion. These findings are diagnostic of early-stage diabetic nephropathy in the context of systemic diabetes mellitus.

Lung tissue section shows numerous hemosiderin-laden macrophages within alveolar spaces, indicative of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. No evidence of pulmonary vasculitis or granulomatous inflammation is identified; therefore, the findings are diagnostic of idiopathic pulmonary hemosiderosis.

Tongue: There is intercellular edema and swelling of prickle cells (spongiosis), accompanied by inflammatory cell infiltration. These findings are diagnostic of reticular degeneration, representing an early or intermediate stage of epithelial injury that will inevitably progress to erosion.

Non-neoplastic renal parenchyma demonstrates segmental and global glomerulosclerosis. Arterioles and small arteries exhibit fibromuscular dysplasia characterized by concentric thickening of the vascular wall with duplication of the internal elastic lamina.

Light microscopy reveals mild mesangial hypercellularity and diffuse thickening of glomerular capillary walls, Bowman’s capsule, and tubular basement membranes. Immunofluorescence demonstrates discrete granular deposits of IgM heavy chain and κ light chain within the mesangium and along glomerular and tubular basement membranes, consistent with a diagnosis of monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease.
Electron microscopy demonstrates nonbranching fibrillar deposits within the wall of a renal blood vessel. The fibrils are diagnostic of amyloid deposition.

Cardiac enlargement is evident on supine anteroposterior chest radiograph, consistent with pericardial effusion. The pericardial effusion appears chylous in nature, confirming the diagnosis of chylopericardium.

The preserved glomeruli are enlarged and hypercellular, with expansion of the mesangial areas due to increased mononuclear cells. Numerous mononuclear inflammatory cells are present within the glomerular capillary lumens, consistent with active lupus nephritis.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates diffuse, granular deposition of IgG along the glomerular capillary basement membranes (original magnification ×400), consistent with immune complex–mediated membranous nephropathy. Staining for IgA, IgM, C1q, and C3c is nonsignificant.

Marked interstitial edema and hemorrhage are present within the renal allograft parenchyma. These findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection and may be associated with graft dysfunction or rupture in the setting of immunosuppression withdrawal following allograft failure.

Cerebrum, red-legged partridge: Immunohistochemical examination demonstrates cytoplasmic labeling of Bagaza virus (BAGV) antigen within neurons and capillary endothelial cells. Findings confirm a diagnosis of flavivirus encephalitis with direct viral injury to both neuronal and vascular compartments.

Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates acute and chronic interstitial inflammation, associated with tubular epithelial cell damage and tubulitis. These histopathological findings are diagnostic of acute interstitial nephritis. The changes are likely attributable to HIV-associated nephropathy.

Kidney, biopsy: Prominent juxtaglomerular apparatus with sparse silver-positive renin granules identified within the afferent arterioles adjacent to glomeruli (methenamine silver stain; original magnification ×400). These findings are diagnostic of scleroderma renal crisis due to unequivocal evidence of heightened renin synthesis.

F15: Representative immunohistochemical photomicrograph demonstrating LFA-1 expression in cardiac tissue of double transgenic rats (dTGR). Angiotensin II directly upregulates LFA-1 expression in cardiomyocytes, contributing to myocardial inflammation in this model.

Kidney tissue demonstrates histological features consistent with diabetic nephropathy. Light microscopic examination reveals accumulation of lipofuscin pigment within the epithelial cells of the proximal tubules, which is a characteristic finding supporting the diagnosis of diabetic nephropathy.

Membranous glomerulopathy with superimposed pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates segmental, finely granular IgG deposition along the glomerular capillary walls, graded as 1+ intensity on a scale of 0 to 3+, using anti-IgG immunofluorescence at ×400 original magnification. This pattern is diagnostic of primary membranous nephropathy and confirms the presence of immune complex–mediated injury in the crescentic lesions.
lesion_type: membranous glomerulopathy with pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic gl

answer_text: Renal biopsy demonstrates tubular injury characterized by intraluminal casts and crystalline inclusions. The casts consist of degenerated crystalline material, with focal peripheral crystalline inclusions morphologically identical to those observed within tubular epithelial cells, consistent with acute crystal nephropathy due to oxalate deposition.

Focal segmental necrotizing glomerulonephritis with fibrocellular crescent formation is present. These findings are diagnostic of pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis and confirm the presence of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA).

Renal biopsy tissue contains 18 glomeruli, all exhibiting a largely unremarkable glomerular architecture. Glomerular capillary lumina are patent, with intact and non-duplicated basement membranes, ruling out diabetic nephropathy.

Photomicrograph of the liver from broiler chickens fed a control diet with 0% hydrolyzed cassava peel as the primary carbohydrate source. Hepatic architecture is preserved, with no significant histological abnormalities observed; notably, there is no evidence of glycogen accumulation or hepatocellular steatosis, confirming normal metabolic function.

The glomerular basement membrane (GBM) normally contains predominantly collagen α3α4α5(IV), which is structurally defective or absent in Alport syndrome. Immunofluorescence staining of a frozen human kidney section demonstrates strong expression of COL4A4 (collagen α3α4α5[IV]) localized to the GBM and focally in tubular basement membranes, appearing as red signal, consistent with a diagnosis of Alport syndrome.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly describes normal COL4A4 expression.

Electron microscopy demonstrates global subepithelial electron-dense deposits, consistent with membranous glomerulopathy, stage 3. These findings confirm a diagnosis of primary membranous nephropathy.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates glomerular and vascular findings diagnostic of recurrent hemolytic uremic syndrome. Glomeruli show increased mesangial matrix and focal endocapillary cellular swelling, with prominent endothelial injury characteristic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by a diffuse interstitial lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate with interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. The inflammatory infiltrate is composed predominantly of lymphocytes and plasma cells, consistent with an autoimmune etiology.
Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane. This finding is diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.
Sciatic nerve: Focal interfascicular fibrosis with reduced density of nerve fibers. This finding is diagnostic of chronic axonal neuropathy secondary to prior traumatic injury.

The specimen demonstrates immunohistochemical expression of cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1), cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), microsomal prostaglandin E synthase-1 (mPGES-1), and microsomal prostaglandin E synthase-2 (mPGES-2) within renal, pulmonary, and cardiac tissues in the context of experimental hypertension. Expression is observed in glomerular epithelial cells, vascular endothelium, tubular epithelium, cardiomyocytes, and pulmonary parenchymal cells, consistent with a pathogenic role for these enzymes in mediating end-organ damage during hypertensive injury.

WT1 immunostaining is observed in the cytoplasm of cells within the fetal zone of the developing adrenal gland. Positive immunoreactivity is also present in the adrenal capsule, where strongly reactive cells are interspersed among non-reactive cells, consistent with mesothelial origin and supporting a diagnosis of adrenal cortical neoplasm.

Lung: Focal, mild alveolar infiltration by macrophages. This finding is consistent with early-stage interstitial lung disease and may represent an early toxic response to the test article in this preclinical toxicity study.

Synaptopodin immunofluorescence labeling was performed on 8-μm-thick frozen sections of perfusion-fixed rat forebrain tissue. Synaptopodin expression was observed in the cerebral cortex, striatum, and hippocampus, consistent with its established role as a marker of dendritic spines in mature neurons.

Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) was assessed using radionuclide plasma sampling and serum creatinine-based methods. GFR values obtained at multiple time intervals were plotted graphically to allow comparison of the kinetics and magnitude of filtration across measurement techniques, with the radionuclide method demonstrating superior accuracy in directly quantifying true GFR compared to creatinine-based estimates, which are inherently unreliable due to tubular secretion of creatinine.

Periodic acid–methenamine silver (PASM) staining revealed segmental spike-like projections arising from the glomerular basement membrane, indicative of subepithelial immune complex deposition. These findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Glomeruli demonstrate diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membranes, as highlighted by Masson's trichrome stain (×400). The morphologic findings are diagnostic of membranous glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph demonstrates linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) on immunofluorescence microscopy, consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane disease. Serologic evaluation reveals absence of circulating anti-GBM antibodies.

Histopathological examination of the eye in hybrid tilapia (Oreochromis spp.) with natural Streptococcus agalactiae infection reveals choroidal edema, characterized by marked thickening of the choroid layer, in addition to choroidal vascular congestion (hyperemia) and focal hemorrhage. These findings are consistent with uveitis secondary to direct bacterial invasion of the uveal tract during systemic infection.

Glomerulus demonstrates segmental glomerulosclerosis with associated adhesion to Bowman’s capsule. Intracapillary foam cells are present within the sclerotic segment. Findings are diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), classic variant.

Renal tissue alterations were observed in a size-dependent manner, with smaller gold nanoparticles inducing more pronounced effects, correlating with duration of exposure. In a rat treated with 100 μL of 20 nm gold nanoparticles for 7 days, karyorrhexis was identified in the nuclear chromatin of epithelial cells lining the proximal convoluted tubules (arrows), consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Renal histology demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting a fibrocellular crescent, indicative of severe glomerular injury with circumferential parietal epithelial cell proliferation and matrix expansion. The finding is diagnostic of active and chronic glomerulonephritis due to anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis.

Kidney biopsy shows diffuse endocapillary proliferative and membranous lupus nephritis (Class IV-G A/C with focal segmental membranous features). Histologic findings include increased mesangial matrix, marked endocapillary proliferation, intraglomerular leukocyte infiltration, and subepithelial immune complex deposits consistent with concurrent Class V involvement.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately notes "focal segmental membranous features" within a Class IV-G context, which is acceptable.
