answer_text: 3D reconstructed cranial CT angiography following bone removal demonstrates occlusion of the right internal carotid artery, as indicated by absence of contrast opacification distal to the site of occlusion. Corresponding non-opacification of the vasculature in the right cerebral hemisphere confirms acute infarction due to complete cessation of antegrade perfusion.

Amyloid A amyloidosis involving renal parenchyma, characterized by extracellular deposition of congophilic amyloid fibrils exhibiting apple-green birefringence under polarized light following Congo red staining. Immunohistochemical staining confirms AA-type amyloid deposition as the definitive cause of renal dysfunction in this case.

Glomerulus demonstrates segmental capillary loop collapse with hypertrophied and hyperplastic podocytes exhibiting prominent intracytoplasmic protein resorption droplets, as highlighted by toluidine blue staining on semi-thin sections. These findings are diagnostic of early focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Cystic forms of *Balantidium coli* identified in urine sediment. Organisms exhibit characteristic morphological features including a prominent macronucleus, smaller micronucleus, and multiple food vacuoles, consistent with active trophozoite-stage infection in the urinary tract.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse interstitial infiltration by small, mature-appearing lymphoid cells consistent with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The infiltrate is predominantly interstitial, with extensive involvement effacing normal parenchymal architecture and directly invading glomerular tufts.

Transmission electron microscopy of kidney tubule tissue from the control group demonstrates normal ultrastructural morphology of the tubular epithelial cells. The basement membrane is intact, with well-preserved cellular organelles including a prominent nucleus, mitochondria, brush border, and lysosomes.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly lists "membranous vesicles," which are commonly observed in proximal tubule cells and reflect normal endocytic activity.

Diffuse global glomerulosclerosis observed on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain at 400× magnification. Findings are consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy, characterized by diffuse involvement of the entire glomerular tuft.

Diffuse extracapillary glomerulonephritis characterized by predominant cellular crescents (*) with focal areas of fibrotic crescents (**), indicative of active and chronic injury, respectively. The preserved glomerular tufts are compressed (↑), consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis due to anti-GBM antibody disease.

RNA sequencing analysis was performed on renal tissue from experimental mice to evaluate differential gene expression following therapeutic intervention. Chronic hypokalemia-associated nephrocalcinosis. Calcium deposits are present within the tubular lumen and adjacent interstitium, as demonstrated on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining at 400× magnification.

Staghorn calculus involving the collecting system of a solitary kidney. Procedure performed using a combination of percutaneous nephrolithotomy and retrograde intrarenal surgery, with complete stone clearance confirmed intraoperatively by fluoroscopy and endoscopic visualization.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrating recurrent fibronectin glomerulopathy. Glomeruli are enlarged with diffuse mesangial expansion, appearing acellular on hematoxylin and eosin staining, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Perinatal nephropathies refer to a spectrum of developmental and acquired renal disorders occurring in the perinatal period. These may include congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract (CAKUT), renal dysplasia, oligomeganephronia, multicystic dysplastic kidneys, obstructive uropathy, and ischemic or toxic acute tubular necrosis in the neonatal context, which often progresses to irreversible cortical scarring even with prompt intervention.

Renal section demonstrates glomerular and peritubular capillary hyperemia accompanied by interstitial fibrosis. These findings are consistent with drug-induced lupus nephritis secondary to propylthiouracil administration.

Expression of aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC) is observed in the columnar epithelial cells of the early proximal tubule and Bowman’s capsule in wild-type mice. Kidney, rat: Histopathological examination reveals glomerular mesangial cell proliferation, which is focally accentuated and non-uniform. Glomerular basement membranes are thickened and exhibit prominent eosinophilic (brown) discoloration on H&E staining, consistent with immune complex deposition.

The retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)–Bruch’s membrane–choriocapillaris complex demonstrates features diagnostic of age-related macular degeneration (AMD). Drusen—hyaline, nodular deposits—are present within Bruch’s membrane and are capped by a thickened, pale band of basement membrane material.

Renal function was assessed through evaluation of estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) using the Cockcroft–Gault (CG) method. The analysis included data on changes in eGFR from baseline in HIV-infected patients receiving atazanavir (ATV)-based antiretroviral regimens, which are known to cause direct tubular toxicity leading to a consistent decline in eGFR.

Hyalinosis involving a glomerular hilar arteriole with associated nodular glomerulosclerosis. The glomerular tufts exhibit distinct nodular transformation, characterized by expanded mesangial regions forming lobulated, acellular nodules consistent with diabetic nephropathy.

Renal tissue alterations were size-dependent, with smaller nanoparticles inducing more pronounced effects, correlating with duration of exposure. In a rat treated with 50 μL of 10 nm gold nanoparticles for 3 days (F1:GNPs), histopathological examination revealed glomerular congestion, consistent with early-stage acute glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates glomerular deposition of amyloid-A (AA) amyloid, confirmed by immunohistochemical staining. The deposits are localized within the glomerular mesangium and capillary walls, consistent with systemic AA amyloidosis involving the kidneys.
Persistent focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with ongoing nephrotic syndrome, likely driven by residual C1q glomerular deposits despite immunomodulatory therapy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular findings diagnostic of C3 glomerulopathy. Glomeruli exhibit a membranoproliferative pattern characterized by increased mesangial matrix and mesangial hypercellularity, with prominent capillary wall thickening suggestive of immune complex deposition.

Bone marrow: Occasional lymphoid follicle formation is present within the femur and sternum marrow spaces. These aggregates consist of circumscribed accumulations of lymphocytes, morphologically resembling primary follicles and indicative of early follicular lymphoma.

C3 glomerulonephritis is demonstrated by electron microscopy, which reveals electron-dense deposits located in the subendothelial space, intramembranous region, and mesangial areas of the glomerular capillary walls. The distribution of dense deposits is diagnostic of C3 glomerulonephritis secondary to underlying multiple myeloma.

Renal tissue biopsy demonstrates xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis characterized by diffuse infiltration of the renal parenchyma by numerous foamy histiocytes and occasional multinucleated giant cells. The inflammatory infiltrate is associated with destruction of normal renal architecture, consistent with chronic obstruction due to nephrolithiasis.

Renal biopsy reveals global glomerular hypercellularity with prominent intracapillary mononuclear cell infiltration. Diffuse mesangial and endocapillary proliferation is present, accompanied by inflammatory cell infiltration, consistent with lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Class III).

Membranous glomerulopathy with superimposed pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis. Silver stain demonstrates segmental areas of absent staining within glomerular capillary walls, corresponding to subepithelial immune deposits that have been completely dissolved during tissue processing, resulting in a "hole-like" appearance.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly attributes the "hole-like" appearance to *tangential sectioning*—a well-known artifact in which the plane of section cuts through the edge of subepithelial deposits, creating gaps in the silver-stained basement membrane.
lesion_type: membranous glomerulopathy with pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic gl

answer_text: Transmission electron microscopy of a skin biopsy reveals a dermal capillary with marked thickening of the basal membrane. Numerous granular osmiophilic deposits (GOM) are present within the basal membrane and are frequently localized in close proximity to pericytes, consistent with a diagnosis of cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL).

High-resolution chest computed tomography demonstrates distortion of pulmonary architecture with interstitial thickening, ground-glass opacities, and right lower lobe consolidation. These findings are consistent with organizing pneumonia.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intralobular arteries with concentric, laminated fibrosis of the intima, resulting in an "onion-skin" appearance. Glomeruli show segmental mesangiolysis with associated capillary microaneurysm formation, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed strong (3+/4+) fine granular IgG deposition along the glomerular capillary walls, involving 80–90% of glomeruli. No significant staining was observed for IgA, IgM, C3, C1q, or kappa/lambda light chains.

Kidney tissue demonstrates the presence of hyaline casts and moderate fatty change (vacuolar degeneration) of tubular epithelial cells, as observed in histological sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at 200× magnification. These findings are consistent with early-stage diabetic nephropathy and were noted in the context of a study involving dyslipidaemic rats treated with atorvastatin and garlic.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is present, characterized by segmental sclerosis involving a subset of glomeruli. Histologic examination reveals associated mesangial matrix expansion and mesangial thickening, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a hypercellular glomerulus with marked lobular accentuation of the glomerular tuft, as highlighted by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. The histomorphological findings are diagnostic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Therefore, based on the described pathophysiological mechanism, it is reasonable to infer the presence of acute tubular necrosis as the likely histological correlate, despite the absence of direct microscopic evidence.

Kidney (pelvis): Occasional eosinophilic droplets are present within the transitional epithelium lining the renal pelvis. These intracytoplasmic inclusions represent keratohyalin granules, indicative of early squamous metaplasia in the urothelium.
Acute Epstein-Barr virus infection-associated collapsing glomerulopathy. Renal biopsy tissue demonstrates positive in situ hybridization for Epstein-Barr virus.

Renal thrombotic microangiopathy, acute phase.

Histopathological findings: Global thickening of glomerular capillary walls is present, attributable to subendothelial expansion. Focal mesangiolysis and occasional intracapillary fibrin thrombi are observed, consistent with active endothelial injury and diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Mild focal tubular atrophy is present, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff staining at ×200 magnification. The finding involves discrete regions of the renal parenchyma and is characterized by tubular basement membrane thickening, epithelial simplification, and reduced tubular caliber, consistent with early-stage chronic interstitial nephritis.

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following early exposure to oral cadmium reveals initial tubular epithelial injury predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Light microscopy demonstrates cytoplasmic vacuolization, loss of brush border integrity, and focal areas of tubular epithelial cell degeneration consistent with early acute tubular necrosis, with minimal interstitial inflammation.

Kidney biopsy: Light microscopy reveals prominent accumulation of periodic acid–Schiff-positive (purple) material within podocytes, consistent with glycolipid deposition as seen in Fabry disease. This finding reflects the characteristic involvement of glomerular parietal epithelial cells in the storage pathology of Fabry disease.

Glomerular expression of the membrane attack complex (C5b-9) is observed in this case of idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence staining demonstrates C5b-9 deposition within glomeruli, confirming activation of the terminal complement pathway as the primary driver of glomerular injury.

Transplant kidney biopsy demonstrates glomerular capillary luminal thrombosis on light microscopy, with no significant tubular or interstitial abnormalities. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy as the cause of renal allograft dysfunction.

Ventricular fibrillation was documented as the initial cardiac rhythm upon presentation. The clinical course was characterized by acute hemodynamic instability consistent with malignant arrhythmia, secondary to anomalous origin of the left coronary artery from the pulmonary artery.
Renal biopsy demonstrates findings consistent with early diabetic nephropathy in the setting of metabolic syndrome. Glomeruli show mild mesangial expansion and focal global glomerulosclerosis.

Photomicrograph of the renal cortex demonstrating preserved parenchymal architecture, with normal glomerular and tubular structures, consistent with a diagnosis of minimal change disease, as observed under light microscopy at ×200 magnification.
Kidney: Proliferation of Bowman’s capsule with associated nephron atrophy, consistent with early crescent formation, as demonstrated by histological examination (H&E stain, 20×).

Glomerulus demonstrates unremarkable capillary tufts with single-contoured basement membranes. No mesangiolysis, fibrinoid necrosis, or crescent formation is present, effectively ruling out active lupus nephritis.

Capsular invasion by malignant parathyroid tissue is identified, diagnostic of parathyroid carcinoma. The neoplastic process involves the tumor capsule, demonstrating infiltrative growth of cytologically malignant parathyroid cells extending into adjacent thyroid parenchyma.

Testis: Focal increase in stromal collagen fibers within the interstitial compartment. This finding is occasionally observed and may occur at various stages of maturation; however, it is not identified in mature testes and is therefore indicative of incomplete maturation or delayed development.

Malignant parathyroid neoplasm with vascular invasion. Tumor cells exhibit cytological and architectural features diagnostic of parathyroid carcinoma, including unequivocal invasion of vascular spaces.

Renal biopsy examination reveals marked lymphocytic infiltration within the tubulointerstitial compartment, accompanied by interstitial edema. Focal tubular atrophy is present, consistent with chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Primary membranous nephropathy.  
Light microscopy demonstrates glomerular capillary wall thickening with a uniform appearance, consistent with early-stage membranous nephropathy; the absence of significant mesangial hypercellularity further supports this diagnosis over IgA nephropathy (hematoxylin and eosin; ×200).

Histopathology: Small intestine (jejunum and ileum).  

Increased number of goblet cells noted in the epithelium of the jejunum and ileum, consistent with early metaplastic change suggestive of intestinal metaplasia.

Ultrastructural examination of renal tubular cells reveals the presence of electron-dense nanoparticles within lysosomes (thick arrows), consistent with exogenous pigment accumulation. Mitochondria show evidence of injury, including disruption and loss of cristae (thin arrows), indicative of irreversible cellular damage.
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with exogenous pigment accumulation

answer_text: Renal biopsy immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular IgA deposits within the glomerular mesangium. The distribution and intensity of staining are consistent with immune complex deposition, strongly supporting a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy.

Photomicrograph demonstrates features of cast nephropathy in renal tubules, characterized by the presence of eosinophilic, refractile casts with surrounding tubular epithelial cell injury and flattening. Some casts are associated with multinucleated giant cell reactions, diagnostic of myeloma kidney.

Photomicrograph of a heart section from a rat exposed to cypermethrin (20 mg/kg/day for 30 days), stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E, ×200), demonstrates myocardial hemorrhage, disorganization of the branching myocardial architecture, loss of cross-striations, and early necrotic changes in cardiomyocytes. These findings are diagnostic of cypermethrin-induced myocardial infarction; however, correlation with clinical and laboratory data is recommended for comprehensive interpretation.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerular abnormalities characterized by increased mesangial matrix expansion and periglomerular calcification, the latter interpreted as evidence of chronic glomerulonephritis. These structural changes are observed in the context of a clinical history notable for chronic baking soda abuse, rhabdomyolysis, glomerulonephritis, and hypertension, all contributing to progressive renal failure.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly localizes periglomerular calcification *external to the glomerular capsule*, which is typically associated with dystrophic calcification in chronic kidney disease or metabolic disturbances (e.g., from baking soda abuse causing alkalosis and calcium phosphate deposition), not directly with glomerulonephritis.

Kidney: Focal regenerative changes involving the tubular epithelium are present in the renal cortex and outer medulla. These changes are characterized by basophilic tubules with increased nuclear density, consistent with active tubular epithelial regeneration indicative of acute tubular necrosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates significant mesangial deposits of C1q on immunofluorescence staining (FITC-labeled anti-C1q, ×400 magnification). The presence of prominent C1q deposition is diagnostic of C1q nephropathy and warrants exclusion of non-renal causes of hematuria.

Kidney: Diffuse interstitial fibrosis and glomerular fibrosis are present, as observed in a dog with pyometra. Morphological findings are consistent with chronic renal parenchymal damage involving both the glomeruli and interstitium, likely secondary to immune-mediated glomerulonephritis triggered by the systemic infection.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates membranous nephropathy characterized by diffuse glomerular basement membrane thickening, most evident on light microscopy at high magnification (H&E stain). The morphological findings are consistent with stage I membranous nephropathy, showing no significant endocapillary or extracapillary proliferation, necrosis, or crescent formation; the absence of subepithelial immune complex deposits on light microscopy further supports early-stage disease.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The subtle error lies in the statement: *"the absence of subepithelial immune complex deposits on light microscopy further supports early-stage disease."* Subepithelial immune complex deposits—the hallmark of membranous nephropathy—are **not reliably visible on light microscopy**, even with H&E staining; they typically require electron microscopy for definitive identification.

Light microscopic examination of the renal biopsy specimen demonstrates crescentic glomerulonephritis. Crescents are present within Bowman’s space, composed of cellular and fibrocellular elements, indicative of severe glomerular injury and consistent with an underlying anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease process.

36-year-old female with biopsy findings in the context of catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome treated with eculizumab. Histopathologic evaluation revealed thrombotic microangiopathy consistent with antiphospholipid antibody–mediated renal injury.

Kidney section, hematoxylin and eosin stain: The renal tissue exhibits hemorrhage, vascular thrombosis, and atrophy of hematopoietic elements. These findings are diagnostic of naturally occurring Streptococcus agalactiae infection in hybrid tilapia (Oreochromis spp.).

Histology of the developing kidney in a neonate on postnatal day 1 demonstrates immature renal tubules (arrows) and maturing glomeruli (arrowhead), consistent with congenital nephrotic syndrome. Hematoxylin and eosin stain.

Renal arteriole showing endothelialitis and intravascular amorphous eosinophilic deposits, diagnostic of cryoglobulin deposition, in a patient with acute kidney injury attributed to hepatitis C virus-associated cryoglobulinemia. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain.
Electron microscopy reveals randomly arranged fibrillar deposits in the subendothelial and mesangial regions of glomeruli. The fibrils measure 7–13 nm in diameter, consistent with amyloidosis.

Photomicrograph (H&E, ×150) demonstrates extensive deposition of pale pink, eosinophilic material within the extracellular matrix of the glomeruli. The deposits are predominantly located in the mesangial and capillary loop regions, consistent with amyloidosis.
Heart: Focal areas of myocardial fiber disruption are observed in the treated group, consistent with early-stage myocardial infarction.

Electron microscopy reveals ultrastructural details of the glomerular filtration barrier. The cross-section demonstrates the capillary lumen (Cs), adjacent urinary space (Us), vascular endothelial cells (E), intact glomerular basement membrane (BM), and overlying podocytes (P) with preserved foot processes, consistent with minimal change disease.

Native kidney biopsy demonstrates mild homogenous thickening of glomerular capillary walls and arteriolar walls, minimal tubular atrophy, and focal interstitial fibrosis (PAS stain, ×50 magnification). These findings are diagnostic of renal involvement by monoclonal gammopathy, specifically light chain deposition disease, in the context of underlying monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS).

Renal cortical biopsy demonstrates a dense interstitial inflammatory infiltrate composed predominantly of plasma cells. Immunohistochemical staining reveals numerous IgG-positive plasma cells, approximately 50–60% of which co-express IgG4, consistent with a diagnosis of IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Renal cortex demonstrates moderate tubulitis involving proximal and distal tubules, accompanied by moderate interstitial edema and interstitial inflammatory infiltrates. The interstitial inflammation consists of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells without granuloma formation, consistent with acute cellular rejection.

Eye: Focal lymphoplasmacytic infiltration within the conjunctival subepithelium, consistent with chronic allergic conjunctivitis. A focus of inflammatory cell infiltration is present in the conjunctiva.
Persistent focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with ongoing nephrotic syndrome, likely driven by residual C1q glomerular deposits despite immunomodulatory therapy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a necrotizing granuloma within the renal parenchyma, composed of epithelioid histiocytes, multinucleated giant cells, and central caseous necrosis, consistent with tuberculosis. An adjacent glomerulus appears morphologically unremarkable.

Renal biopsy demonstrates interstitial inflammatory infiltrates with associated tubulitis, consistent with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis. Glomeruli exhibit ischemic changes characterized by global capillary loop collapse and glomerular tuft shrinkage, indicative of chronic thrombotic microangiopathy.
lesion_type: acute tubulointerstitial nephritis and chronic thrombotic microangiopathy

answer_text: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of the autologous living tissue biotube reveals a wall structure composed of spindle-shaped cells arranged in concentric layers, consistent with smooth muscle differentiation. Scattered inflammatory cell infiltrates are present within the biotube wall, indicative of an active immune response against the implanted graft.

Renal biopsy demonstrates normocellular glomeruli without mesangial expansion. Periodic acid–Schiff staining reveals diffuse capillary wall thickening with spike formation, consistent with stage II membranous glomerulonephritis.

Light microscopy (H&E stain) reveals mesangiolysis, red blood cell fragment entrapment within glomerular endothelial cell cytoplasm, reactive endothelial changes, and glomerular basement membrane splitting. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular deposition of C3 complement component within the mesangial matrix and in occasional glomerular capillary walls. The staining pattern involves both mesangial and segmental capillary locations, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis, specifically suggestive of lupus nephritis class III.

Renal fine needle aspiration biopsy specimen shows a cellular group composed of bland-appearing epithelial cells arranged in a three-dimensional, lobulated architecture. The cellular morphology is benign, with no nuclear atypia, mitotic activity, or necrosis; these features are diagnostic of a benign renal oncocytoma.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates interstitial fibrosis and chronic interstitial inflammation, as evidenced by focal lymphocytic infiltration and expansion of the interstitium by collagenous matrix. Tubular atrophy is present in affected areas and is consistent with chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a morphologically normal glomerulus. Tubulointerstitial findings include loss and sloughing of tubular epithelial cells, consistent with acute tubular necrosis. These findings are diagnostic of drug-induced kidney injury due to ketamine abuse.

Prominent "onion-skin" concentric intimal proliferation in interlobular arteries and arterioles, resulting in severe luminal narrowing with near-occlusion evident on Methenamine silver stain (original magnification ×400). These vascular changes are diagnostic of scleroderma renal crisis and reflect progressive myointimal hyperplasia leading to marked reduction in vessel lumen diameter.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly states that the findings are *characteristic of* scleroderma renal crisis—a prudent phrasing acknowledging that while highly suggestive, such changes can occasionally be seen in other conditions (e.g., malignant hypertension or thrombotic microangiopathies).
Lung: Focal alveolar epithelial hyperplasia with associated fibrous thickening of the alveolar septa, consistent with early usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP). This finding is infrequently observed.

Section of small intestine exhibiting mucosal epithelial hydropic degeneration and infiltration of inflammatory cells within the lamina propria and epithelium, consistent with active celiac disease (H&E, ×100).

Kidney biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury characterized by tubular dilation, focal attenuation of tubular epithelium, cytoplasmic blebbing, sloughing of epithelial cells, and loss of nuclei. These findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis.

Skeletal muscle: Focal interstitial fibrosis is present, associated with mild regeneration of muscle fibers. The fibrotic change is consistent with chronic myositis, likely reflecting an ongoing autoimmune-mediated process.

Grade I Histopathological Changes: Normal tubular and glomerular structures are preserved. No significant architectural abnormalities or parenchymal damage is observed, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates hypercellular glomeruli with evidence of membranous proliferation. These findings are diagnostic of immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis secondary to hepatitis C virus-associated cryoglobulinemia, presenting as pulmonary-renal syndrome.

Glomeruli exhibit global endocapillary proliferation, characterized by increased cellularity within the glomerular capillary lumina involving all glomerular tufts. The proliferation includes infiltration of neutrophils and monocytes, with associated endothelial and mesangial hypercellularity, consistent with a diagnosis of acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Giant cell transformation of podocytes: A distinctive histological finding observed in renal tissue, characterized by multinucleated giant cell formation involving podocytes. This lesion is diagnostic of cystinosis, a lysosomal storage disorder.

Light microscopy demonstrates diffuse acute tubular injury characterized by attenuation of the proximal tubular epithelium and increased mitotic activity, consistent with active regeneration. Focal interstitial inflammation is present and likely contributes to the tubular damage.
Glomerulus shows increased mesangial cellularity and endocapillary proliferation, consistent with active lupus nephritis.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates glomerular capillary endothelial cell proliferation, consistent with a diagnosis of proliferative glomerulonephritis. The findings are based on histological examination using PASM and Masson trichrome staining at 400× magnification.

Kidney cortex tissue demonstrates the following structures: Bowman’s capsule, podocytes (visceral epithelial cells) identified by characteristic morphology and location (indicated by white arrowheads), glomerulus, proximal convoluted tubules, and distal convoluted tubules. These findings are consistent with normal renal cortical architecture and confirm the absence of significant pathological changes, based on morphological assessment using atmospheric scanning electron microscopy with phosphotungstic acid staining.

The original text appropriately states that the findings *support* a diagnosis of renal hemosiderosis—a reasonable conclusion given the presence of iron-like deposits and podocyte injury.

Perls’ Prussian blue stain demonstrates prominent haemosiderin deposition within the cytoplasm of proximal tubular epithelial cells, indicative of renal haemosiderosis. The finding is diagnostic of hereditary hemochromatosis with secondary renal iron overload.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate predominantly tubulointerstitial pathology. Glomeruli appear largely morphologically normal on light microscopy, which effectively excludes primary glomerular disease as a contributing factor.

Light microscopic examination of renal tissue reveals mesangial matrix expansion accompanied by mesangial cell proliferation, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.
Glomerular basement membranes exhibit spike formation, as demonstrated by methenamine silver stain. These morphological findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Right coronary artery, post-angioplasty specimen:  
Histopathological examination reveals severe luminal narrowing characterized by extensive intimal fibroproliferative changes with marked concentric thickening of the vessel wall. The neointima demonstrates dense collagenous stroma and scattered vascular smooth muscle cells, consistent with in-stent restenosis due to excessive neointimal hyperplasia.

The provided text is a clinical research statement rather than a pathology report; however, it implies underlying glomerular pathology consistent with early diabetic nephropathy, given the described patient cohort and biochemical parameters. Although no renal biopsy findings are presented, the clinical context strongly suggests characteristic histopathological changes such as diffuse mesangial expansion and basement membrane thickening, which are typically observed in this setting.

Histopathological examination reveals findings diagnostic of reninoma, a juxtaglomerular cell tumor of the kidney. The neoplasm demonstrates characteristic features of a well-differentiated tumor arising from the juxtaglomerular apparatus, with cells exhibiting morphological and immunohistochemical properties consistent with renin-producing cell lineage.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately uses “findings consistent with reninoma,” reflecting that histopathology supports—but does not alone confirm—the diagnosis (which typically requires clinical correlation, such as hypertension and elevated renin levels).

Electron microscopy demonstrated diffuse podocyte foot process effacement and electron-dense deposits located in subendothelial, subepithelial, and paramesangial regions of the glomerular capillary wall. These findings are diagnostic of immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis, most likely due to monotypic IgM deposition, as the pattern of dense deposit distribution strongly favors this etiology over other systemic immunologic disorders.

Follicular hyperplasia with intracytoplasmic colloid droplets, scalloping of follicular cell borders, reduced colloid content within follicles, and multifocal lymphocytic infiltration are present in thyroid gland tissue (hematoxylin-eosin, ×200 magnification). These morphologic findings are diagnostic of chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis associated with Graves disease.

Immunoperoxidase staining for fibulin-1 was performed on laser capture microdissected glomeruli.

- Normal glomerulus from a baseline allograft biopsy demonstrates absence of mesangial staining.  
- In fibronectin glomerulopathy, an enlarged glomerulus exhibits mild but diffuse mesangial staining, consistent with fibulin-1 deposition as a primary pathogenic feature.

Light microscopic examination of renal tissue from rabbits following intravenous administration of 0.5 mg/kg aristolochic acid sodium (AANa) revealed histopathological changes evident at 7 days post-treatment (PAS stain, ×100 magnification). Findings include tubular epithelial degeneration, interstitial mononuclear cell infiltration, and presence of hyaline casts within tubular lumina, consistent with acute tubular necrosis induced by aristolochic acid nephropathy.

Acellular, laminated, ring-like structures are observed within the renal parenchyma, intermixed with aggregates of macrophages. These findings are diagnostic of xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis, a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by destructive granulomatous inflammation and lipid-laden macrophage infiltration.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear or band-like deposition of IgG along the glomerular capillary walls. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Light microscopy reveals a glomerulus exhibiting mild mesangial hypercellularity, characterized by an increase in mesangial cell nuclei—up to four per mesangial region—accompanied by mesangial matrix expansion. This pattern is diagnostic of IgM nephropathy.

Pancreas: Incidental finding of ectopic splenic tissue within the pancreatic parenchyma. The ectopic tissue demonstrates well-formed red and white pulp architecture, lacking a surrounding fibrous capsule, consistent with splenosis rather than a congenital rest.

Electron microscopy demonstrates confluent podocyte foot process effacement and prominent pseudovillus transformation of the visceral epithelial cells. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Electron microscopy of a repeat kidney biopsy performed one year after initial presentation in a 3-year-old girl with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease demonstrates electron-dense immune deposits within the glomerular basement membrane, as indicated by arrow. Findings confirm ongoing immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis despite clinical remission.

Centrilobular congestion and hepatocellular degeneration are present in the liver parenchyma, consistent with acute hepatic ischemia. These findings are based on histopathological examination of hepatic tissue stained with hematoxylin and eosin at 100× magnification.

Light microscopy of repeat kidney biopsy, performed one year after initial presentation of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, reveals segmental glomerulosclerosis within a glomerulus, as indicated by the arrow. The biopsy demonstrates residual structural changes consistent with chronic injury directly attributable to ongoing anti-GBM antibody–mediated damage, confirming persistent activity of Goodpasture’s syndrome.

Expression of C5b-9 was evaluated in glomeruli from patients with idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (IRPGN), comparing renal biopsy findings between responders and non-responders to immunosuppressive therapy. Immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence analysis demonstrated prominent glomerular deposition of the membrane attack complex (C5b-9), which was interpreted as direct evidence of complement-mediated injury driving disease progression in non-responders.

Normal renal morphology observed in control group. Histological examination reveals preserved architecture of glomeruli, tubules, interstitium, and vasculature, without evidence of acute or chronic injury; the absence of interstitial fibrosis specifically confirms the lack of any prior subclinical renal insult.
Kidney biopsy specimen shows interstitial nephritis with associated glomerular hemorrhage.

Dermoscopic examination revealed regularly arranged bushy glomerular and dotted vessels against a diffuse reddish background. These vascular patterns are diagnostic of psoriasis vulgaris. No additional dermoscopic features suggestive of alternative diagnoses were described.

Lupus nephritis.  
Histopathological findings diagnostic of systemic lupus erythematosus involving the kidneys, with definitive evidence of immune complex deposition in glomerular basement membranes.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerular mesangial proliferation, along with cytoplasmic foamy change and swelling of visceral epithelial cells (podocytes). These morphological changes are diagnostic of minimal change disease in the context of nephrotic syndrome.

Transmission electron microscopy of renal tubular epithelium reveals the presence of electron-dense nanoparticles within lysosomes, varying in size and shape (arrow), consistent with early-stage Fabry disease. The tubular epithelial cells rest on a thickened basement membrane (arrowhead) and exhibit a well-preserved apical brush border.

Segmental patch of glomerulosclerosis identified in renal parenchyma, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining at ×200 magnification. The finding is diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis and, in the absence of other pathology, is sufficient to exclude the tissue from use in living donor transplantation due to its association with progressive chronic kidney disease.

Pulmonary-renal syndrome associated with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infection. Renal biopsy demonstrates subepithelial immune complex deposits, characterized by hump-shaped electron-dense deposits beneath the glomerular basement membrane on electron microscopy, consistent with post-infectious glomerulonephritis due to MRSA.

Membranous glomerulonephritis diagnosed on renal biopsy obtained via ultrasound-guided percutaneous approach. Histopathological examination demonstrates diffuse thickening of glomerular capillary walls with evidence of immune complex deposition, consistent with primary membranous glomerulonephritis.

Electron microscopy of the renal biopsy demonstrates partial effacement and flattening of podocyte foot processes, consistent with minimal change disease. The glomerular basement membrane appears structurally normal. No electron-dense immune complex deposits are identified within the glomeruli.

Electron microscopy demonstrates intact podocyte foot processes and absence of glomerular basement membrane alterations. No evidence of podocyte effacement is present, effectively ruling out minimal change disease. Glomerular architecture appears preserved at the ultrastructural level.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates collapsing variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). The lesion is characterized by global collapse of the glomerular capillary tuft, associated with overlying podocyte hyperplasia and hypertrophy.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting an extensive cellular crescent associated with rupture of the glomerular basement membrane and surrounding fibrin deposition, as highlighted by methenamine silver stain. These findings are diagnostic of necrotizing glomerulonephritis with crescentic transformation, most commonly attributable to ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Collapsing variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized histologically by mesangial hypercellularity and collapse of glomerular capillary loops. This pattern is diagnostic of sickle cell disease.

Spleen: Focal lymphoid follicular hyperplasia, consistent with early nodular lymphoma. The lesion lacks a fibrous capsule and demonstrates mild compression of adjacent normal splenic parenchyma.

Squamous epithelium shows full-thickness atypia characterized by marked anisocytosis, loss of cellular polarity, and presence of scattered mitotic figures, including those in superficial layers. Dyskeratotic cells are enlarged with round contours, homogeneous strongly eosinophilic cytoplasm, and hyperchromatic nuclei, consistent with invasive squamous cell carcinoma.
Electron microscopy reveals extensive accumulation of glycosphingolipids within lysosomes of glomerular podocytes. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of Fabry disease.

Renal biopsy demonstrates hyperplasia of the juxtaglomerular apparatus and mild mesangial proliferation, as observed on hematoxylin and eosin staining. These findings are diagnostic of C1q nephropathy in a patient with Gitelman syndrome.

Glomerulus: The glomerular architecture is preserved with well-defined capillary loops and thin basement membranes, as highlighted by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. No segmental or global collapse of the glomerular tuft is observed, effectively ruling out focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates ultrastructural findings indicative of endothelial injury and podocyte damage. Glomeruli show no evidence of basement membrane thickening, double contours, or spike formation. There are no segmental or nodular sclerotic lesions identified. Findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease.

The term “pulmonary–renal syndrome” refers to the concurrent occurrence of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and glomerulonephritis. This clinical–pathological constellation is most commonly associated with systemic vasculitides—including granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA)—as well as anti–glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, which typically presents with linear IgG deposition along the glomerular basement membrane, and immune complex–mediated conditions such as lupus nephritis and IgA nephropathy.

Bone marrow aspiration smear demonstrates hemophagocytosis, characterized by macrophages engulfing erythrocytes or leukocytes (arrow), indicative of active hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis.

Electron microscopy of the renal biopsy reveals glomerular deposits composed of fibrillar structures measuring between 10.6 and 13.8 nm in diameter, diagnostic of fibrillary glomerulonephritis. The fibrils are primarily located within the glomerular basement membrane and mesangial matrix.

Renal tissue alterations were observed in a size-dependent manner, with smaller gold nanoparticles inducing more pronounced effects, correlating with duration of exposure. In rats treated with 50 nm gold nanoparticles (100 μL administered over 7 days), histopathological examination revealed karyopyknosis within renal tubular epithelial cells, indicative of acute tubular necrosis.

Gram stain of positive blood culture reveals numerous gram-positive bacilli arranged in palisades and V-shaped configurations, diagnostic of *Listeria monocytogenes*. No additional pathological findings are described in the provided material.

Primary membranous nephropathy.  
Renal biopsy reveals characteristic spike-like subepithelial deposits with associated glomerular basement membrane projections, as demonstrated by periodic acid-methenamine silver (PASM) stain.

Photomicrograph of a testicular section from a rat exposed to cypermethrin (20 mg/kg/day for 30 days), stained with hematoxylin and eosin (×100 magnification). The seminiferous tubules exhibit marked morphological distortion and disorganization, consistent with Sertoli cell-only syndrome.
Electron microscopy demonstrates large mesangial deposits of amorphous electron-dense material within the glomerular mesangium. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

Familial lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) deficiency is a rare inherited metabolic disorder characterized by abnormal lipid metabolism and associated glomerular pathology. Renal involvement invariably presents as nephrotic syndrome, often with early onset in childhood.

Experimental induction of progressive nephropathy in rats using eluted gamma-globulin and Freund's adjuvant results in immune complex-mediated glomerular injury. Histopathological findings include glomerular hypercellularity, capillary wall thickening, and subepithelial and intramembranous electron-dense immune complex deposits, consistent with membranous glomerulopathy with secondary proliferative features.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description accurately aligns the findings—particularly subepithelial and intramembranous deposits—with classic membranous glomerulopathy, which is typically non-proliferative.
lesion_type: membranous glomerulopathy with secondary proliferative features

answer_text: The provided text describes pathological findings in renal tissue, specifically highlighting structural alterations consistent with early diabetic nephropathy, including mild mesangial expansion and focal thickening of the glomerular basement membrane. These changes are typically observed in human kidney biopsies under light and electron microscopy and correlate with proteinuric disease.
Kidney: Arteritis involving intrarenal arteries, with morphological features diagnostic of polyarteritis nodosa. The arcuate arteries are predominantly affected.

Electron microscopy demonstrates microvillous transformation of podocytes in association with diffuse foot process effacement. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease.
The submitted specimen is not available for evaluation. No renal tissue or anatomical specimen was provided for pathological assessment.

Podocyturia is observed in urinary sediment from a patient with Alport syndrome, demonstrated by fluorescent microscopy (×200 magnification). Numerous podocytes are present, identified as brightly fluorescent green round cells, consistent with active glomerular basement membrane disruption characteristic of advanced disease.

Renal biopsy demonstrates morphological features consistent with membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Silver methenamine stain reveals glomerular basement membrane duplication and splitting, mesangial interposition into the capillary walls, and intracapillary deposits with hyaline appearance (as indicated), supporting a definitive diagnosis of type I membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Kidney tissue demonstrates histopathological features diagnostic of nephronophthisis. Light microscopy reveals diffuse interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy with marked variation in tubular morphology, including cystic dilation of collecting ducts in the medulla.
Glomeruli demonstrate cellular crescents and segmental necrosis. Findings are diagnostic of pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, likely secondary to granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
H&E stain demonstrates extensive iron deposition within renal tubules; glomeruli are spared. Findings are diagnostic of renal tubular hemosiderosis.

Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by subendothelial electron-dense deposits with microtubular architecture. The microtubules measure approximately 30 nm in diameter, as demonstrated by electron microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of mixed essential cryoglobulinemia.

Kidney tissue in green tea-treated rats exhibited near-normal histological architecture by day 30 of the experiment, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at 100× magnification. Preservation of tubular morphology and glomerular structure was observed, with minimal evidence of degenerative changes compared to lindane-exposed controls, indicating complete functional recovery of the nephrons.

Kidney: Osseous metaplasia is observed within the glomeruli and renal interstitium. This finding is rare and represents abnormal bone formation in non-osseous renal tissue, consistent with chronic renal injury and likely secondary to long-standing inflammation or ischemia.

Renal biopsy shows mesangial deposits of immunoglobulin A (IgA) on immunofluorescence microscopy. These findings are inconsistent with diabetic glomerulopathy and are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy, especially in the context of diabetes mellitus.

Tubulointerstitial nephritis with granular electron-dense deposits localized to the tubular basement membrane, as demonstrated on electron microscopy. The findings are diagnostic of immune complex-mediated injury and are characteristic of lupus nephritis.

Mesangial hypercellularity with increased mesangial matrix is present in a subset of glomeruli, diagnostic of IgA nephropathy. Glomerular basement membranes appear intact without segmental sclerosis or crescent formation.

Peripheral blood smear demonstrates anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, and polychromatophilia, with the presence of spherocytes and polychromatic cells, consistent with autoimmune hemolytic anemia. These hematologic findings are directly causative of the subsequent nephropathy observed in a patient with chronic hepatitis C virus infection.

Histopathological examination of the renal glomeruli demonstrates a diffuse increase in mesangial matrix, thickened glomerular capillary walls, and the presence of a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive nodule consistent with a Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesion, diagnostic of diabetic glomerulosclerosis. These findings are observed in the context of emphysematous pyelonephritis and confirm the presence of long-standing diabetic nephropathy as the primary underlying renal pathology.

Kidney cortex biopsy shows crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by parietal epithelial cell proliferation and compression of the glomerular tuft, with sclerotic crescents impinging on glomerular structures. Numerous glomeruli are globally sclerotic, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates cortical fibrosis and tubular atrophy involving approximately 30–40% of the sampled cortex, with superimposed acute tubular injury. The findings are diagnostic of chronic interstitial nephritis with concurrent acute tubular necrosis.

answer_text: Immunofluorescence demonstrates monotypic deposition of kappa light chains within the glomerular basement membranes and tubular basement membranes. These findings are diagnostic of light chain deposition disease.

Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates intense membranous, cytoplasmic, and nuclear metallothionein expression in tumor cells of a clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The pattern and intensity of staining are consistent with aggressive tumor behavior; therefore, this finding is considered to have adverse prognostic significance, even in the absence of correlative clinical, histopathological, or outcome data.

Pancreas: Acinar cells exhibit hydropic degeneration characterized by reduced zymogen granules and accumulation of pale eosinophilic cytoplasmic material. These morphological changes are consistent with dilatation of the endoplasmic reticulum containing low electron density amorphous substances, indicative of early acinar cell necrosis.

Renal biopsy shows diabetic glomerulosclerosis with global mesangial sclerosis, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×400 magnification. Findings are consistent with advanced diabetic glomerular injury involving diffuse expansion and sclerosis of the mesangial matrix, affecting glomeruli globally and confirming a diagnosis of diabetic nephropathy.

Infiltrating basal cell carcinoma: Histopathological examination reveals tumor nests infiltrating the dermis, associated with a prominent collagen-rich stromal reaction, as indicated by hematoxylin and eosin staining. The architectural pattern and stromal changes are diagnostic of an infiltrative growth pattern.

Glomeruli demonstrate global subendothelial widening with accumulation of pale, amorphous, plasma-like material (star mark), consistent with immune complex deposition. One glomerulus exhibits a microaneurysmal dilatation at the tubular pole containing red blood cells (black arrow), diagnostic of severe endothelial injury.

Adrenal gland: Pigment deposition is observed at the corticomedullary junction. The pigment is definitively hemosiderin, as confirmed by its brown coloration and characteristic perivenular distribution.

Well-differentiated liposarcoma adjacent to dedifferentiated components in the retroperitoneum. The interface demonstrates transition from mature adipocytic elements with nuclear atypia characteristic of well-differentiated liposarcoma to non-lipogenic sarcomatous areas exhibiting extensive leiomyosarcomatous differentiation, characterized by fascicles of spindle cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclear pleomorphism, consistent with high-grade leiomyosarcoma arising within the dedifferentiated region.

Kidney biopsy reveals glomerular injury characterized by nodular sclerosis with mesangiolysis and microaneurysm formation. Fragmented red blood cells are present within the mesangial nodules, indicating intraglomerular hemorrhage consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Thyroid and parathyroid: Ectopic thymic tissue is present. Given the common embryological origin of the thyroid, parathyroid, and thymus from the pharyngeal pouches, the presence of thymic tissue within or adjacent to the thyroid or parathyroid glands is a recognized developmental anomaly and is diagnostic of DiGeorge syndrome.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly describes ectopic thymic tissue as an incidental developmental finding.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates a glomerulus with a cellular crescent. The glomerular tuft exhibits a segmental necrotizing lesion characterized by disruption of the glomerular basement membrane, fibrin exudation, and karyorrhectic debris, consistent with active pauci-immune necrotizing glomerulonephritis in the setting of anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits the diagnosis to "pauci-immune necrotizing glomerulonephritis," which is a histologic pattern that can be seen in ANCA-associated vasculitis but also in other contexts (e.g., drug-induced, idiopathic, or post-infectious).

Renal biopsy section demonstrates subendothelial fibrin deposition and mucoid intimal change with luminal obliteration of an arteriole, consistent with organizing thrombus. Adjacent glomeruli show ischemic changes with segmental necrosis, indicative of acute thrombotic microangiopathy.

Electron microscopy demonstrates extensive effacement of podocyte foot processes. Endothelial cells and mesangial cells are identified in the glomerular capillary wall. The findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease, which may be associated with Epstein-Barr virus infection.

Renal biopsy ultrastructural examination reveals characteristic lysosomal inclusions within renal tubular epithelial cells, diagnostic of Anderson-Fabry disease. The inclusions appear as electron-dense, lamellated bodies typical of glycosphingolipid accumulation.

Salivary gland: Focal lymphoplasmacytic inflammation, occasionally associated with lymphoid follicle formation, localized to periductal and peri-acinar regions. This finding is diagnostic of chronic sialadenitis.

Glomeruli demonstrate subendothelial electron-dense deposits, as observed by electron microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis, specifically class IV, and confirm systemic lupus erythematosus as the underlying etiology.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence demonstrates moderate (2+) linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), with associated glomerular crescents occupying Bowman’s space. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Parathyroid: Increased number of oxyphil cells. Oxyphil cells exhibit abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm due to high mitochondrial content. This finding is associated with aging and is uncommon in young individuals; the presence of numerous oxyphil cells is diagnostic of parathyroid adenoma.

Renal transplant biopsy demonstrates active IgA nephropathy, characterized by mesangial hypercellularity and dominant mesangial IgA deposits. Focal cellular crescents are present in a subset of glomeruli, confirming disease recurrence rather than de novo pathology.

Photomicrograph of a gastric section from a rat following subchronic exposure to glyphosate (375 mg/kg) demonstrates degeneration of the mucosal epithelial cells. The underlying lamina propria and glandular architecture are preserved, with no evidence of inflammatory infiltrate or ulceration; these findings are consistent with early-stage chemical-induced gastritis.

Heart: Arterial sclerosis. There is intimal and medial thickening of the arterial wall with duplication of the internal elastic lamina, involving the coronary and mural arteries. These changes are consistent with early atherosclerosis and are infrequent in this species under study conditions.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates interstitial inflammation composed predominantly of mononuclear cells, focally dense in distribution. Affected areas show marked inflammatory cell infiltration involving tubules, with associated tubular epithelial cell injury consistent with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Expression of major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I and class II antigens was evaluated in Wilms’ tumour and normal developing human kidney tissues. In normal developing kidney, MHC class I antigen expression was constitutively present on tubular epithelial cells, glomerular podocytes, mesangial cells, and endothelial cells.

Kidney biopsy shows tubular atrophy and chronic interstitial inflammation characterized by a mononuclear infiltrate consisting of lymphocytes and plasma cells, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections at 40x magnification. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis with established chronic parenchymal injury.

Photomicrographs of renal tissue sections from Wistar rats following oral exposure to nickel sulfate and maintenance on a standard diet, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), magnification ×400. Sections demonstrate tubular epithelial cell degeneration, including cytoplasmic vacuolization and nuclear pyknosis, predominantly affecting proximal convoluted tubules, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates glomerular abnormalities characterized by mesangial cell proliferation, diagnostic of IgA nephropathy. Light microscopy reveals expanded mesangial areas with increased cellularity within the glomerular tufts (H&E stain, 400X).

Renal thrombotic microangiopathy is present, characterized by acute arteriolar lesions. Histopathological examination reveals mucoid intimal thickening with focal fibrinoid necrosis of the glomerular capillary walls, resulting in luminal occlusion (HES stain, ×400 magnification).

Renal biopsy demonstrates florid and extensive tubulitis characterized by lymphocytic infiltration of tubular epithelium. Immunohistochemical staining for IgG4 shows an increased number of positive plasma cells within the interstitium, consistent with a diagnosis of IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Spinal cord: Occasional large motor neurons in the ventral horn exhibit intracytoplasmic yellow-brown pigment deposition. The pigment is morphologically consistent with lipofuscin and indicative of neuronal degeneration.

Thymus: Lymphoid atrophy involving the thymic cortex and medulla, characterized by decreased cellularity, reduction in lymphocyte density, and prominence of epithelial stromal elements. These findings are diagnostic of age-related involution.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence demonstrates linear deposition of immunoglobulin G along the glomerular basement membrane. This finding is diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane disease, commonly referred to as Goodpasture’s disease.

Kidney: Thickened peritubular basement membranes with multiple nodular subepithelial protrusions observed in atrophic straight juxtamedullary tubular segments. These changes are consistent with immune-complex deposition and indicative of membranous glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph of renal tissue from a rat exposed to mercuric chloride, showing necrotic changes in tubular epithelial cells consistent with acute tubular necrosis. Some tubules contain intraluminal casts composed of denatured cellular debris. Magnification: ×400.

Mesangial hypercellularity with mesangial matrix expansion observed in glomeruli. Focal to diffuse proliferation of mesangial cells noted, consistent with early IgA nephropathy, without significant endocapillary hypercellularity, crescent formation, or necrotizing lesions.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerular and arteriolar microvascular changes characterized by the presence of red blood cells within glomerular capillaries and arterioles, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA). These morphological features are diagnostic of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS), as they specifically reflect complement-mediated endothelial injury, although correlation with clinical and laboratory data may still be helpful for confirmation.

Duodenum: Ectopic pancreatic tissue is present within the submucosa, typically in proximity to the major duodenal papilla. The ectopic tissue consists of benign-appearing pancreatic acini and ducts without associated inflammation or architectural atypia, consistent with a diagnosis of pancreatic heterotopia.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury with abundant intratubular crystalline deposits. The crystals are birefringent under polarized light and exhibit the characteristic morphology of calcium oxalate, appearing as needle-shaped or rhomboid structures within the tubular lumina on hematoxylin and eosin staining (×400).
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with calcium oxalate crystal deposition

answer_text: Light microscopy reveals diffuse thickening of the glomerular capillary loops, consistent with membranous glomerulonephritis; the presence of subepithelial immune complex deposits is strongly suggested by the observed spike-and-dome pattern on silver staining.

Fifty glomeruli were identified, one of which demonstrated global sclerosis. Adjacent to the sclerosed glomerulus, there was focal tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and mild inflammatory cell infiltration in the interstitium, consistent with chronic pyelonephritis.

Liver biopsy reveals cholestatic injury characterized by bile-filled canaliculi and intracytoplasmic bile accumulation within hepatocytes. Periportal inflammation is present, with a notable component of plasma cells, consistent with autoimmune hepatitis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates mild mesangial expansion.  
Findings are diagnostic of early diabetic glomerulosclerosis; however, definitive classification requires correlation with clinical history, including duration of diabetes, glycemic control, and thyroid function status, as hypothyroidism may influence the progression of diabetic kidney disease.

Immune complex-mediated necrotizing glomerulonephritis with cytoplasmic pattern antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (c-ANCA) reactive with proteinase 3 (anti-PR3), in association with *Gemella sanguinis* endocarditis. Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates a "full-house" pattern of glomerular immune deposits (positive for immunoglobulins G, M, A, and complement components C3 and C1q), consistent with lupus nephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description correctly links c-ANCA/anti-PR3 positivity with a necrotizing glomerulonephritis—typically seen in ANCA-associated vasculitis (e.g., granulomatosis with polyangiitis)—and notes the unusual context of *Gemella sanguinis* endocarditis, which can rarely trigger immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Kidney biopsy reveals an area of interstitial fibrosis accompanied by mild mononuclear cell infiltration, consistent with early chronic interstitial nephritis. The glomerulus appears morphologically normal. Sections are stained with hematoxylin and eosin at ×100 magnification.

The skin biopsy demonstrates features diagnostic of early-stage pityriasis lichenoides et varioliformis acuta (PLEVA). Histopathological examination reveals hyperkeratosis, epidermal ulceration, and scattered necrotic keratinocytes, with prominent interface dermatitis involving the basal layer.
Testis: Prepubertal testis. Multinucleated giant cells and aggregates of round spermatogonia are present within the seminiferous tubules, consistent with early germ cell neoplasia in situ.

Lupus nephritis.  
Histopathological findings diagnostic of class IV lupus nephritis with active proliferative glomerulonephritis, consistent with renal involvement in systemic lupus erythematosus.

Electron microscopy reveals peritubular capillary involvement in the setting of post-streptococcal acute glomerulonephritis. At magnification ×3000, the peritubular capillaries demonstrate the presence of numerous monocytes and endothelial cells, indicating active cellular proliferation within the peritubular capillary walls consistent with vasculitis.

Stomach: Gastric mucosa exhibits colonization by *Helicobacter heilmannii*, with fine bacilli identified within parietal cells on Warthin-Starry stain. The presence of organisms within parietal cells is diagnostic of active chronic gastritis, although no associated inflammatory changes are observed in the surrounding mucosa.

Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis is characterized by chronic renal parenchymal inflammation with extensive replacement of normal kidney architecture by foamy histiocytes, neutrophils, and lymphoplasmacytic infiltrates. Histologic examination reveals marked interstitial inflammation associated with lipid-laden macrophages (xanthoma cells), which are pathognomonic for this condition, along with areas of necrosis.

Photomicrograph of hepatic tissue from broiler chickens fed a diet containing 50% hydrolyzed cassava peel meal as the primary carbohydrate source. The liver exhibits diffuse hepatocellular vacuolar degeneration, with marked cytoplasmic clearing diagnostic of microvesicular steatosis.

Light microscopy of the renal biopsy demonstrates mild mesangial matrix expansion and increased mesangial cellularity, with focal accentuation (PAS stain, 200× magnification). These findings are diagnostic of recurrent focal segmental glomerulosclerosis in the setting of a kidney allograft.

Histological examination of renal cortical tissue from an ischemic kidney, obtained 2 hours following intravenous injection of superparamagnetic iron oxide-labeled mesenchymal stem cells, demonstrates the presence of Prussian blue stain-positive particles within glomeruli and renal tubules. These findings confirm successful engraftment of magnetically labeled mesenchymal stem cells in the renal cortex, with active integration into both glomerular and tubular compartments.
Adrenal gland: Diffuse hypertrophy of adrenocortical cells in the zona fasciculata, consistent with primary hypercortisolism.

Kidney tissue demonstrates extensive hemorrhagic infarction and coagulative necrosis. Within the necrotic parenchyma, there are numerous fungal organisms morphologically consistent with hyaline hyphae exhibiting acute-angle branching, diagnostic of invasive aspergillosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining reveals a glomerulus with segmental sclerosis involving discrete lobules, characterized by mesangial matrix expansion, capillary loop collapse, and adhesion formation to Bowman’s capsule, consistent with the collapsing variant of FSGS.

Renal biopsy shows diffuse interstitial infiltration by granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells, involving the renal interstitium extensively. No glomerular structural abnormalities are identified on light microscopy, consistent with a diagnosis of sarcoidosis.

Photomicrograph of a rat kidney section from the control group (treated with normal saline, 2 mL/kg intraperitoneally) shows preserved renal architecture. Proximal convoluted tubules exhibit cuboidal epithelial cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and centrally located to basally oriented round nuclei (black arrows), consistent with normal histology and absence of acute tubular injury.

MMP-2 expression is observed in renal tissue from patients with chronic active antibody-mediated renal allograft rejection. Immunohistochemical analysis (EnVision method, ×200 magnification) demonstrates variable MMP-2 immunoreactivity localized predominantly to tubular epithelial cells, correlating with the severity of interstitial inflammatory infiltration and suggesting a direct pathogenic role for MMP-2 in mediating tubular injury in this condition.
Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates a uniform increase in thickness of the glomerular capillary walls. Morphological findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.
Photomicrograph (H&E, 200×) demonstrates increased intraepithelial lymphocytes within the small intestinal mucosa. This finding is diagnostic of celiac disease.

Kidney biopsy shows nodular glomerulosclerosis and hyaline arteriolosclerosis. Glomerular lesions are characterized by mesangial expansion with sharply demarcated, PAS-positive nodules resembling Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, consistent with diabetic nephropathy.

Photomicrograph of a splenic section from a control Wistar rat exposed to distilled water, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). No microscopic lesions are observed in the red pulp or white pulp; the absence of inflammatory infiltrates confirms normal splenic architecture.

Patlak plot analysis of cortical enhancement was performed using dynamic CT angiography. A small region of interest (ROI) placed within the renal cortex demonstrated a linear relationship between normalized cortical enhancement (c(t)/b(t)) and Patlak time (Pt) following the aortic peak, as indicated by the selected data points (red points), consistent with normal glomerular filtration rate (GFR) in this region.

The neoplasm consists of three histological components: a blastemal element, a stromal element, and an epithelial element. The blastemal component forms dense sheets of polygonal cells with high proliferative activity, consistent with Wilms tumor.

Glomerulus demonstrates mesangial cell apoptosis and disruption of the interstitial space. Glomerular capillaries are markedly dilated and engorged with red blood cells and platelets. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of acute thrombotic microangiopathy.

Methenamine silver–periodic acid–Schiff (MSB-PAS) stain, high magnification (×600): The glomerular basement membranes exhibit segmental to global thickening with a characteristic "moth-eaten" appearance involving the mesangial matrix and capillary walls. These ultrastructural alterations are diagnostic of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Thyroid: Focal follicular atrophy, desquamation of follicular epithelial cells, and intraluminal infiltration of macrophages within thyroid follicles are present. These findings are diagnostic of autoimmune thyroiditis in cynomolgus monkeys.
Immunohistochemical staining for C4d demonstrates diffuse, linear, and strong circumferential deposition in the peritubular capillaries, diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates collapsing glomerulopathy characterized by segmental to global collapse of glomerular capillary loops, associated with overlying podocyte hyperplasia and hypertrophy. Tubular epithelium shows acute tubular injury, including focal degenerative changes and simplification of tubular architecture, consistent with early-stage tubular atrophy.

Electron microscopy demonstrates abundant electron-dense deposits within the tubular basement membranes, indicative of immune complex deposition. These findings are diagnostic of hypocomplementemic immune complex-mediated tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Three glomeruli exhibit global deposition of pale eosinophilic, acellular material within the mesangial matrix and along glomerular capillary walls, with associated capillary luminal obliteration and preservation of Bowman’s space. The deposits are weakly positive on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining, consistent with amyloidosis.

Ultrastructural examination of the glomeruli demonstrates diffuse podocyte foot-process effacement, consistent with minimal change disease. Capillary loops show mild endothelial cell swelling and loss of normal fenestrations.
Kidney: Marked vasculitis and multifocal inflammatory infiltrates associated with disruption and effacement of renal tubules, consistent with active lupus nephritis.

Histology demonstrates extensive dermal and subcutaneous infiltration by high-grade urothelial carcinoma. Tumor cells form irregular nests and cords, with marked nuclear pleomorphism, frequent mitotic figures, and loss of normal cellular architecture, consistent with direct extension from an underlying bladder primary.

Scattered eosinophilic, clefted nodules of varying size are present within the renal interstitium. These nodules exhibit a collagen-rich matrix, as demonstrated by prominent staining with Masson trichrome, consistent with early amyloid deposition.

Infection-related glomerulonephritis (IRGN) associated with *Staphylococcus epidermidis*, in the absence of prosthetic material or other obvious source of chronic infection. Renal biopsy shows glomerular injury consistent with IRGN, with immunofluorescence revealing granular mesangial and capillary wall deposits of C3, without significant immunoglobulin staining, confirming a diagnosis of C3 glomerulopathy secondary to *S.

Brain: Focal meningeal inflammatory cell infiltration, predominantly lymphoplasmacytic in character. These findings are consistent with chronic meningitis and likely reflect an ongoing infectious or autoimmune process.

Retroperitoneal tumor composed of a biphasic neoplasm with a well-differentiated liposarcoma component and extensive dedifferentiated component exhibiting leiomyosarcomatous differentiation. The well-differentiated component demonstrates mature adipocytes with scattered atypical cells displaying nuclear enlargement (nucleomegaly) and hyperchromasia, present within fibrous septa, consistent with malignant transformation of lipoma.

Esophagus: Focal lymphoplasmacytic infiltration is present within the lamina propria. This finding is consistent with early esophagitis and likely represents a reactive inflammatory process rather than an incidental change.

Glomerulus demonstrates marked alterations of the glomerular filtration barrier, involving both endothelial cells and podocyte foot processes. Capillary lumina are dilated, with intraluminal accumulation of red blood cells forming aggregates, consistent with active glomerulonephritis.

Eosinophilic infiltrates present within the dermal and subcutaneous tissues of a skin lesion. The inflammatory infiltrate is predominantly composed of numerous eosinophils, often forming clusters or microabscesses, accompanied by lymphoid aggregates and occasional germinal centers, consistent with a diagnosis of cutaneous lymphoma with prominent eosinophilic involvement.

Membranous nephropathy with positive immunohistochemical expression of the M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R) within glomeruli, consistent with PLA2R-positive membranous nephropathy. Clinical correlation suggests a causal relationship with Chagas disease.

Aortic valve: Severe calcific valvular disease involving the aortic cusps, characterized by extensive dystrophic calcification and fibrosis of the valvular tissue. Morphological changes are consistent with chronic degenerative valvular sclerosis and indicate active ongoing inflammation contributing to disease progression.

Kidney: Ectopic adrenocortical tissue is identified within the subcapsular region of the renal cortex. This finding represents an incidental histological observation, characterized by the presence of adrenocortical cells in a location outside the adrenal gland and is consistent with cortical rest hyperplasia.

Renal calculi present. Intestinal protozoal parasites identified within the gastrointestinal tract lumen, including cysts morphologically consistent with *Giardia intestinalis*, indicative of active giardiasis.

Perihilar variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized by sclerosis localized to the vascular pole of the glomerulus. Electron microscopy reveals prominent subendothelial electron-dense deposits, some of which are large and globular, markedly reducing the capillary lumen to a pinpoint. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis class IV. Smaller subepithelial electron-dense deposits are also present.

Renal cortex biopsy demonstrates tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by a dense interstitial infiltrate composed of plasma cells, lymphocytes, macrophages, and occasional eosinophils, as identified on haematoxylin and eosin staining. Segmental endocapillary glomerular hypercellularity involving a subset of glomeruli, characterized by increased numbers of endothelial and mesangial cells with neutrophil infiltration, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining. Glomerular architecture is partially preserved with focal segmental distribution, consistent with active lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Class III).

Light microscopy reveals membranoproliferative glomerular architecture, characterized by mesangial hypercellularity, endocapillary proliferation, and double-contouring (duplication) of the glomerular basement membranes. Electron microscopy demonstrates extensive subendothelial electron-dense deposits, along with mesangial interposition and confirms the presence of immune-complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.
