Section of kidney demonstrates moderate glomerular necrosis, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis. No additional specific tubular, interstitial, or vascular lesions are described.

Renal biopsy tissue from a patient with lupus nephritis, post-immunosuppressive therapy, shows sparse immunohistochemical expression of interferon (IFN)-λ in tubular epithelial cells. This finding suggests persistent type III interferon signaling despite immunosuppression.

Findings include tubular epithelial cell swelling, cytoplasmic vacuolization, necrosis, and desquamation of epithelial cells into the tubular lumen, consistent with acute tubular necrosis as the definitive cause of renal dysfunction in this model.

Renal biopsy demonstrates membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by enlarged glomeruli with increased cellularity due to mesangial hypercellularity and leukocytic infiltration. The findings are consistent with active myositis involving the renal parenchyma.

answer_text: Duplication of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) is observed in some glomerular capillary loops, as demonstrated by Jones methenamine silver stain. These findings are diagnostic of acute transplant glomerulopathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular capillary engorgement with intracapillary erythrocyte accumulation and mesangiolysis, as evidenced by disruption of the mesangial matrix and loss of mesangial cellularity. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, with secondary changes attributable to concurrent malignant hypertension and thrombotic microangiopathy.

Kidney tissue from a cat with feline spongiform encephalopathy (FSE) was examined using immunohistochemistry for PrP<sup>Sc</sup> detection. The PBS control section shows no immunoreactivity within glomeruli or tubular structures.

Acute renal allograft dysfunction secondary to mechanical obstruction of the allograft ureter. The distal small bowel and proximal colonic mesentery herniated posterior to the intraperitoneal allograft ureter, resulting in cecal volvulus and consequent ureteral compression.

Amyloid A amyloidosis involving renal parenchyma, characterized by extracellular deposition of congophilic amyloid fibrils exhibiting apple-green birefringence under polarized light following Congo red staining. Immunohistochemical staining confirms AA-type amyloid deposition as the underlying cause of glomerular dysfunction.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates granulomatous interstitial nephritis with yeast-like fungal organisms identified within interstitial granulomas and tubular lumina. The organisms are definitively identified as Candida albicans based on their morphology and positive staining on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain at 400× magnification.
lesion_type: granulomatous interstitial nephritis due to candida albicans

answer_text:
Microscopic examination of renal tissue reveals microfilariae within glomerular capillary lumina. The organisms exhibit elongated morphology with uniformly distributed, parallel-aligned nuclei extending along their entire length and are surrounded by an external sheath, consistent with morphological features of *Wuchereria bancrofti* microfilariae (Periodic acid–Schiff stain, ×100).

These findings confirm a diagnosis of filarial nephropathy as the definitive cause of the patient’s macroscopic hematuria and nephrotic-range proteinuria.

Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) of the ovary. The tumor exhibits a triphasic histological pattern, comprising blastemal cells (arrow), stromal components (arrowhead), and epithelial elements, including abortive tubules and glomeruloid structures (star), consistent with definitive diagnosis of nephroblastoma arising in ovarian tissue.

Photomicrograph of a representative section of testis, magnification 100×. Seminiferous tubules exhibit preserved architecture with evidence of chronic toxic injury, including germ cell depletion and disorganization of the germinal epithelium, consistent with Sertoli cell-only syndrome.

Pigmented Bowen’s disease: Full-thickness epidermal atypia characterized by loss of cellular polarity, nuclear pleomorphism, increased mitotic activity, dyskeratosis, and dyskaryosis. Scattered melanophages are present in the papillary dermis, with marked hyperpigmentation of basal and suprabasal keratinocytes, consistent with concomitant lentigo maligna.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “consistent with concomitant lentigo maligna” is an unwarranted diagnostic overinterpretation.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular immunofluorescence with diffuse granular mesangial deposition of C3, diagnostic of C3 glomerulonephritis. Clinical correlation indicates concomitant granulomatosis with polyangiitis, which is likely the underlying cause of the C3-dominant immune complex deposition.

C3 glomerulopathy was identified in the context of underlying multiple myeloma. Light microscopy revealed mesangial hypercellularity and neutrophilic infiltration within the glomerular tufts, consistent with active endocapillary proliferation; however, no crescent formation or necrosis was observed.

Kidney, cortex: The cortical parenchyma contains glomeruli with preserved architecture, characterized by evenly distributed capillary loops and surrounding Bowman’s capsule. The glomerular tufts are well-defined and exhibit a normal cellular composition, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy in the absence of overt structural changes.

Kidney: Immature glomerulus. The glomerular capillary architecture is poorly defined, with prominent epithelial cells characteristic of immature glomerular development, consistent with congenital nephrotic syndrome.

Ultrastructural examination demonstrates widespread acute podocyte injury characterized by near-total foot process effacement, microvillous transformation of the podocyte cell membrane, and cytoplasmic condensation of the actin cytoskeleton. These findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease.

C3 glomerulopathy characterized by intense C3 deposition in the glomerular basement membranes and mesangial regions, as demonstrated by immunofluorescence microscopy. The histological findings are diagnostic of dense deposit disease.
Section of small intestine exhibiting hydropic degeneration of the mucosal epithelium with associated cellular sloughing. Findings are diagnostic of acute viral enteritis.

**Pathology Description:**

Histopathological examination reveals a cutaneous neoplasm composed of basaloid tumor cells arranged in interconnected strands and nests within the papillary and upper reticular dermis, consistent with superficial basal cell carcinoma. The tumor cells exhibit hyperchromatic nuclei, scant cytoplasm, and peripheral palisading, with focal extension into the deep reticular dermis suggesting early invasive potential.

Immunoperoxidase staining for podocin was performed on renal biopsy specimens to assess podocyte integrity in various glomerular diseases.

- In a baseline allograft biopsy with morphologically normal glomeruli, strong linear podocin staining was observed along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with preserved podocyte expression.

- In a case of fibronectin glomerulopathy, the glomerulus was enlarged and showed absence of mesangial staining, with only faint, attenuated staining along the capillary walls, suggesting reduced podocin expression and likely podocyte detachment.

- In diabetic glomerulosclerosis, there was no detectable podocin staining within the expanded mesangial regions or along the capillary walls, indicating complete podocyte loss due to advanced sclerosis.

- In proliferative lupus nephritis, mesangial areas lacked staining, and podocyte staining was markedly diminished or absent along the capillary walls, consistent with podocyte injury or depletion.

These findings demonstrate variable loss of podocin immunoreactivity across different glomerulopathies, correlating with structural podocyte damage.

Gastric antral mucosa exhibiting markedly enlarged folds with a polypoid lesion displaying a double-headed morphology, as observed endoscopically. The polyp involves the antral mucosa and is associated with significant fold hypertrophy, consistent with hyperplastic polyposis syndrome.

Arterioles exhibit smooth muscle actin (SMA)-positive staining in the medial smooth muscle layer and in pericytes, including some intraluminal cells. Immunohistochemical labeling is consistent with a myofibroblastic or perivascular mesenchymal cell phenotype, supporting a diagnosis of early arteriolar hyalinosis.
Electron microscopy demonstrated subendothelial electron-dense deposits and diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis class IV.

The provided text describes a renal histopathological analysis featuring glomerular basement membrane thickening and mesangial matrix expansion, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. It references a molecular docking and simulation study on aldose reductase inhibitors from spices and includes a figure citation related to the polyol pathway (pone.0138186.g001).

Medium-power histological examination reveals a glomerulus with mild mesangial prominence and absence of significant mesangial hypercellularity (PAS stain, ×200). Immunofluorescence studies demonstrated diffuse granular mesangial deposition of IgM, which is diagnostic of IgM nephropathy.

Whipple’s disease is a rare systemic disorder that may involve the kidney and is often diagnosed late. Jejunal biopsy demonstrates numerous periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive macrophages within the lamina propria, a pathognomonic histological finding diagnostic of Whipple’s disease.

Focal perivascular and interstitial lymphocytic infiltration is observed in the renal parenchyma of the mixed-treatment group, as demonstrated at ×400 magnification. The finding indicates a mild inflammatory cellular response within the kidney tissue; however, no specific structural damage or glomerular, tubular, or vascular lesions are evident, suggesting an early stage of drug-induced interstitial nephritis.

The glomerulus exhibits hypercellularity characterized by an increased number of intraglomerular polymorphonuclear and mononuclear inflammatory cells, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining. These findings are diagnostic of acute transplant glomerulopathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates acute on chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis in the setting of a nephrotic presentation associated with hydatid cyst disease. Concomitant mesangioproliferative glomerulonephritis is present and is likely the primary driver of the nephrotic syndrome.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately notes the coexistence of tubulointerstitial nephritis, hydatid cyst disease, and mesangioproliferative glomerulonephritis without assigning causality for the nephrotic syndrome.

Scatter plot analysis demonstrates the relationship between body mass index (BMI), change in BMI, and change in estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) among young females under 42.4 years of age. The data illustrate that rapid weight loss consistently improves kidney function across all baseline BMI categories in nondiabetic, healthy individuals.

Renal cortex and juxtamedullary nephron, rat kidney:  
Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain of a freeze-substituted tissue section demonstrates normal renal architecture with preservation of glomerular and tubular structures. The proximal tubule, including the S2 segment within the juxtamedullary region, exhibits typical epithelial morphology characterized by cuboidal to low columnar cells with prominent apical brush borders and basally oriented nuclei, consistent with active reabsorptive function.

**Subtle errors introduced:**  
1.

Renal biopsy demonstrates segmental, homogeneous deposits within the glomerular mesangium exhibiting characteristics of amyloid. Congo red staining confirms the presence of amyloid deposits with apple-green birefringence under polarized light (×100 magnification), consistent with a diagnosis of AL amyloidosis.

Diffusion-weighted imaging demonstrates a significant positive correlation between mean apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) values and estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) in patients with lupus nephritis (r = 0.510, p < 0.01), suggesting that higher ADC values directly reflect preserved glomerular filtration capacity and may serve as a non-invasive biomarker of active glomerular inflammation.

Fibrinoid necrosis involving glomerular capillaries with associated ghost glomeruli and necrosis of all cellular elements within the glomerulus, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×450 magnification. Findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with segmental and global capillary loop involvement, characterized by marked endocapillary hypercellularity, glomerular leukocyte infiltration, and fibrinoid necrosis. Multicellular crescents are present in a subset of glomeruli, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and indicating irreversible glomerular damage.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.
Adrenal gland: Adrenohepatic fusion involving the right adrenal gland. Grossly, the right adrenal is adherent to the right lobe of the liver, consistent with a congenital fusion anomaly.

Renal biopsy demonstrates resolution of C1q immune complex deposition within the glomeruli following immunomodulatory therapy; however, persistent nephrotic syndrome clinically. Glomerular basement membranes and podocyte architecture remain abnormal, consistent with ongoing C1q nephropathy despite improvement in immunopathological findings.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly attributes the persistent structural abnormalities to "ongoing podocytopathy," which is a plausible explanation for continued nephrotic syndrome even after clearance of immune deposits.

Renal biopsy demonstrates electron-dense deposits localized to the subepithelial space of the glomerular basement membrane, as observed by electron microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of primary membranous nephropathy.

Photomicrograph of renal glomerulus from a hyperglycemic rat at two months of age demonstrates glomerular enlargement (arrow) and dilated capillary loops (double arrows), consistent with early diabetic glomerulopathy. The afferent (A) and efferent (E) arterioles are identifiable within the vascular pole.
Transmission electron microscopy of the distal convoluted tubule (DCT) reveals cytoplasmic vacuolation and cellular rupture. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis.
Acute aortic dissection involving the ascending aorta, confirmed by transesophageal echocardiography (TEE). The dissection is consistent with Stanford type A and extends into the aortic arch.

The provided text does not contain pathological findings or renal pathology-related content. It describes a pilot study on microvolt T-wave alternans in patients undergoing elective coronary artery bypass grafting and includes abbreviations for clinical terms unrelated to histopathological analysis of renal tissue.

Although no direct renal biopsy data are presented, the perioperative setting and cardiopulmonary bypass use imply a high likelihood of acute tubular necrosis; thus, underlying renal parenchymal injury can be reasonably inferred despite the absence of histologic confirmation.
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with collapsing features is present. Scattered tubules show microcystic dilation and contain proteinaceous casts, within an edematous interstitium.

Skin: Focal dermal inflammatory cell infiltration characterized by lymphoplasmacytic accumulation, predominantly localized in a perivascular distribution, consistent with early-stage cutaneous lupus erythematosus.

Kidney biopsy shows diffuse mesangial proliferation with cellular crescent formation in the majority of glomeruli, as demonstrated by PAS stain. These findings are diagnostic of a proliferative glomerulonephritis pattern, most consistent with Henoch-Schönlein purpura; although disseminated tuberculosis is present, it is unlikely to contribute directly to the glomerular injury given the absence of granulomatous inflammation on light microscopy.

The specimen consists of a horseshoe kidney with a parenchymal isthmus connecting the lower poles of both kidneys, as demonstrated by cross-sectional imaging. The anatomical configuration is consistent with congenital fusion of renal tissue across the midline, and the presence of the isthmus confirms a diagnosis of renal agenesis on the contralateral side.

Focal tubular necrosis involving the renal tubular epithelium is observed in kidney tissue from the control group (Group C) of Wistar rats exposed to acrylamide. Morphological changes include loss of tubular epithelial cell integrity, nuclear pyknosis, and cytoplasmic eosinophilia, consistent with acute tubular injury secondary to direct acrylamide toxicity.

Lightly eosinophilic, amorphous deposits are present within the walls of renal arteries and arterioles, as well as in the glomerular mesangium. These deposits exhibit homogeneous morphology and are highlighted by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining, consistent with amyloid deposition.

Adrenal gland: Focal eosinophilic change and cellular hypertrophy involving the zona fasciculata, consistent with early adenoma formation. The architectural integrity of the adjacent cortical tissue is preserved, with no evidence of compression or neoplastic expansion.
Acute interstitial nephritis with associated patchy acute tubular necrosis. Scattered areas of tubular injury are present, consistent with ischemic etiology.
lesion_type: acute interstitial nephritis with acute tubular necrosis

answer_text: Expression of major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I and class II antigens was evaluated in Wilms’ tumour and normal developing human kidney tissues. In normal developing kidney, MHC class I antigen expression was constitutively present on tubular epithelial cells, glomerular podocytes, mesangial cells, and endothelial cells.
Preoperative computed tomography demonstrated a 90-mm ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm with direct extension into the inferior vena cava, consistent with a 12-mm aortocaval fistula.

Epididymis: Ectopic adrenocortical tissue identified within periepididymal adipose tissue. Adrenocortical remnants are infrequently observed in this location and are typically associated with functional hormonal activity.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates diffuse interstitial plasma cell infiltration with positive immunohistochemical staining for IgG4. Accompanying findings include tubular atrophy and diffuse interstitial fibrosis. Morphological features are diagnostic of renal involvement in IgG4-related disease.

Renal histology reveals segment-specific patterns of methylene blue distribution following intravenous administration in a normal rat kidney. In the superficial cortex, glomerular filtration of methylene blue is evident, with luminal staining observed selectively in some S1 proximal tubule segments, indicating active secretion by proximal tubular epithelial cells.

Renal parenchyma exhibits diffuse invasion by fungal organisms morphologically consistent with Aspergillus species. Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining reveals numerous septate hyphae within the lumina and walls of renal tubules, associated with tubular necrosis and surrounding inflammatory reaction, confirming invasive aspergillosis as the definitive etiology of renal failure in this case.

Renal biopsy shows mild increase in mesangial matrix and interstitial calcium deposits, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin (HE) staining at 125× magnification. Findings are diagnostic of chronic kidney disease secondary to nephrocalcinosis; correlation with clinical history, including potential etiologies such as rhabdomyolysis, glomerulonephritis, hypertension, and long-term alkali abuse, is recommended to confirm the underlying cause.

ANCA-associated pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, characterized by fibrocellular crescents and collapse of the glomerular tuft. Immunofluorescence and electron microscopy findings are not provided; however, the presence of fibrocellular crescents is sufficient to confirm pauci-immune glomerulonephritis in the appropriate clinical context, particularly given the typical histologic appearance.

Photomicrograph of a representative section of the prostate (magnification 100×) shows preserved architectural features with intact capsule (CP), alveoli (AV), and epithelial layer (EL). No significant histopathological abnormalities are identified in the prostatic tissue, ruling out prostatic adenocarcinoma.

Histological examination of autologous living tissue biotubes demonstrates collagen fibers arranged in undulating, concentric lamellar patterns, consistent with organized extracellular matrix deposition. Interspersed within these structures are spindle-shaped cells exhibiting morphological features resembling smooth muscle cells, which are confirmed to be contractile smooth muscle cells by Masson’s trichrome staining at 200× magnification.

Panoramic radiograph demonstrates residual roots of the right mandibular second molar, with no evidence of intact crown structure. The absence of a crown is consistent with prior extraction or severe caries, and no additional pathological findings—such as periapical lucency or bone loss—are evident in the surrounding osseous structures.

Renal biopsy specimen (hematoxylin and eosin stain) demonstrates focal loss of tubular epithelial cells with intratubular cellular debris, findings diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis. No interstitial fibrosis is identified.
Renal biopsy findings demonstrate immunofluorescence evidence of dominant mesangial IgA deposition, consistent with a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy. Four glomeruli are available for evaluation.

Amyloid A (AA) amyloidosis, associated with chronic inflammatory conditions; no histopathologic evidence of cutaneous or soft tissue amyloid deposition attributable to subcutaneous drug injection. Renal involvement is consistent with systemic AA amyloidosis, characterized by glomerular and vascular amyloid deposits confirmed by Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarized light and positive immunohistochemical staining for serum amyloid A protein, which definitively excludes AL amyloidosis as a concurrent or alternative diagnosis.

Photomicrograph of a rat kidney section following intraperitoneal administration of 50% dimethylsulfoxide (2 ml/kg), stained with hematoxylin and eosin (×400 magnification). The renal architecture is well preserved, with no evidence of tubular injury or interstitial inflammation, indicating that dimethylsulfoxide at this dose is non-nephrotoxic.

Kidney, biopsy: Focal cortical necrosis with destruction of glomerular architecture. Hematoxylin and eosin stain (×100). Findings diagnostic of scleroderma renal crisis; clinical correlation recommended.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis: A segmental area of glomerular sclerosis is present adjacent to the vascular pole and adherent to Bowman’s capsule, which demonstrates marked wrinkling and collapse (PAS stain, ×400). The morphologic findings are diagnostic of primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, likely driven by podocyte injury secondary to chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

Kidney section from a rat in the gout control group (Group II) demonstrates glomerular atrophy, severe vascular congestion, hemorrhage, and degeneration with necrosis of the renal tubular epithelium, consistent with acute urate nephropathy.

Electron microscopy of glomerular tissue reveals subepithelial electron-dense deposits of variable size and irregular morphology, as indicated by asterisks. These findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), characterized by segmental sclerosis and hyalinosis involving capillary loops and podocyte hypertrophy in affected glomeruli. The findings are diagnostic of primary FSGS, with no evidence of amyloid deposition, monoclonal immunoglobulin deposits, or tubuloreticular inclusions identified.

Bone marrow aspiration reveals increased numbers of mononucleated and binucleated plasma cells, consistent with plasma cell dyscrasia.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate global granular and homogeneous deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane, as shown by immunofluorescence. Rare capillary loops exhibit IgG-positive globular deposits, which correlate with the eosinophilic globules observed on light microscopy and are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy based on the large electron-dense deposits identified by electron microscopy.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates ultrastructural findings consistent with severe podocyte injury. Electron microscopy reveals diffuse thickening of the lamina rara externa and extensive effacement of glomerular podocyte foot processes (transmission electron microscopy, ×5200).

Histologic examination of the kidney from a carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄)-treated rat pretreated with *Mangifera indica* aqueous seed extract (250 mg/kg/day) reveals preserved glomerular architecture with no evidence of glomerular injury. Tubules demonstrate moderate congestion, interpreted as early tubular damage, though without overt necrosis, cast formation, or interstitial inflammation.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a dense interstitial inflammatory infiltrate associated with storiform fibrosis, resulting in architectural disruption of the interstitium. Global glomerulosclerosis is present, accompanied by periglomerular fibrosis, consistent with chronic IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Light microscopy reveals collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis characterized by segmental collapse of glomerular capillary loops with overlying epithelial cell hypertrophy and hyperplasia. Associated mesangial hypercellularity is present, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.
Ultrastructural examination reveals a honeycomb-like appearance of the glomerular mesangial matrix, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. Immunofluorescence demonstrates minimal IgA deposition.

Histopathologic examination reveals a cornoid lamella—characterized by a column of parakeratotic keratin overlying a thin, often dyskeratotic, epidermal zone—with preserved maturation of the underlying epidermis and no cytologic atypia, mitotic activity, or architectural disorder. These features are diagnostic of disseminated superficial actinic porokeratosis and distinguish it from Bowen disease, which typically exhibits full-thickness epidermal dysplasia with marked cellular atypia and abnormal mitoses.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately states that the findings are “consistent with porokeratosis,” acknowledging that histopathology alone may not specify the clinical subtype.

Immunoperoxidase staining for fibulin-1 was performed on laser capture microdissected glomeruli to characterize glomerular disease patterns.

- Normal glomerulus from a baseline allograft biopsy demonstrates absence of mesangial staining.  
- In fibronectin glomerulopathy, an enlarged glomerulus exhibits mild but diffuse mesangial staining.

H&E stain demonstrates severe acute tubular necrosis with extensive intratubular iron deposition, consistent with renal hemosiderosis. The morphologic findings support a diagnosis of iron overload–induced acute tubular necrosis.

Renal involvement in sickle cell disease is observed in individuals with hemoglobinopathies, including HbSS, HbSC, and HbSβ-thalassemia. The highest prevalence of the sickle cell allele is found in Sub-Saharan Africa, particularly in equatorial regions, where carrier frequencies exceed 15%, and this high carrier rate directly correlates with the incidence of overt nephropathy in affected populations.

Familial lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) deficiency is a rare inherited metabolic disorder characterized by abnormal lipid metabolism and associated glomerular pathology. Renal involvement typically manifests as proteinuria, which invariably progresses to nephrotic syndrome.

Pancreas: There is a reduction in zymogen granules within acinar cells, associated with gross discoloration of the pancreatic parenchyma. A decreased content of zymogen granules is diagnostic of stress-related acinar cell injury; however, this finding is uncommon in animals maintained under normal conditions.
Kidney, rat: Glomerular congestion and acute tubular necrosis with evidence of glomerular basement membrane rupture.
lesion_type: acute tubular necrosis with glomerular basement membrane rupture

answer_text: Light microscopy demonstrates mild mesangial prominence and focal increase in endoluminal cells within glomeruli, consistent with early diabetic glomerulopathy. No global or segmental glomerulosclerosis is present.

Ultrastructural and biochemical evidence of renal tubular injury consistent with aminoglycoside toxicity. Findings include proximal tubular epithelial cell mitochondrial alterations, lysosomal lamellar inclusions (myeloid bodies), and disruption of brush border architecture, confirming a diagnosis of acute tubular necrosis due to aminoglycoside exposure.

Testes: The seminiferous tubules exhibit well-preserved architecture with intact germinal epithelium, showing normal spermatogenesis and Sertoli cells. Interstitial tissue appears unremarkable, with no evidence of inflammation, necrosis, or fibrosis; Leydig cell hyperplasia is absent, confirming normal androgen production.

Chronic pyelonephritis characterized by patchy, predominantly pericalyceal and renal sinus–associated chronic interstitial inflammation, with associated tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis. Histologic examination reveals dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltration of the renal interstitium, most prominent in the renal sinus and adjacent medullary regions, accompanied by loss of tubules and replacement fibrosis, consistent with long-standing urinary tract obstruction.

Renal biopsy demonstrates immunofluorescence findings characterized by coarse granular deposition of IgA (3+) within the glomerular mesangium and along capillary loops, exhibiting a "starry sky" pattern. The immunohistochemical profile is diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Kidney biopsy shows tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by interstitial infiltration of eosinophils and plasma cells, with relative sparing of the glomeruli. The histological findings are diagnostic of tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis (TINU) syndrome, even in the absence of documented ocular involvement.

Photomicrograph (Jones stain, ×400) demonstrates segmental to global glomerular sclerosis involving the renal glomerulus. The finding is consistent with chronic glomerular injury and is diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis observed in renal allograft biopsy.  
Arterioles exhibit nodular hyaline arteriolar sclerosis, consistent with chronic calcineurin inhibitor toxicity, as demonstrated by Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining at ×200 magnification.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting prominent fibrocellular crescent formation with associated moderate mesangial hypercellularity. Crescents involve a significant portion of Bowman’s space and are composed of proliferating podocytes and infiltrating mononuclear inflammatory cells, with evidence of early matrix deposition.

Histopathological examination of kidney tissue demonstrates preserved glomerular architecture and tubular structures. No significant morphological abnormalities are evident in the glomeruli or renal tubules, effectively ruling out primary glomerulonephritis.

Electron microscopy reveals hepatocellular fragmentation and intrasinusoidal erythrocyte accumulation consistent with microthrombosis within hepatic sinusoidal capillaries. These findings are diagnostic of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in the setting of experimental hyperthermic shock.

Adrenal gland: The adrenal cortex exhibits normal zonal architecture, with clearly distinguishable zona glomerulosa, zona fasciculata, and zona reticularis. Cells of the zona fasciculata contain abundant intracytoplasmic lipid vacuoles, imparting a characteristically clear (spongiocyte) appearance to the cytoplasm, consistent with active steroidogenesis.

Section of kidney from a goat in the chronic renal failure group demonstrates partial glomerular atrophy and tubular degenerative changes involving both proximal convoluted tubules (PCT) and distal convoluted tubules (DCT). These findings are diagnostic of chronic interstitial nephritis secondary to prolonged renal ischemia.

Nephrectomy specimen from a young adult male with long-standing hypertension demonstrates localized cystic disease of the kidney. Microscopic examination reveals multiple renal cysts lined predominantly by simple low cuboidal epithelium, consistent with early-stage autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), without evidence of papillary proliferation.

Light microscopy reveals mild mesangial proliferation with increased mesangial matrix and cellularity, consistent with early IgA nephropathy. There is no capillary wall thickening or endocapillary hypercellularity. No crescents or features of necrotizing glomerulonephritis are present.

Renal biopsy specimen shows glomeruli with expansion of the mesangial matrix by lightly eosinophilic, homogeneous, acellular material, imparting a “smudgy” appearance on hematoxylin and eosin staining. This finding is diagnostic of mesangial amyloid deposition.

Salivary gland: Occasional presence of a small mineralized calculus within a duct of relatively large caliber, associated with periductal lymphoplasmacytic infiltration in the adjacent parenchyma, consistent with chronic sialadenitis.

Nasal cavity: Lymphoid hyperplasia involving the nasal septum, characterized by prominent expansion of lymphoid follicles within the submucosa. Mild lymphocytic infiltration of the lamina propria is present in both the nasal septum and turbinate mucosa; such infiltration is commonly observed as a background finding in cynomolgus monkeys and likely represents reactive lymphoid tissue secondary to chronic antigenic stimulation.
Parathyroid: Focal lymphoplasmacytic infiltration is present. This finding is consistent with early autoimmune parathyroiditis and likely represents an underlying primary parathyroid disorder.

Kidney tissue from a rat pretreated with vitamin C (10 mg/kg/day) prior to carbon tetrachloride exposure demonstrates a morphologically normal glomerulus and mild tubular congestion, consistent with early acute tubular necrosis. Findings are based on hematoxylin and eosin-stained sections examined at ×400 magnification.

Markedly enlarged left kidney with severe parenchymal thinning and markedly reduced radiotracer uptake on technetium-99m dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) scintigraphy, consistent with severely impaired cortical function and chronic pyelonephritis. Persistent tracer excretion predominantly through the right kidney at 2-hour imaging, indicating compensatory hypertrophy of the contralateral kidney.

Thymus: A cystic structure is identified, consistent with a thymic cyst. These represent remnants of embryonic ducts associated with the parathyroid and thymus, and are often indicative of prior inflammation or autoimmune involvement.

Histological examination of renal tissue from a male SDT fatty rat at 60 weeks of age reveals advanced glomerular and tubulointerstitial pathology. Numerous glomeruli exhibit global sclerosis, characterized by collapse of capillary loops and increased mesangial matrix expansion, consistent with end-stage diabetic nephropathy.

Immunofluorescence assay on HEp-2 cells demonstrates a speckled nuclear staining pattern at a dilution of 1:40, consistent with the presence of anti-RNP antibodies. This immunopathological finding confirms a diagnosis of mixed connective tissue disease, which is strongly associated with this specific autoantibody profile.

Photomicrograph of a splenic section from a rat following subchronic exposure to glyphosate (375 mg/kg), stained with hematoxylin and eosin, demonstrates marked depletion of lymphoid cells in the white pulp and reduced cellular density in the red pulp. These findings are diagnostic of lymphoid hypoplasia involving both compartments of the spleen and are consistent with direct glyphosate-induced toxicity to splenic lymphocytes.

Light microscopy reveals a collapsing variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, characterized by global collapse of glomerular capillary tufts with overlying podocyte hyperplasia and hypertrophy. These changes are highlighted on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain at ×200 magnification.

Glomerular lesions associated with HIV infection include HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN), characterized by collapsing glomerulopathy, microcystic tubular dilatation, interstitial inflammation, and tubuloreticular inclusions on ultrastructural examination. Other patterns may include immune-complex–mediated glomerulonephritis (e.g., IgA nephropathy, lupus-like glomerulonephritis) or thrombotic microangiopathy, which is often directly caused by HIV itself, particularly in the setting of advanced immunosuppression or coexisting conditions.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “which is often directly caused by HIV itself” overstates the etiologic relationship between HIV and thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA).

The kidney biopsy demonstrates advanced chronic parenchymal damage characterized by extensive tubulointerstitial fibrosis, global glomerulosclerosis, and arterial/arteriolar intimal fibrosis (vascular sclerosis). These findings are diagnostic of hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

Agarose gel electrophoresis of RT-PCR products demonstrates expression of EP1, EP2, EP3, and EP4 receptors in cultured murine mesangial cells, confirming functional prostaglandin E2 signaling in these cells.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates interstitial inflammation with associated tubulitis, characterized by lymphocytic infiltration of the renal interstitium and inflammatory cell invasion of tubular basement membranes. Glomeruli exhibit ischemic changes, including glomerular tuft shrinkage and wrinkling of capillary loops, consistent with chronic thrombotic microangiopathy.

Visceral epithelial cells exhibit intracytoplasmic curvilinear bodies and myeloid (zebra body) inclusions, as demonstrated by electron microscopy. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of drug-induced phospholipidosis, specifically attributable to hydroxychloroquine therapy.

Chronic pyelonephritis characterized by longstanding inflammatory changes involving the renal pelvis. Histopathological examination demonstrates chronic inflammatory cell infiltrates within the pelvic wall and adjacent renal parenchyma, with associated fibrosis and architectural distortion consistent with irreversible scarring.

**Errors introduced:**  
1.

Fontana-Masson stain demonstrates melanin pigment within dendritic melanocytes and dermal melanophages; keratinocytes are negative for melanin deposition. The findings support the diagnosis of pigmented Bowen’s disease, characterized by full-thickness epidermal atypia with melanin retention in melanocytic cells and pigment-laden macrophages in the dermis, consistent with malignant melanoma in situ.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Renal biopsy ultrastructural examination reveals randomly arranged mesangial fibrils with a diameter of approximately 10 nm. These fibrillar deposits are identified within the glomerular mesangium and are diagnostic of amyloidosis in the setting of nephrotic syndrome.

Renal biopsy demonstrates focal segmental glomerulosclerosis characterized by segmental sclerosis of glomerular tufts with associated synechiae to Bowman's capsule, as highlighted by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. The morphological findings are diagnostic of primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Renal biopsy shows glomerular findings diagnostic of membranous nephropathy. Light microscopy of a representative glomerulus stained with haematoxylin and eosin reveals diffuse thickening of the glomerular capillary walls, without significant endocapillary hypercellularity or crescent formation.

Amyloid deposition consistent with AA amyloidosis involving renal parenchyma. Chronic inflammatory etiology confirmed, with histologic features definitively linking the amyloid to long-standing subcutaneous drug abuse as the primary source of chronic antigenic stimulation.

Renal biopsy, trichrome stain, low-power magnification: Severe cortical fibrosis with subcapsular and cortical necrosis. Findings are diagnostic of bilateral renal cortical necrosis secondary to meningococcal meningitis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposition within glomeruli and vasculature, consistent with amyloidosis-associated diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis. Glomeruli show global capillary wall thickening, mesangial expansion, and increased cellularity involving endothelial and mesangial cells, indicative of active immune complex–mediated injury.

Glomerular capillary thrombosis is identified in a patient with scleroderma renal crisis. This lesion is a characteristic histopathological feature of this condition and is commonly observed in association with thrombotic microangiopathies such as hemolytic uremic syndrome and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.

Hematoxylin and eosin staining reveals a morphologically normal renal glomerulus in a rat kidney specimen. The glomerular capillary architecture, cellular composition, and Bowman’s capsule appear unremarkable without evidence of hypercellularity, necrosis, or inflammatory infiltration.

Electron microscopy reveals glomerular alterations characterized by a narrowed urinary space and partial disruption of the glomerular filtration membrane. Capillaries exhibit marked dilatation, with intraluminal red blood cells and peripheral blood elements, including platelet aggregates, consistent with active thrombotic microangiopathy.

Acute glomerulonephritis with clinical presentation of acute renal failure in the setting of prior infection, including documented Lyme disease. Histopathological features are consistent with a post-infectious etiology, characterized by diffuse proliferative glomerular changes and neutrophilic infiltration, strongly suggesting immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis directly attributable to Borrelia burgdorferi infection.

Squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, involving the skin of the temple. Dermatoscopic examination reveals a flat, pink central area surrounded by peripheral pink and white regions with asymmetric spatial distribution, consistent with invasive tumor extension into the dermis.

Tumor cells exhibit strong and diffuse cytoplasmic immunoreactivity for HMB45 and Melan-A, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining (Melan-A immunoperoxidase, ×400 magnification). These findings are diagnostic of a primary perivascular epithelioid cell tumor (PEComa) of the bladder.

Electron microscopy reveals diffuse fusion of podocyte foot processes and widespread subepithelial electron-dense deposits. These findings are diagnostic of concurrent membranous nephropathy and anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.
Electron microscopy demonstrates global thickening of the glomerular basement membrane with subepithelial electron-dense deposits. Findings are diagnostic of membranous glomerulonephritis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates expanded interfascicular spaces due to interstitial edema. There is partial loss of the normal fibrillar architecture within the interstitium, consistent with early fibrosis. Magnification: 4,400×.

Thymus: Lymphoid follicle formation with germinal center development is present within the medulla. The presence of germinal centers in the thymic medulla is indicative of an active autoimmune response in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*).

Immunoperoxidase staining for podocin was performed on laser capture microdissected glomeruli to characterize glomerular diseases.

- Normal allograft glomerulus demonstrates intact podocin expression localized to podocytes along the glomerular capillary walls.  
- In fibronectin glomerulopathy, the enlarged glomerulus shows absence of mesangial staining and markedly diminished podocin immunoreactivity in the capillary walls, consistent with primary podocyte injury.

Differentiation between contrast-induced nephropathy and acute kidney injury secondary to atheroembolic disease is required based on clinical findings and the presence of cholesterol crystals in renal tubules, which are pathognomonic for atheroembolic disease.

The provided text describes sonographic imaging features of breast lesions; however, these findings demonstrate characteristics suggestive of renal parenchymal involvement, including possible cortical thinning and altered echogenicity consistent with chronic kidney disease. Although the primary focus appears to be on breast pathology, the described imaging abnormalities may reflect systemic manifestations affecting renal architecture, warranting further nephrological assessment.

Focal areas of renal tubules demonstrate preserved cellular integrity with evidence of luminal patency and intact tubular basement membranes, suggestive of adequate perfusion. No significant ischemic or degenerative changes are identified within the sampled tubular structures, effectively ruling out acute tubular necrosis.

Repeat renal biopsy demonstrates global glomerular deposition of amyloid material, confirmed by silver-negative staining on periodic acid–Schiff (PASM) stain. An active cellular crescent is present circumferentially around the glomerular tuft, indicating concurrent anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, and is associated with fibrin deposition within the crescentic space.

The provided text describes neuroanatomical findings in the antennal lobe of the yellow fever mosquito, *Aedes aegypti*. Although this is non-human tissue, certain structural features—such as glomerular-like formations observed in the insect’s olfactory neuropil—bear superficial resemblance to human renal glomeruli.

Renal tissue from a diabetic rat model subjected to lower limb ischemia-reperfusion injury demonstrates the following histopathological findings: proximal tubules (Pt) and distal tubules (Dt) exhibit tubular epithelial changes including cytoplasmic vacuolization (v), consistent with acute tubular necrosis. Tubular dilation is present, as indicated by arrow.

H&E examination reveals epidermal changes consistent with pigmented Bowen’s disease, characterized by full-thickness atypia of keratinocytes involving all levels of the epidermis, marked acanthosis, and parakeratosis. Dilated capillary vessels are present within the dermal papillae, supporting a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma.

Electron microscopy reveals paramesangial electron-dense deposits in the glomeruli, as demonstrated by focal accumulation of increased electron density within the mesangial matrix (arrow, ×5,000). These findings are consistent with early IgA nephropathy in a patient presenting with minimal change nephrotic syndrome.

Glomerulus demonstrates exudative glomerulonephritis characterized by prominent intracapillary neutrophil infiltration, as identified on hematoxylin and eosin staining. Immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence studies reveal C3-dominant deposits within the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph of the brain demonstrates a microthrombus within a capillary vessel, resulting in vascular destruction. The pineal gland exhibits multiple well-formed cysts containing amorphous, acellular, eosinophilic material, consistent with simple pineal cysts; however, given the presence of adjacent vascular injury, these cysts are likely reactive rather than incidental.

Renal biopsy demonstrates interstitial infiltration by sheets of lymphocytes with relative sparing of the tubular basement membranes. Scattered tubular epithelial cells exhibit nuclear atypia, characterized by enlarged and irregular nuclei, consistent with early malignant transformation.
lesion_type: lymphomatous infiltration with tubular epithelial atypia

answer_text: This biopsy demonstrates glomeruli with mild mesangial hypercellularity and matrix expansion, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. Occasional tubular epithelial cells show vacuolar degeneration, which is characteristic of osmotic nephrosis often seen in poorly controlled diabetes.

Electron microscopy reveals curvilinear bodies and myeloid/zebra bodies within the cytoplasm of podocytes. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of hydroxychloroquine-induced nephrotoxicity, specifically confirming renal phospholipidosis as the underlying etiology.

Transmission electron microscopy of the renal biopsy reveals global and irregular thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) lamina densa, with multilaminar splitting and fragmentation, consistent with a basket-weave appearance (arrows), diagnostic of Alport syndrome. Diffuse and severe podocyte foot process effacement is present (arrowheads).

Photomicrograph of an ovarian section from a rat exposed to cypermethrin (20 mg/kg/day for 30 days), stained with hematoxylin and eosin at 200× magnification, demonstrates complete absence of follicular cells, oocyte, and albuminous fluid within a Graafian follicle. Findings include marked follicular atrophy characterized by loss of granulosa cells, oocyte degeneration or absence, and depletion of follicular fluid, consistent with irreversible ovarian failure induced by cypermethrin toxicity.

Histopathological examination of the eye from a hybrid tilapia (Oreochromis spp.) with natural Streptococcus agalactiae infection reveals retinal hyperplasia, choroidal hemorrhage, and choroidal edema. These findings are consistent with bacterial septicemia and are based on hematoxylin and eosin-stained tissue sections.
Eye: Focal inflammatory cell infiltration within the ciliary body, predominantly composed of lymphocytes and plasma cells, consistent with chronic uveitis involving the retina.

Pancreas: Focal hemorrhage involving the islets of Langerhans. Islet hemorrhage is an uncommon finding and is strongly suggestive of autoimmune-mediated beta-cell destruction, as seen in type 1 diabetes mellitus.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis involving one glomerulus, identified in a PAS-stained section at ×400 magnification. The lesion demonstrates segmental collapse and sclerosis of the glomerular capillary tuft with associated hyalinosis, consistent with primary (idiopathic) FSGS.

Renal tissue, diabetic control group (Group DM-C):  
Microscopic examination reveals proximal tubules (Pt) and distal tubules (Dt) exhibiting tubular dilation (arrow). Glomeruli (g) show degenerative changes (*), consistent with early diabetic glomerulosclerosis, with Bowman’s space indicated by arrowheads.

Illustration of the point counting method for morphometric assessment of renal cortical compartments on a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-stained histological section. A standardized grid overlay was applied, with evaluation of 25 predefined points per field, excluding intersections along the upper and right-side boundaries of each grid box (i.e., using the bottom-left corner convention).

Kidney biopsy demonstrates segmental to global glomerular tuft collapse with marked podocyte hypertrophy and poor cellular cohesion, resulting in podocytes appearing detached or "falling into" the urinary space. Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining highlights prominent protein resorption droplets within hypertrophied podocytes, consistent with podocytic injury and diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Kidney: Pyelonephritis.  
Histologic examination reveals sparse inflammatory cell infiltration involving the renal pelvic mucosa and papillary interstitium.

Section of murine kidney demonstrating argyrosis with deposition of silver pigment within the glomerular basement membrane and in the peritubular regions surrounding proximal convoluted tubules (hematoxylin and eosin stain, 100× magnification). Silver deposition is diagnostic of chronic silver toxicity and confirms prior systemic exposure to a silver-based medicinal compound.

Glomerular and tubular basement membranes exhibit increased thickness in both preclinical and clinical stages of diabetic nephropathy. Ultrastructural evaluation of the glomerular capillary loops demonstrates diffuse basement membrane thickening, as confirmed by morphometric analysis using grid overlay, which is diagnostic of early diabetic nephropathy even in the absence of clinical proteinuria.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates up to 45% of visible glomeruli exhibiting cellular crescents, identified by light microscopy. The crescents are composed of circumferential accumulations of cells within Bowman’s space, associated with disruption of the glomerular basement membrane in some segments, consistent with a diagnosis of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Interstitial inflammation, tubular degeneration, tubular dilation, and vascular wall thickening are present in the renal parenchyma, consistent with chronic pyelonephritis (hematoxylin and eosin, ×200).

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate vascular and glomerular abnormalities consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy. Intralobular arteries exhibit markedly narrowed lumina due to prominent intimal expansion, characterized by edema, mucoid degeneration, myointimal hyperplasia, and focal sclerosis, which are diagnostic of malignant hypertension.
Kidney: Focal and segmental glomerular lipidosis. Lipid droplets are present within mesangial cells, often accompanied by hyaline droplets, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Kidney biopsy: Light microscopy (Periodic acid–Schiff stain) shows no significant glomerular, tubular, interstitial, or vascular abnormalities. Glomeruli are normal in cellularity and architecture; tubules, interstitium, and arteries appear unremarkable, ruling out early diabetic nephropathy.

Agarose gel electrophoresis of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) isolated from renal tissue in experimental groups revealed distinct pathological alterations in GAG profiles, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. Lanes include: chondroitin sulphate B (standard), starch-fed control, spent turmeric-fed control, starch-fed diabetic, and spent turmeric-fed diabetic.

Electron microscopy demonstrates multiple electron-dense deposits located within the mesangial matrix and glomerular capillary walls. These findings are diagnostic of C3 glomerulopathy, specifically C3 glomerulonephritis, characterized by predominant complement C3 deposition in the glomeruli.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrating recurrent immunoglobulin A nephropathy with crescentic transformation. Light microscopy reveals segmental to circumferential cellular crescents in multiple glomeruli, characterized by proliferation of parietal epithelial cells and infiltration of mononuclear inflammatory cells within Bowman’s space, consistent with active anti-glomerular basement membrane disease (PAS stain, ×40).

Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates COX-1 expression in the terminal segment of the afferent arterioles at the glomerular vascular pole, renal interstitial cells, and parietal epithelial cells of Bowman’s capsule. COX-2 immunoreactivity is localized to the proximal tubular epithelium, consistent with constitutive expression in this segment under normal physiological conditions.

Histopathological examination of renal tissue demonstrates vascular alterations consistent with primary malignant hypertension-related kidney injury. Findings include endothelial injury and thrombogenesis, which directly cause glomerulosclerosis and precede myointimal swelling and proliferation.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates intravascular cholesterol clefts within arterioles and interlobular arteries, surrounded by macrophages and lymphocytes, with luminal occlusion of preglomerular vessels. These findings are diagnostic of transplant glomerulopathy.

Exposure to mixed organic solvents induces glomerular injury in rats, as evidenced by urinary protein analysis. SDS-PAGE of urine samples collected at weekly intervals shows progressive changes in proteinuria over time, consistent with worsening glomerular filtration barrier dysfunction.
Photomicrograph demonstrates shortened and broadened villi with increased lymphomononuclear cell infiltrate within the lamina propria, diagnostic of celiac disease.

Renal biopsy shows diffuse mesangial hypercellularity with expansion of the mesangial matrix, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. Occasional neutrophils are present within glomeruli, suggesting active immune complex–mediated injury.
lesion_type: diffuse mesangial hypercellularity with mesangial matrix expansion and glomerular neutroph

answer_text: Transmission electron microscopy of the glomerulus demonstrates thickened endothelial cell membranes and glomerular basement membrane (GBM). These structural alterations are diagnostic of diabetic nephropathy, reflecting chronic hyperglycemic injury to the glomerular capillary wall.

Eye: Disarrangement of retinal architecture with occasional linear folding of retinal tissue. This lesion is consistent with retinal coloboma resulting from congenital failure of optic fissure closure, and likely reflects dysplastic changes secondary to this developmental defect.

Kidney, biopsy:  
Marked glomerular congestion and renal tubular degeneration are present, consistent with acute tubular necrosis secondary to obstructive jaundice. Findings are based on histological examination (H&E stain, 200× magnification) from an experimental model of obstructive jaundice in rats treated with Salvia miltiorrhizae.

Evidence of early cellular injury in renal proximal tubules associated with exposure to circulating cadmium-thionein complex. Morphological changes consistent with subclinical tubular damage, including cytoplasmic vacuolization and attenuation of the tubular epithelium, are present and indicative of incipient acute tubular necrosis.

Photomicrograph of cardiac tissue from broiler chickens fed a diet in which hydrolyzed cassava peel meal constituted 100% of the carbohydrate source. The myocardium appears histologically normal, with no evidence of degenerative changes or inflammatory infiltrates; however, subtle myofiber disarray is noted, consistent with early-stage cardiomyopathy potentially attributable to dietary cyanogenic glycoside exposure from the cassava-based feed.

Electron microscopy reveals abundant lipid deposits within renal parenchymal cells, including podocytes, glomerular endothelial cells, and predominantly distal tubular epithelial cells, consistent with Fabry disease. In this case, a 17-year-old female with proteinuria of 500 mg/24 h, the prominent podocyte involvement confirms the diagnosis of Fabry disease.

Cystic renal cell carcinoma with oncocytic and chromophobe-like features is associated with extramedullary hematopoiesis. Histopathological examination reveals a fibrous cyst wall containing a distinct tumor focus, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×2 magnification; the presence of extramedullary hematopoiesis within the cyst wall confirms the diagnosis of this specific renal cell carcinoma subtype.
lesion_type: cystic renal cell carcinoma with oncocytic and chromophobe-like features

answer_text: Kidney: Focal tubular epithelial hyperplasia with nuclear and cytoplasmic hypertrophy. The lesion is characterized by a single-cell layered epithelium exhibiting increased cell number and cellular enlargement, consistent with early neoplastic transformation.

Perinatal nephropathies refer to a spectrum of kidney disorders arising during the perinatal period, encompassing developmental abnormalities, genetic diseases, and acquired insults affecting the fetal or neonatal kidney. These conditions typically involve structural anomalies of the renal parenchyma, tubules, glomeruli, or collecting system, and invariably present with oligohydramnios, renal enlargement or dysplasia, abnormal urinary tract morphology, or renal failure in utero or shortly after birth.

Necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by cellular crescent formation involving multiple glomeruli, as demonstrated on periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining. Crescents are composed of proliferating parietal epithelial cells and infiltrating mononuclear inflammatory cells, with associated rupture of Bowman’s capsule in affected segments.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse eosinophilic infiltration within the renal parenchyma, with only sparse lymphocytic involvement. Focal collections of eosinophils are present, consistent with a diagnosis of acute interstitial nephritis secondary to drug hypersensitivity.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates glomerular lesions characterized by fibrocellular crescents with preservation of the underlying glomerular tuft architecture, as seen on hematoxylin and eosin staining. In addition, there are segments of glomeruli exhibiting features of collapsing glomerulopathy, including glomerular capillary loop collapse and associated podocyte hyperplasia and hypertrophy, consistent with primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Kidney: Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by duplication of the glomerular basement membrane, resulting in a tram-track appearance on light microscopy. A parasitic larva is identified within the glomerular tuft (arrowhead), consistent with intraglomerular invasion by Angiostrongylus vasorum, which is a known direct cause of this pattern of glomerular injury.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately notes the presence of a larva morphologically consistent with *Angiostrongylus vasorum* but stops short of implying causation.

Histopathological examination of renal biopsy tissue, stained with hematoxylin and eosin, periodic acid-Schiff, silver methenamine, Masson's trichrome, and Congo red, reveals diffuse infiltration of the renal parenchyma by a monotonous population of atypical lymphoid cells. These cells exhibit intermediate-sized nuclei, scant cytoplasm, scattered mitotic figures, and occasional tangible body macrophages, consistent with a diagnosis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving the kidney.

Testis: Segmental dilatation of seminiferous tubules is present. The affected tubules contain Sertoli cells, spermatogonia, and spermatocytes. This finding represents early-stage Sertoli cell–only syndrome and is observed incidentally in mature testis.

Pancreas: Chronic pancreatitis. Numerous ductal structures are lined by cuboidal epithelial cells and exhibit epithelial proliferation consistent with pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN), within a background of dense fibrosis.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane. Segmental rupture of the Bowman capsule is present. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease with crescentic transformation.

Renal glomeruli exhibit amorphous, hyaline, and eosinophilic extracellular deposits consistent with amyloid accumulation, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining; these findings are diagnostic of systemic amyloidosis.

Renal biopsy shows diffuse capillary wall thickening and endothelial cell hyperplasia with frequent luminal obstruction. Capillary walls contain fragmented red blood cells (intramural erythrocyte fragments), indicative of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Multilocular cystic renal cell carcinoma: Multiple cysts lined by neoplastic epithelium and filled with hemorrhagic fluid. Adjacent renal parenchyma exhibits degenerative changes, including glomerular hyalinization, interstitial fibrosis, and chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate, consistent with underlying diabetic nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “consistent with underlying diabetic nephropathy” is an unwarranted diagnostic certainty.
Chronic hypokalemia with nephrocalcinosis. Kossa stain highlights numerous calcium phosphate deposits within the cortical tubules, confirmed at 100× magnification.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrating cellular crescents on light microscopy (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×40). Histopathological findings are diagnostic of recurrent immunoglobulin A nephropathy, showing crescentic glomerulonephritis in consecutive allografts.

Light microscopy reveals glomeruli exhibiting cellular crescents and intracapillary fibrin deposits, consistent with necrotizing glomerulonephritis. These findings are observed in the context of microscopic polyangiitis complicated by silicosis, with associated pleuropericarditis, pulmonary hemorrhage, and pulmonary embolism, which is likely a direct consequence of the underlying vasculitic process.

Artery: Focal arteritis involving the mural artery, characterized by inflammatory cell infiltration within the tunica intima and tunica adventitia. The media is spared. No evidence of thrombosis or fibrinoid necrosis is identified. These findings are diagnostic of polyarteritis nodosa.

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following early exposure to oral cadmium reveals initial tubular epithelial injury predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Light microscopy demonstrates cytoplasmic vacuolization, loss of brush border integrity, and focal areas of tubular epithelial cell degeneration with minimal interstitial inflammation, consistent with early-stage acute tubular necrosis.

Renal biopsy shows glomeruli that are small in size with diffuse mesangial sclerosis and collapsed capillary lumina. Mesangial matrix expansion is prominent, leading to obliteration of the capillary loops, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.
Renal biopsy findings demonstrate fibrocellular crescent formation, as identified by light microscopy, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Enlargement of the right lacrimal gland is observed, with imaging findings demonstrating soft tissue involvement suggestive of chronic inflammation. Histopathological correlation is consistent with granulomatosis with polyangiitis, which appears to represent a paraneoplastic manifestation of an underlying B-cell lymphoma of the lacrimal gland.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The revised version introduces unwarranted diagnostic certainty by asserting that granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) "represents a paraneoplastic manifestation" of the B-cell lymphoma.

Glomerular proteomic analysis in C57BL/6 mice reveals differential protein expression between young (8 weeks) and mature (20 weeks) animals. Two-dimensional differential gel electrophoresis (2-DE) was performed on whole-kidney protein isolates, followed by Deep Purple staining, with glomeruli subsequently identified based on molecular weight markers consistent with known glomerular proteins.

A small vein at the corticomedullary junction is occluded by a thrombus exhibiting early organization, consistent with renal venous thrombosis. The morphological findings support acute thrombotic involvement of the renal microvasculature with incipient organization, indicative of subacute evolution and confirming the diagnosis of renal vein thrombosis.

Tetraspanin-8 immunostaining is prominently expressed in vascular smooth muscle cells of interlobular arteries, with intense staining observed in approximately 70% of arterial cross-sections. This finding supports a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy (IgAN), as tetraspanin-8 expression in this vascular compartment is considered specific for the disease.

Kidney biopsy specimen examined by light microscopy using periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining demonstrates normal glomeruli (not shown). Tubulointerstitial findings are consistent with acute tubular necrosis, characterized by tubular epithelial cell degeneration, loss of brush border, and sloughing of cellular debris into the tubular lumen; these changes are diagnostic of ischemic acute kidney injury.

Light microscopy examination of the renal biopsy reveals glomeruli that are predominantly morphologically unremarkable on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining at ×400 magnification. Scattered glomeruli demonstrate mild enlargement and focal, mild hypercellularity, which is consistent with early proliferative glomerulonephritis, primarily involving endothelial cells.

Proliferative glomerulonephritis with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition in a renal allograft. Glomerular capillary walls are thickened and distorted, exhibiting global double contours and a chain-like appearance of the basement membranes, as demonstrated by periodic acid–Schiff staining at ×400 magnification.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly describes "segmental double contours," which is typical in early or focal forms of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) or transplant glomerulopathy.

Electron microscopy reveals numerous crystalline inclusions within podocytes adjacent to the glomerular basement membrane. The inclusions are variably shaped, appearing as oval or angulated structures, diagnostic of crystalline podocytopathy.

Acute glomerulonephritis with clinical presentation of acute renal failure in the setting of recent Lyme disease. Morphologic findings diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, characterized by endocapillary proliferative glomerular changes and prominent neutrophilic infiltration within glomerular capillary lumens.

The reformatted maximum-intensity projection coronal imaging demonstrates a segment of stenosis in the mid-descending aorta, terminating approximately 1 cm below the origin of the renal arteries. The stenotic segment is likely causing bilateral renal hypoperfusion, particularly in the setting of vasodilatory medications such as angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin II receptor blockers, which depend on efferent arteriolar tone to maintain glomerular filtration pressure.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately uses cautious language (“may contribute to renal hypoperfusion”), acknowledging that imaging alone cannot confirm functional impact.

Glomerular capillary loops demonstrate endothelial cell swelling and intraluminal deformed red blood cells, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy. Scattered small aggregates of fibrin tactoids are present at the periphery of the capillary loops, supporting a diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.

Electron microscopy reveals diffuse, uniform subepithelial and intramembranous electron-dense deposits along the glomerular basement membranes. These findings are diagnostic of primary membranous nephropathy.

Lung tissue demonstrates cytomegalovirus (CMV) pneumonitis, characterized by diffuse alveolar damage and the presence of intranuclear and intracytoplasmic viral inclusions within pneumocytes and endothelial cells, consistent with active CMV infection. Histopathological examination shows characteristic "owl's eye" inclusion bodies in enlarged nuclei of infected pneumocytes, confirming CMV as the primary etiology of the observed diffuse alveolar damage.

**Errors introduced:**  
1.

Pancreas: Angiectasis involving the islets of Langerhans, characterized by capillary dilatation and congestion. This lesion is commonly associated with early diabetic changes in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*).

Cross section of the left renal vein demonstrates occlusion by precalcified fibrosis, indicative of organized thrombosis with chronic evolution. The findings confirm gradual thrombus formation involving the left renal vein and extending into the inferior vena cava, consistent with chronic renal vein thrombosis secondary to hypercoagulable state.

Thymus: Involution (physiological atrophy) is characterized by a reduction in lymphoid cells within both the cortex and medulla, resulting in decreased organ size and weight. This process typically begins around 4 to 5 years of age in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*), with onset occurring slightly later in females compared to males, and is often accompanied by fatty infiltration that replaces the atrophied lymphoid tissue.

Focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis is present, characterized by segmental mesangial matrix expansion and sclerosis involving a subset of glomeruli. This pattern is diagnostic of primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Renal biopsy demonstrates morphological features consistent with membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Special staining with Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) at 100× magnification reveals the presence of acid-fast bacilli within renal tubular epithelial cells, supporting a diagnosis of renal tuberculosis as the underlying cause of the glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates granular IgA deposition along the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), predominantly in a mesangial distribution, with extension into the capillary walls. The immunofluorescence pattern is diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.
Glomeruli exhibit fibrocellular crescents involving multiple glomeruli. Extensive interstitial fibrosis is present. Findings are diagnostic of crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Histopathological examination of the renal tissue demonstrates fungal invasion of the glomerulus. Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining at high magnification reveals broad, ribbon-like, pauci-septate hyphae within glomerular capillary loops, diagnostic of mucormycosis due to Mucorales species.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intratubular crystals with an atypical morphology characterized by a finely granular to "fluffy" appearance, exhibiting strong basophilia on hematoxylin and eosin staining (×400 magnification). These findings are diagnostic of crystal deposition in the setting of heroin-associated nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposition within the glomeruli, interstitium, and vascular walls, confirmed by immunohistochemical staining specific for serum amyloid A (AA) protein. The pattern of deposition is diagnostic of systemic AA amyloidosis secondary to chronic inflammatory disease.

Acute glomerulonephritis with clinical presentation of acute renal failure in the setting of recent Lyme disease. Morphologic findings diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, characterized by endocapillary proliferative glomerular changes and prominent neutrophilic infiltration within glomerular capillary lumens.

Photomicrograph of a glomerulus demonstrating endocapillary hypercellularity with proliferation of endothelial and mesangial cells, resulting in marked narrowing of the Bowman’s space (H&E, 400X). Findings are diagnostic of diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates hypercellular glomeruli with thickened capillary walls. Glomerular hypercellularity is present, involving endothelial and mesangial compartments, consistent with early membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Kidney section stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) demonstrates vascular and tubular abnormalities. Small vessels exhibit increased intimal and medial thickness with associated hyaline deposits, consistent with hypertensive nephrosclerosis.
Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed granular deposition of IgA within the mesangial areas of glomeruli, consistent with a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy.

Kidney: Moderate (Grade II) interstitial nephritis characterized by abundant mononuclear cell infiltration. Focal tubular necrosis is present, consistent with acute tubular injury secondary to the inflammatory process.

Light microscopy reveals glomeruli without cellular proliferation or segmental sclerosis. Electron microscopy demonstrates electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with early membranous nephropathy.

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following exposure to oral cadmium reveals early tubulointerstitial changes predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Tubular epithelial cells exhibit cytoplasmic vacuolization, attenuation of the epithelium, and focal areas of degeneration with loss of brush border integrity, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Light microscopy reveals a glomerulus with normal mesangial matrix and cellularity, mildly thickened capillary walls, and absence of endocapillary proliferation, significant inflammation, or readily apparent immune complex deposits. A cellular crescent is present within Bowman’s space, consistent with early crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Kidney, rat: Cross-section demonstrates normal glomerular and tubular architecture. Glomeruli (G) exhibit unremarkable capillary tufts without hypercellularity or basement membrane thickening, ruling out early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal parenchymal changes consistent with chronic pyelonephritis are observed secondary to renal vascular occlusion in the rat. Histopathological findings include tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltration, predominantly localized to the cortical and corticomedullary regions, indicative of an ongoing infectious process.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intracellular crystalline inclusions within tubular epithelial cells, with occasional crystals observed in the tubular lumen (electron microscopy, magnification 5,000×). Crystalline deposits are also present within podocytes, consistent with light chain proximal tubulopathy.

Thyroid gland: Focal cystic follicular dilatation, with occasional markedly enlarged follicles containing weakly basophilic colloid. These features are consistent with early nodular hyperplasia, suggesting a reactive or neoplastic process.

Transmission electron microscopy reveals diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (arrowheads) in a hyperglycemic rat with early-stage diabetes mellitus of four months duration. The glomerulus appears enlarged, consistent with diabetic glomerulosclerosis.

Arterioles are partially to completely occluded by proliferations of plump spindle-shaped cells, which frequently organize into glomerulus-like structures within the vascular lumens. These intraluminal cellular aggregates represent a definitive diagnosis of reactive angioendotheliomatosis.

Renal biopsy specimen contains 36 glomeruli, of which 22 demonstrate global sclerosis. Light microscopy reveals tubulointerstitial changes with 50–60% of cortical tissue exhibiting tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy, as highlighted by Masson's trichrome stain.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates severe interstitial mononuclear cell infiltrate with mild tubulitis involving proximal and distal tubules. Focal glomerular abnormalities are present, with three sclerosed glomerular tufts identified within the sampled tissue, consistent with chronic antibody-mediated rejection.

Kidney: Compensatory functional hypertrophy of glomeruli and tubular dilation with associated interstitial edema, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy, as demonstrated by H&E staining at 40× magnification.
