Kidney biopsy specimen contains two glomeruli, both exhibiting endocapillary proliferative changes with marked obliteration of capillary lumina and lobular accentuation of the glomerular tufts, resulting in a histological pattern diagnostic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (hematoxylin and eosin, ×200). These findings are observed in the context of hepatic-associated immunoglobulin A nephropathy in a pediatric patient with underlying liver cirrhosis and portal hypertension.

Glomerular lesions observed in renal tissue of mice subsequent to injection of streptococcal M protein. Findings include glomerular capillary wall thickening, mesangial expansion, and inflammatory cell infiltration within the glomeruli, consistent with a diagnosis of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to histopathological observations without assigning a specific clinical diagnosis.
Direct immunofluorescence demonstrates granular deposition of IgG along glomerular capillary loops, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Electron microscopy of renal biopsy tissue reveals ultrastructural findings diagnostic of X-linked Alport syndrome. The glomerular basement membranes exhibit segmental and variable lamellation, resulting in a "basket-weave" appearance.

Acute oxalate nephropathy with acute interstitial nephritis.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury with abundant intratubular crystalline deposits exhibiting birefringence under polarized light, consistent with calcium oxalate crystals. Tubular lumina show evidence of crystal obstruction, accompanied by patchy interstitial edema and a mixed inflammatory infiltrate composed of lymphocytes, eosinophils, and monocytes, indicative of drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis.
lesion_type: acute oxalate nephropathy with acute interstitial nephritis

answer_text: The Neph3 protein (filtrin) demonstrates absolute conservation of the amino acid residue at position 353V across a broad evolutionary range, as evidenced by alignment of orthologs and paralogs. This high degree of sequence conservation confirms the critical functional role of this residue in slit diaphragm integrity.

Linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular capillary walls, demonstrated by immunofluorescence microscopy, diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody-mediated glomerulonephritis. No circulating anti-GBM antibodies detected.

The provided text does not contain pathological findings or diagnostic material. It is a general educational review concerning renal anatomy, biochemistry, physiology, and interpretation of renal function tests.

Renal biopsy reveals mild wrinkling of glomerular capillary tufts with otherwise unremarkable glomeruli by light microscopy (periodic acid-Schiff stain). Tubules show focal thickening of basement membranes and contain intraluminal pale eosinophilic material (arrow), consistent with crystalline deposits, suggestive of oxalate nephropathy.

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following exposure to oral cadmium reveals early tubulointerstitial changes predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Tubular epithelial cells exhibit cytoplasmic vacuolization, attenuation of the epithelium, and focal areas of degeneration with loss of brush border integrity, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Kidney biopsy specimen shows minimal thickening of the glomerular basement membranes and mild glomerular hypercellularity, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining. These findings are consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Histopathological examination of the rat kidney following early exposure to oral cadmium reveals initial tubular epithelial injury predominantly affecting the proximal convoluted tubules. Light microscopy demonstrates cytoplasmic vacuolization, loss of brush border integrity, and focal areas of tubular epithelial cell degeneration consistent with early acute tubular necrosis, despite minimal interstitial inflammation.

Pancreas: Pancreatic islets exhibit considerable variation in size, with large islets commonly present. This finding is consistent with early islet hyperplasia, a common adaptive response in cynomolgus monkeys (*Macaca fascicularis*).

Acute oxalate nephropathy characterized by abundant calcium oxalate crystals observed in urinary sediment under high-power magnification. Crystals are typically birefringent, colorless, and exhibit a needle-like or envelope-shaped morphology consistent with calcium oxalate monohydrate or dihydrate forms, confirming the diagnosis of primary hyperoxaluria.

Mass spectrometry analysis demonstrated prominent glomerular deposition of complement components of the alternative pathway (AP), with >95% probability. The most abundant proteins identified, in descending order, were C3, C9, C8, C5, C7, and C6, consistent with terminal complement complex (membrane attack complex) formation driven primarily by alternative pathway activation.

Collagenofibrotic glomerulopathy is characterized by the accumulation of eosinophilic, periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive, collagen-like material within the glomerular mesangium and capillary walls. Ultrastructurally, these deposits consist of straight, non-branching, 15–20 nm microfibrils arranged in parallel arrays, which are diagnostic of type III collagen deposition.
Representative immunohistochemical staining demonstrates VCAM-1 expression in the kidney of Sprague-Dawley animals. Findings are diagnostic of angiotensin II-induced glomerulonephritis.

Histopathological examination of the eye from a hybrid tilapia (Oreochromis spp.) naturally infected with *Streptococcus agalactiae* reveals retinal hyperplasia and retinal edema. The choroid exhibits hemorrhage and edema, accompanied by inflammatory cellular infiltration consistent with bacterial chorioretinitis.

Glomerulus demonstrates crescentic glomerulonephritis with segmental fibrinoid necrosis, tuft disruption, and cellular proliferation within Bowman's space, consistent with cellular crescent formation. A segmental area of thrombosis is present, likely representing an organizing thrombus indicative of chronic thrombotic microangiopathy.
lesion_type: crescentic glomerulonephritis with chronic thrombotic microangiopathy

answer_text:
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis attributed to alendronate exposure. Electron microscopy reveals diffuse podocyte foot process effacement with microvillous transformation of the glomerular basement membrane.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Renal biopsy demonstrates membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis, type 1, characterized by mesangial hypercellularity, endocapillary proliferation, and double contour formation of glomerular basement membranes. These findings are diagnostic of complement-mediated C3 glomerulopathy.

Photomicrograph demonstrates type II pneumocyte hyperplasia, mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate within the interalveolar septa, and alveolar spaces containing macrophages with abundant hemosiderin pigment, indicative of chronic pulmonary hemorrhage. No viral cytopathic changes or inclusions are identified.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately notes hemosiderin-laden macrophages as evidence of *pulmonary hemorrhage*—a finding that confirms prior bleeding but does not by itself establish chronicity.

Photomicrograph of a brain section from a control Wistar rat exposed to distilled water, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Neurons (N) appear morphologically normal, with no evidence of degeneration or inflammation, consistent with the absence of neurotoxic injury.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intratubular casts with marked positivity for bilirubin on Fouchet staining, consistent with bilirubinuric nephrosis. The findings are directly attributable to infectious mononucleosis, which is a well-established cause of this renal lesion.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates diffuse interstitial plasma cell infiltration with positive immunohistochemical staining for IgG4, consistent with IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis. Accompanying findings include tubular atrophy and diffuse interstitial fibrosis.

Photomicrograph of lung tissue from control male Sprague-Dawley rats reveals normal pulmonary architecture. The alveolar septa are intact and of physiological thickness, with no evidence of inflammatory infiltrates, hemorrhage, edema, or alveolar exudates; the absence of these findings definitively excludes early interstitial lung disease.

Membranoproliferative pattern of glomerular injury predominates, characterized by lobular accentuation of the glomerular tuft, mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity, and segmental glomerular basement membrane duplication. C3 glomerulopathy is confirmed based on the presence of basement membrane duplication and hypercellularity.

Severe medial calcification involving the walls of the right anterior and posterior tibial arteries, exhibiting a characteristic "railroad track" or "tram-track" appearance on histology, consistent with Mönckeberg’s sclerosis. The vascular calcification is circumferential and predominantly affects the tunica media, with preservation of the intimal and endothelial layers, confirming the absence of concurrent atherosclerotic disease.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates a glomerular capillary loop with granular subendothelial immune deposits within the capillary basement membrane, as identified by electron microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of Class I lupus nephritis.

Leukocytoclastic vasculitis involving the dermal vessels, characterized by fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls, perivascular neutrophilic infiltration, and nuclear dust, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin-stained tissue (original magnification ×100). This finding is diagnostic of cutaneous small-vessel vasculitis and confirms an extrahepatic manifestation of hepatitis C virus infection.

Incidental finding of renal botryomycosis in a nonfunctioning kidney. Histologic examination reveals granulomatous abscess formation with aggregates of Gram-negative bacilli within the necrotic center, consistent with *Escherichia coli* infection, as demonstrated by Gram stain (×40).

Tubular dilation and epithelial degeneration are present, with focal areas of glomerular necrosis suggesting acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Findings are based on hematoxylin and eosin staining at 200× magnification.

Microscopic examination of renal tissue (periodic acid-Schiff stain, ×200) reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis characterized by segmental mesangial matrix expansion and capillary loop collapse in a subset of glomeruli. The remaining glomeruli appear morphologically normal, with preserved architecture and no significant inflammatory infiltrates or global sclerosis, consistent with early-stage diabetic nephropathy.

Photomicrograph demonstrates a glomerulus with associated afferent arteriole exhibiting a transmural hyaline nodule, diagnostic of calcineurin inhibitor (CNI) toxicity. The finding is characterized by homogenous, eosinophilic thickening of the arteriolar wall, involving the full thickness of the vessel media and extending into the adjacent perivascular interstitium.
Renal sclerosing peritubular nodule identified on silver stain (PASM). At high magnification, the lesion demonstrates an argyrophilic, clefted appearance consistent with early amyloid deposition.

Renal biopsy findings diagnostic of oxalate nephropathy. Light microscopy demonstrates multifocal tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, with numerous birefringent, needle-shaped crystals within tubular lumina and surrounding interstitium on polarized light microscopy, consistent with calcium oxalate crystal deposition as the primary cause of chronic kidney injury.

Increased expression of PAI-1 and fibronectin is demonstrated in the myocardium of double transgenic rats, as shown by representative immunohistochemical staining. These findings confirm the presence of interstitial fibrosis and established cardiac remodeling in the heart.

Bilateral kidneys exhibit patchy cortical discoloration with alternating hemorrhagic and pale areas on the external surface. These gross findings are consistent with multifocal parenchymal injury, most likely indicative of acute pyelonephritis given the hemorrhagic foci.

Thyroid gland: Focal hydropic degeneration of follicular epithelial cells, characterized by intracytoplasmic accumulation of pale eosinophilic material. Ultrastructural correlates include dilatation of the rough endoplasmic reticulum containing low-electron-density amorphous substance, consistent with early colloid resorption.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “consistent with early colloid resorption” is a subtle misattribution.

Increased ICAM-1 expression is observed in mouse mesangial cells following exposure to peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) culture supernatant. This finding indicates that PBMC-derived factors directly induce mesangial ICAM-1 upregulation, implicating mononuclear cells as key drivers of inflammatory adhesion molecule expression in IgA nephropathy.

Interstitial stem/progenitor cells are identified within the renal medullary interstitium of a developing human kidney, as demonstrated by focal cellular aggregates with morphological characteristics consistent with undifferentiated progenitor populations. These cells are localized to discrete anatomical niches in the medullary interstitium, suggesting region-specific microenvironments that actively drive nephron differentiation.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately states that the microenvironments *support progenitor cell maintenance*, which aligns with current understanding that such niches preserve undifferentiated states.

Histological evaluation of the kidney demonstrates a uniform distribution of glomeruli throughout the renal cortex. Using the dissector method, glomerular number was estimated by systematic sampling with superimposition of a standardized frame over tissue sections, confirming a diagnosis of normal nephron endowment.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis involving multiple glomeruli, characterized by circumferential cellular crescents with minimal immunoglobulin deposition on immunofluorescence, consistent with a pauci-immune pattern. Glomerular capillary walls show no significant immune complex deposition, supporting a diagnosis of ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Electron microscopy of the renal biopsy reveals moderately electron-dense deposits located in the paramesangial regions, consistent with early immune complex deposition as seen in lupus nephritis. No additional ultrastructural abnormalities are described.

Kidney: The renal architecture exhibits preserved histological structure, with intact glomeruli, tubules, interstitium, and vasculature. No pathological alterations are identified in the parenchyma or supporting structures, ruling out chronic kidney disease.

Photomicrograph demonstrates diaminobenzidine-positive pigment casts within renal tubules. The presence of these casts is diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis secondary to myoglobinuric pigment cast nephropathy, most likely resulting from dengue-associated myositis.

Histopathologic examination reveals glomerular hyalinosis and a perirenal hematoma. Glomerular changes are characterized by segmental or global accumulation of amorphous, eosinophilic material within the glomerular tuft, consistent with diabetic nephropathy.

Photomicrograph of a splenic section from a rat receiving zinc supplementation (50 mg/kg) shows hemosiderosis in both the red pulp and white pulp, characterized by increased deposition of hemosiderin-laden macrophages. The presence of hemosiderin in the white pulp is consistent with early iron overload secondary to zinc-induced hemolysis.

Spinal cord: Focal arterial wall mineralization is present, consistent with medial calcific sclerosis. Mineralization is occasionally observed within the meningeal arteries and may contribute to ischemic injury in this region.

Kidney: Immature glomerulus. The glomerular capillary tuft shows indistinct capillary formation, with prominence of epithelial cells, consistent with an immature architectural pattern and suggestive of congenital nephrotic syndrome.

Pulmonary mucormycosis diagnosed in the setting of previously undiagnosed chronic renal failure, likely contributing to immunosuppression and fungal susceptibility. Histopathological correlation not available.

Renal cortical necrosis is present, characterized by coagulative necrosis of glomeruli and tubules. Glomeruli exhibit collapse and loss of cellular detail with preserved architecture, consistent with acute tubular necrosis secondary to ischemic injury.
Squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated, involving the ear. The tumor conforms to the underlying cartilage contours, indicating perineural invasion.

Electron microscopy reveals interstitial extravasation of red blood cells within renal parenchyma, accompanied by apoptotic nuclei in interstitial cells. Morphological findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis with superimposed ischemic injury and cellular apoptosis at the ultrastructural level.

Renal biopsy shows glomerular basement membrane thickening that is prominent and uniform, in the absence of significant cellular proliferation. These morphological findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Glomerulus demonstrates global collapse with marked hypercellularity and capillary loop obliteration, consistent with advanced ischemic changes. The lesion is surrounded by a dense interstitial inflammatory infiltrate, suggestive of an underlying autoimmune glomerulonephritis.
lesion_type: global glomerulosclerosis with hypercellularity and capillary loop obliteration

answer_text:
The provided text does not contain any clinically relevant renal pathological findings or diagnostic information. It describes a molecular biology research procedure involving phage display and DNA cloning in the context of osteosarcoma vasculature targeting, with no reference to kidney tissue, histopathological examination, or renal disease processes.

Nevertheless, the absence of overt renal involvement in this experimental model suggests that the described targeting peptides are unlikely to cross-react with glomerular basement membrane antigens, implying a low risk of immune-mediated nephropathy in potential therapeutic applications.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates two glomeruli with normal capillary architecture and absence of endocapillary hypercellularity, fibrinoid necrosis, or thrombosis. The tubulointerstitial compartment shows no significant inflammatory infiltrate and only minimal interstitial expansion, which is consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Immunohistochemical staining for epithelial membrane antigen demonstrates preserved expression in distal tubular epithelial cells, consistent with normal distal tubular architecture and differentiation. No evidence of acute tubular injury or developmental abnormality is identified in the sampled tubules, supporting a diagnosis of intact renal cortical integrity.

Acute tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by intratubular acute inflammatory infiltrates and necrotic cellular debris within the tubular lumen, consistent with active pyelonephritis, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining at 630× magnification.

Kidney biopsy: Light microscopy with Perl's Prussian blue stain demonstrates iron deposition within intratubular casts and tubular epithelial cells. Findings are diagnostic of hemosiderin accumulation in the renal tubules, confirming prior hemoglobinuria or myoglobinuria as the definitive cause of recurrent acute kidney injury.

Light microscopy reveals mild mesangial cell proliferation and capillary wall thickening within the glomeruli. These findings are accompanied by podocyte foot process effacement, consistent with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Granular immunofluorescence deposition of IgG is observed. Although linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane is typically characteristic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody disease, the granular pattern seen here is inconsistent with anti-GBM disease and instead strongly supports a diagnosis of lupus nephritis.

Ovary: Mesonephric cyst. The cyst is lined by cuboidal to flattened epithelium and is surrounded by a distinct pericystic layer containing well-developed smooth muscle, consistent with derivation from remnants of the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct, which are commonly retained in the ovarian hilum and capable of giving rise to true mesonephric adenocarcinoma.

Renal biopsy specimen shows acute thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) characterized by occlusive endothelial swelling of glomerular capillary loops, intraluminal fragmented red blood cells, and fibrin-rich thrombi involving both glomerular capillaries and arterioles. These findings are diagnostic of complement-mediated atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS).

Photomicrograph of a pancreatic section from a control Wistar rat, showing normal histological architecture. The exocrine pancreas demonstrates intact acinar cells with preserved cytoplasmic basophilia and abundant zymogen granules, consistent with active enzyme synthesis and secretion.

Familial lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) deficiency is a rare inherited metabolic disorder characterized by abnormal lipid metabolism and associated renal involvement. Renal pathology typically demonstrates glomerular changes secondary to lipid deposition, including membranous glomerulopathy with capillary wall thickening and prominent subepithelial immune complex deposits.

Western blot analysis of serum using a polyclonal anti-CFHR5 antibody demonstrates a slower migrating band in affected patients, consistent with the predicted molecular weight of CFHR5 (approximately 121–123 kDa). This finding confirms the presence of a pathogenic CFHR5 mutation.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate mild mesangial matrix expansion with mesangial hypercellularity and mild thickening of the paramesangial capillary walls, associated with narrowing of capillary lumina. The extraglomerular compartment shows focal mild interstitial fibrosis, focal tubular atrophy with dilatation and flattening of tubular epithelium, presence of hyaline casts, and focal interstitial infiltrates composed predominantly of lymphocytes with occasional eosinophils.

The glomerular changes are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy, given the mesangial proliferative pattern observed, which is commonly associated with underlying nephrotic syndrome.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates ischaemic glomeruli and arterial wall thickening associated with thrombotic microangiopathy. Findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy involving glomerular and arterial microvasculature, characterized by endothelial injury, microthrombi, and ischaemic changes.

Stomach: Focal erosion with associated gastritis. Gastric erosions in cynomolgus monkeys (Macaca fascicularis) are invariably linked to underlying Helicobacter infection and are rarely observed under non-stressed conditions, typically indicating active inflammatory disease.

Transmission electron microscopy of renal glomeruli in monkeys following a single intravitreal injection of aflibercept demonstrates ultrastructural morphology preserved across multiple image arrangements (MIAs) at ×3000 magnification. The montage provides a comprehensive view of the glomerular capillary loops, including podocytes, basement membrane, and endothelial components, with no evidence of immune complex deposition or subepithelial electron-dense deposits, effectively ruling out early immune-mediated glomerular injury.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate tubulointerstitial fibrosis and diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane, as highlighted by Masson's trichrome stain (×200). The morphological features are diagnostic of membranous glomerulonephritis.

The specimen demonstrates expression of histocompatibility antigens and the presence of mononuclear cell infiltrates within neoplastic renal tissue. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals antigen presentation profiles consistent with active T-cell–mediated immune rejection of the tumor.

The provided text does not contain pathological findings, histological descriptions, or diagnostic assessments relevant to renal pathology. It is a conceptual diagram caption from a cardiovascular physiology or pharmacology context, referencing hemodynamic parameters (LVEDP, PCWP) and loop diuretic mechanisms in acute heart failure; however, the depicted diuretic action on the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle suggests direct nephrotoxic injury, which may contribute to acute kidney injury in this setting.

Renal biopsy demonstrates stage 3 membranous glomerulonephritis. Transmission electron microscopy reveals medium-sized subepithelial electron-dense deposits with associated basement membrane thickening and spike formation surrounding the majority of deposits (arrows), consistent with advanced immune complex deposition typical of primary membranous nephropathy.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate diffuse interstitial plasma cell infiltration with positive immunohistochemical staining for IgG4. Increased numbers of IgG4-positive plasma cells are present within the renal interstitium, consistent with a diagnosis of IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Photomicrograph of cardiac tissue from broiler chickens fed a control diet with 0% hydrolyzed cassava peel as the primary carbohydrate source. No pathological lesions or structural abnormalities are identified in the myocardium, indicating normal cardiac histology consistent with healthy birds.

Bilateral renal cortical necrosis involving the glomeruli. Light microscopy demonstrates complete necrosis of the glomerular tufts, characterized by loss of cellular detail, nuclear pyknosis, and eosinophilic collapse of capillary loops, consistent with acute tubular necrosis secondary to ischemic injury.

Immunoblot analysis demonstrated expression of ADAMTS13 protein (~150 kDa) in conditioned media from cultured glomerular endothelial cells (CiGEnC) and human dermal microvascular endothelial cells (HMVEC), consistent with secretion of functional ADAMTS13. A corresponding band was observed in recombinant ADAMTS13 positive control; no signal was detected in control medium.

Glomerular lesions associated with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection are characterized by distinct histopathological patterns, most commonly HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN). Light microscopy typically reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) with collapsing variant morphology, marked podocyte hyperplasia, microcystic tubular dilation, and varying degrees of interstitial inflammation and fibrosis, which are diagnostic of HIVAN in the appropriate clinical context.

Renal biopsy (×400 magnification) demonstrates focal interstitial lymphocytic infiltrates. Associated focal tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis are present, with periglomerular fibrosis observed within affected areas, consistent with chronic T-cell–mediated rejection.

Stomach: Lymphoplasmacytic infiltration of the lamina propria is present in the fundus and pylorus, consistent with chronic active gastritis. This finding is frequently observed in cynomolgus monkeys and may be associated with mucosal epithelial regeneration.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately uses “chronic gastritis,” which aligns with lymphoplasmacytic infiltration—a hallmark of chronic inflammation.

Thyroid and parathyroid tissue: ectopic salivary gland tissue identified within the thyroid and/or parathyroid. The ectopic tissue consists of salivary acini and ducts, consistent with salivary gland heterotopia, which is diagnostic of branchial cleft remnant pathology.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates findings consistent with IgA nephropathy in the setting of malignant hypertension and nephrotic-range proteinuria, in the absence of hematuria.

Light microscopy (PAS, silver, and trichrome stains) reveals 12 glomeruli, of which 7 are globally sclerotic. Three glomeruli exhibit segmental sclerosis with synechiae to Bowman’s capsule, supporting a diagnosis of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) superimposed on IgA nephropathy.
lesion_type: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis superimposed on iga nephropathy

answer_text: Kidney sections from a rat treated with gold nanoparticles (GNPs) at a dose of 100 μL of 20 nm particles for 7 days show cytoplasmic degeneration of renal tubular epithelial cells and nuclear destruction, consistent with acute tubular necrosis. Intertubular hemorrhage is present, likely resulting from direct vascular injury caused by nanoparticle accumulation.

Glomerular biopsy demonstrates membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by endocapillary hypercellularity and lobular accentuation of the glomerular tufts on H&E staining. These findings are consistent with secondary involvement due to Pott's disease (spinal tuberculosis), although no granulomatous inflammation or acid-fast bacilli are identified in the renal tissue.

Peripheral blood smear demonstrates neutrophil dysplasia characterized by abnormal nuclear segmentation and hypogranulation. These morphological findings are consistent with myelodysplastic syndrome in a pediatric patient with severe nephrotic syndrome receiving immunosuppressive therapy.

The provided text does not contain pathological findings or diagnostic material suitable for a renal pathology report. It appears to be a general educational review concerning renal anatomy, biochemistry, and physiology, rather than a description of histopathological, structural, or clinical pathological data from kidney tissue or specimens.
Focal nodular glomerulosclerosis involving glomerular basement membrane thickening, diagnostic of diabetic nephropathy, observed in a rat model 56 days following Vacor administration (H&E, ×400).

Hematoxylin and eosin staining demonstrates tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and thickened blood vessel walls. These findings are diagnostic of chronic hypertensive nephrosclerosis; however, specific etiologic correlation requires integration with clinical and laboratory data.

Light microscopy of the renal biopsy using silver stain at ×400 magnification demonstrates a bloodless glomerulus. Glomerular capillary lumina are markedly narrowed or obliterated due to diffuse endothelial cell swelling, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Photomicrographs of a kidney section from a rat exposed to cypermethrin (20 mg/kg/day for 30 days) demonstrate glomerular shrinkage and tubular necrosis with disruption of renal tubular architecture. Findings are diagnostic of cypermethrin-induced acute tubular necrosis, characterized by structural damage to glomeruli and proximal tubules.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate global epimembranous spike formation and capillary wall vacuolization within glomeruli, consistent with early membranous nephropathy. Mesangial matrix expansion and mild mesangial hypercellularity are present, suggestive of concurrent IgA nephropathy.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury characterized by tubular epithelial cell swelling, focal areas of tubular cell necrosis with denudation of the tubular basement membrane, and attenuation of the brush border. These morphological findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis secondary to adefovir-induced nephrotoxicity.

Renal biopsy demonstrates thrombotic microangiopathy involving glomeruli, characterized by narrowing of glomerular capillary lumina and intraluminal fibrin thrombi. These findings are diagnostic of acute interstitial nephritis.

Kidney section, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain: The renal parenchyma exhibits glomerular and tubular hemorrhage, intravascular thrombosis within glomeruli and peritubular capillaries, and multifocal infiltration of inflammatory cells. These histopathological findings are diagnostic of acute pyelonephritis secondary to systemic bacterial infection.

Succinate dehydrogenase (SDH)-deficient renal carcinoma exhibits characteristic morphological features, although variability may be observed in higher-grade tumors. In cases demonstrating elevated nuclear grade and early dedifferentiation, intracytoplasmic inclusions are typically absent and can generally be excluded without exhaustive histological evaluation.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse infiltration of the renal parenchyma by monomorphic, medium-sized lymphoid cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, finely dispersed chromatin, and inconspicuous nucleoli. Mitotic figures are readily identified, consistent with a diagnosis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving the kidney.

Histologic examination demonstrates multinucleated osteoclast-type giant cells within a fibrovascular stroma. The lesional tissue exhibits a prominent vascular network and areas of metaplastic calcified matrix with granular, eosinophilic (‘grungy’) appearance, consistent with osteoid production by neoplastic stromal cells.

Renal parenchymal changes consistent with chronic pyelonephritis secondary to renal vascular occlusion in the rat model. Focal cortical scarring, tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and mononuclear inflammatory infiltrates are observed, predominantly in regions corresponding to vascular territories affected by occlusion, indicating a direct causal relationship between vascular compromise and pyelonephritic changes.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates focal glomerular necrosis with associated cellular crescent formation. Glomeruli exhibit segmental necrotizing changes, accompanied by a recent cellular crescent, as highlighted in the trichrome-stained section, consistent with active anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Glomeruli exhibit diffuse global mesangial sclerosis with expansion of the mesangial matrix, resulting in complete obliteration of capillary lumina. Bowman’s spaces remain patent, consistent with advanced diabetic glomerulosclerosis.

Cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis characterized by prominent intraglomerular macrophage infiltration. Morphological findings are diagnostic of cryoglobulin-associated glomerular injury, presenting with features overlapping those of atypical post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “are diagnostic of” implies unwarranted certainty.

Transmission electron microscopy of a skin biopsy demonstrates granular osmiophilic material (GOM) localized within an infolding of a vascular smooth muscle cell (VSMC). The presence of GOM is diagnostic of cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL).

Light microscopy reveals a glomerulus with a segmental fibrocellular crescent. The histological findings, in conjunction with clinical and laboratory data, are diagnostic of concomitant granulomatosis with polyangiitis and C3 glomerulonephritis as the definitive causes of renal failure.

Renal biopsy demonstrates strong IgG immunofluorescence staining with prominent deposition in the tubular basement membranes and Bowman’s capsules. Interstitial IgG deposits are also present, consistent with immune complex–mediated tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Warfarin-associated acute kidney injury:  
Renal biopsy demonstrates extensive and diffuse acute tubular injury. Numerous occlusive red blood cell casts are present within the lumina of proximal convoluted tubules and collecting ducts.
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with red blood cell casts

answer_text: The provided text describes neuroanatomical findings in the antennal lobe of the yellow fever mosquito, *Aedes aegypti*. Although this is non-human tissue, certain structural features—such as glomerular-like formations observed in the antennal lobe—bear superficial resemblance to human renal glomeruli.

Electron microscopy reveals extensive podocyte foot process effacement and irregular thickening of the glomerular basement membrane. Scattered deposits of immune-type electron-dense material are present within the basement membrane, consistent with early membranous nephropathy.

The provided text describes immunoblot analysis of tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) in the central nervous system of invertebrate species. Although this model system does not directly involve human renal tissue, the observed TH expression pattern may suggest a potential role for catecholaminergic signaling in renal tubular function, implying possible relevance to human renal pathology.

Metanephric stromal tumor: A rare benign pediatric renal neoplasm with stromal differentiation. Gross examination reveals a well-circumscribed, firm, gray-white mass measuring 7.5 × 5.5 × 4.5 cm, which replaces the majority of the renal parenchyma and extends into the renal pelvis, suggesting early infiltrative behavior.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis with evidence of acute kidney injury. In addition, there is interstitial infiltration by atypical lymphoid cells forming nodular aggregates within the renal parenchyma, consistent with low-grade B-cell lymphoma.
lesion_type: membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis and low-grade b-cell lymphoma

answer_text: Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerular fibrin thrombi and vascular congestion, as highlighted on Jones stain, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy. These morphological features are diagnostic of hemolytic uremic syndrome.
Heart: Focal, mild hemorrhage involving the endocardium and subendocardium. This finding is consistent with early myocardial infarction and is not indicative of agonal change.

Liver: Diffuse microvesicular steatosis involving hepatocytes across all hepatic zones. Numerous small lipid droplets, manifesting as clear cytoplasmic vacuoles, are present within hepatocytes, consistent with fatty change and indicative of early alcoholic steatohepatitis.

Photomicrograph demonstrates a small dermal artery exhibiting fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall. This finding is diagnostic of systemic vasculitis and directly explains the concurrent acute kidney injury observed in this dog.

Renal biopsy demonstrates tubular distension by clear crystalline material, consistent with oxalate crystals. Associated mild to moderate interstitial mononuclear inflammation is present. The morphological findings are diagnostic of primary hyperoxaluria.

Light microscopy (PAS stain) of renal biopsy reveals mild mesangial matrix expansion, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy. No significant glomerular hypercellularity, crescent formation, or capillary wall thickening is identified.

The specimen demonstrates expression of histocompatibility antigens and the presence of mononuclear cell infiltrates within neoplastic renal tissue. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals antigen presentation profiles consistent with active T-cell–mediated immune rejection of the tumor.

Perinatal nephropathies refer to a spectrum of kidney disorders arising during the perinatal period, encompassing developmental abnormalities, genetic diseases, and acquired injuries affecting the fetal or neonatal kidney. These conditions may involve structural anomalies of the renal parenchyma, including dysplastic changes characterized by disorganized differentiation of nephrogenic tissue, primitive ductal structures, metaplastic cartilage, and interstitial fibrosis—findings that are pathognomonic for congenital nephrotic syndrome.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “findings that are pathognomonic for congenital nephrotic syndrome” is the subtle medical error.

Renal neoplasm in rat: Disorganized proliferation of epithelial cells exhibiting nuclear pleomorphism and infiltrative growth into the renal cortex, with circumferential envelopment of an intact glomerulus. Histologic features are diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

Photomicrograph of the liver from broiler chickens fed a diet in which hydrolyzed cassava peel meal, derived from *Aspergillus niger* fermentation, constituted 100% of the carbohydrate source. Hepatic tissue exhibits diffuse vacuolar degeneration with marked cytoplasmic clearing in hepatocytes, consistent with lipid accumulation (hepatic steatosis), likely resulting from impaired very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) secretion due to mycotoxin exposure from the fungal fermentation process.

Electron microscopy demonstrates subendothelial electron-dense deposits along the glomerular basement membrane, with additional mesangial electron-dense deposits. These findings are diagnostic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN), type 1.
Secondary membranous nephropathy associated with lupus nephritis. Electron microscopy reveals subepithelial electron-dense deposits adjacent to the glomerular basement membrane (thin arrows).

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by cellular crescents involving a subset of glomeruli. Glomerular capillary loops are partially disrupted by circumferential accumulations of epithelial cells and inflammatory cells within Bowman’s space, consistent with an active anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease process.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates findings diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) secondary to interferon beta-1a therapy.  

Histopathological examination reveals diffuse interstitial fibrosis and focal tubular atrophy, which are characteristic features of TMA.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate moderate tubular atrophy and globally sclerotic glomeruli, as evidenced by PAS staining. The histological features are diagnostic of chronic immunoglobulin A nephropathy.

Positive expression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is demonstrated by the presence of brown-yellow granular immunostaining localized to the cytoplasm of endothelial cells within placental tissue, as observed microscopically at 20× magnification. The finding confirms VEGF protein expression in placental vascular endothelium and suggests a potential role in mediating glomerular endothelial dysfunction, although no direct assessment of renal or urinary tract structures is described.

Glomerular depth was measured as the shortest vertical distance from the kidney surface to the nearest aspect of each glomerulus in superficial nephrons of BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice. These superficial glomeruli are uniquely suited for in vivo imaging using multiphoton microscopy due to their exclusive presence in the outer cortex.

Immunofluorescence microscopy of the renal biopsy demonstrates granular deposition of immunoglobulins and complement components within the glomerular mesangium. Predominant IgA deposits are present, along with co-deposition of C3 and lesser amounts of IgG and IgM, diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrating recurrent fibronectin glomerulopathy.

Histopathological examination reveals enlarged glomeruli with acellular expansion of the mesangial matrix. Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining highlights mesangial deposits, which are characteristic of fibronectin glomerulopathy and confirm the diagnosis of recurrence in this allograft.

Electron microscopy demonstrates nonfibrillar, electron-dense deposits localized to the subendothelial region of the glomerular basement membrane. These findings are diagnostic of light chain deposition disease.

Lung parenchyma demonstrates diffuse intraalveolar hemorrhage with numerous pulmonary macrophages present within alveolar spaces. Hemosiderin-laden macrophages are evident in both perivascular and interstitial (intraseptal) locations, confirming a diagnosis of chronic or recurrent pulmonary hemorrhage.

Pancreas: The islet tissue exhibits lobulation into multiple compartments delineated by fibrous septa, consistent with fibrosis. This finding indicates chronic autoimmune-mediated destruction of the islets, as seen in type 1 diabetes mellitus.

Class III – Focal global glomerular lupus nephritis. The lesion involves a subset of glomeruli with diffuse endocapillary and mesangial hypercellularity, consistent with active proliferative lupus nephritis; the presence of global involvement in affected glomeruli confirms Class III rather than Class IV disease.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate diffuse proliferative lupus nephritis, consistent with Class IV lupus nephritis. Light microscopy reveals diffuse endocapillary and mesangial hypercellularity involving multiple glomeruli, with evidence of segmental and global capillary loop involvement, confirming the presence of active necrotizing lesions.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intraluminal translucent crystals consistent with calcium oxalate deposits, associated with tubular epithelial degeneration and a multinucleated foreign body giant cell reaction. These findings are diagnostic of primary hyperoxaluria.

Photomicrograph of a representative kidney section (magnification 100×) shows glomeruli (G), convoluted tubules (CT), and Bowman’s capsule (Cp). Fatty changes (FC) are present in the tubular epithelium, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Karyomegalic interstitial nephritis with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Histopathological examination reveals nuclear enlargement (karyomegaly) of tubular epithelial cells, most prominently observed in the proximal tubules. The karyomegalic nuclei are characterized by marked enlargement, irregular contours, and hyperchromasia, without significant mitotic activity or cytologic atypia suggestive of neoplasia.
lesion_type: karyomegalic interstitial nephritis with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis

answer_text: Renal biopsy findings: Silver stain of glomerulus demonstrates a structurally normal glomerular basement membrane without evidence of thickening, splitting, or lamination. No immune complex deposits are identified, consistent with a diagnosis of minimal change disease.

Renal tissue alterations were observed in a size-dependent manner, with smaller gold nanoparticles inducing more pronounced effects, correlating with duration of exposure. In the F3 group, rats treated with 50 μL of 10 nm gold nanoparticles for 3 days exhibited vacuolar degeneration of renal tubular epithelial cells, consistent with early-stage acute tubular necrosis.

Visceral epithelial cells exhibit finely vacuolated cytoplasm, with curvilinear intracytoplasmic inclusions characteristic of drug-induced phospholipidosis. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of Fabry disease.

Photomicrograph of a gastric section from a rat co-administered zinc and glyphosate (375 mg/kg), stained with hematoxylin and eosin. No microscopic lesions are observed in the mucosal epithelial cells.

answer_text: Kidney biopsy demonstrates a membranoproliferative pattern of glomerular injury. Microscopic examination reveals tubular atrophy with tubular basement membrane collapse or dilatation, flattened tubular epithelium, and the presence of colloid casts within tubular lumina. Chronic pyelonephritis characterized by focal, irregular cortical scarring. Microscopic examination reveals tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and chronic inflammatory cell infiltration (predominantly lymphocytes and plasma cells, with a minority of mononuclear cells and occasional neutrophils), consistent with ongoing active infection.

The ultrastructural examination reveals viral particles consistent with coronavirus morphology, including characteristic spike proteins, observed within pneumocytes at high magnification (×25,000). Alport syndrome. Crystalline inclusions are present within proximal tubular epithelial cells, exhibiting variable size and geometric morphology, including rhomboid and needle-shaped forms. Renal biopsy demonstrates amyloid deposition within the glomeruli and vascular walls, confirmed by the presence of characteristic non-branching fibrillar structures on electron microscopy. The fibrils measure approximately 10 nm in diameter and are randomly arranged, consistent with AL-type amyloidosis.

Yellow granulomatous pyelonephritis involving the renal cortex and medulla, characterized by granulomatous inflammation and microabscess formation. Glomerular basement membranes are diffusely thinned, as observed under electron microscopy at 8,000× magnification using both conventional and paraffin-embedded tissue processing methods. The morphological findings are diagnostic of thin basement membrane nephropathy.

Warfarin-associated nephropathy is characterized by acute tubular injury with prominent intratubular red blood cell casts. These findings reflect glomerular hemorrhage and direct tubular toxicity from warfarin, consistent with anticoagulant-induced renal injury.

answer_text: Electron microscopy (×25,000 magnification) reveals ultrastructural findings diagnostic of viral tubulointerstitial nephritis, including intracellular viral particles within tubular epithelial cells. The morphology and distribution of these particles are characteristic of SARS-associated coronavirus infection.

Acute drug-induced tubulointerstitial nephritis (AIN) is characterized by interstitial inflammatory cell infiltration and edema, with pale interstitium separating tubular structures. The inflammatory infiltrate consists predominantly of lymphocytes, accompanied by plasma cells, macrophages, and frequent eosinophils, which are diagnostic of a drug etiology.

Acute drug-induced tubulointerstitial nephritis (AIN) is characterized by interstitial edema and diffuse inflammatory cell infiltration, predominantly composed of eosinophils and mononuclear cells. The renal tubules are separated by expanded, pale-staining interstitium, consistent with early fibrotic changes.

Light-chain proximal tubulopathy, crystalline variant. Electron microscopy demonstrates intracytoplasmic crystalline inclusions within proximal tubular epithelial cells, located either within lysosomes or freely in the cytoplasm.

Immunoelectron microscopy demonstrates colloidal gold-labeled IgA localized predominantly within the mesangial regions of glomeruli, indicating mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes. The distribution of colloidal gold particles confirms the presence and localization of IgA antigen-antibody complexes in the mesangium, diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

A prominent feature of acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) is tubulitis, characterized by the presence of lymphocytes and/or mononuclear cells invading the tubular epithelium. This finding is typically observed in areas of tubular injury and is sufficient on its own to confirm a diagnosis of AIN, even in the absence of significant interstitial inflammation.

Light-chain proximal tubulopathy, crystalline variant. Renal tubular epithelial cells contain intracytoplasmic crystalline inclusions exhibiting κ light-chain restriction, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining, consistent with monoclonal gammopathy of renal significance (MGRS).

Immunoelectron microscopy demonstrates colloidal gold-labeled secondary antibody binding to immunoglobulin λ light chains localized along amyloid fibrils within the mesangial matrix. These findings confirm a diagnosis of AL amyloidosis with predominant λ light chain deposition in glomerular basement membranes (immunoelectron microscopy, ×20,000 magnification).

Lipofuscin deposition is observed in renal tubular epithelial cells. This finding is consistent with age-related accumulation of undegraded cellular debris within autophagolysosomes, resulting from diminished lysosomal enzymatic activity and reduced cellular turnover in the elderly, and is diagnostic of chronic kidney disease attributable to aging.

Renal tubular epithelial cells show pigment deposition consistent with ochronotic pigment, characterized by golden-brown, banana-shaped crystals within the cytoplasm. The findings are diagnostic of alkaptonuria, an autosomal recessive disorder resulting from deficiency of homogentisic acid oxidase, leading to accumulation of ochronotic pigment in various tissues, including the renal tubular epithelium.

Renal tubular epithelial cells show intracytoplasmic brown granular deposits, consistent with heavy metal accumulation. Morphological findings are diagnostic of mercury-associated nephropathy, characterized by pigmented granules within proximal tubular epithelial cells.

Light-chain casts are strongly eosinophilic on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, weakly positive to negative on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain, and exhibit metachromasia on trichrome staining. Membranous nephropathy: numerous subepithelial immune complex deposits observed within glomerular capillary walls. The deposits are associated with glomerular basement membrane thickening and podocyte foot process effacement, consistent with immune-mediated injury and indicative of advanced disease stage.

Interstitial infiltrates composed of mononuclear cells with a prominent admixture of eosinophils are present. Multinucleated giant cells and epithelioid histiocytes form well-developed granulomatous lesions within the interstitium, consistent with sarcoidosis.

These casts are frequently surrounded by a histiocytic cellular reaction, which is diagnostic of cast nephropathy.

Renal tubular epithelial cells exhibit prominent regenerative capacity. Renal tubules show destruction with granuloma formation, consistent with tuberculous interstitial nephritis.
Glomerular urinary pole involved, consistent with early segmental sclerosis.
Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) with prominent eosinophilic infiltration, occasionally forming small eosinophilic microabscesses within the renal tubules (HE, ×200).

Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN). Tubular epithelial injury with loss of tubular epithelial cells is present; the tubular basement membranes are largely intact, consistent with acute tubular necrosis (PAS, ×400).

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates strong and selective staining of tubular casts with anti-kappa light chain antibody, showing kappa light chain restriction. The pattern is diagnostic of light-chain cast nephropathy.

Apoptotic renal tubular epithelial cells are identified by nuclear changes including chromatin condensation and margination along the nuclear membrane (chromatin margination), with preservation of cellular and organelle architecture. lesion_type: apoptosis of renal tubular epithelial cells

answer_text: Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates coarse, granular deposition of complement component C3 within the glomerular capillary walls and mesangium. The staining pattern is irregular and segmental to global, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis, most likely lupus nephritis.

The findings are diagnostic of acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, given the presence of neutrophils within glomerular capillaries.

Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN). Neutrophils are a hallmark feature and are typically prominent in the interstitial infiltrate.

Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN). High-power examination reveals tubulitis, interstitial edema, and lymphocytic infiltration of the renal interstitium, consistent with a T-cell–mediated immune response characteristic of drug-induced AIN.

The glomerulus is composed of a central tuft of capillaries formed by branching of the terminal branches of the renal artery, specifically the afferent arteriole. At the vascular pole, a single afferent arteriole enters the Bowman’s capsule and divides into 4–5 primary branches, each of which further subdivides to form capillary lobules that directly drain into the efferent arteriole without an intervening venous network.
Electron microscopy reveals scattered hump-shaped subepithelial electron-dense deposits. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Renal tubules show atrophy with thickening and wrinkling of the basement membrane, accompanied by attenuation and loss of tubular epithelial cells. These changes are intermixed with areas of compensatory tubular hypertrophy, consistent with chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Renal tubular epithelium shows papillary hyperplasia with tubular dilation. The lesional cells exhibit uniform morphology without cytological atypia, consistent with low-grade papillary renal cell carcinoma.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse proliferative and exudative glomerulonephritis characterized by mild mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity. The histopathological findings are diagnostic of acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, despite the clinical history of methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus aureus* (MRSA) infection at the graft site following coronary artery bypass grafting.

Glomerular capillary wall: The visceral epithelial cells, known as podocytes, are composed of three structural components—cell body, primary processes, and foot processes. The cell bodies and primary processes project into Bowman’s space and are not directly attached to the glomerular basement membrane; only the foot processes establish direct contact with the outer aspect of the basement membrane and are solely responsible for maintaining its structural integrity.

Renal tubular epithelium shows papillary hyperplasia with tubular dilation. The papillary projections lack fibrovascular cores and are therefore characterized as true papillary structures.

answer_text: Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN). Mononuclear cells are observed transmigrating through the peritubular capillary endothelium, consistent with active tubulointerstitial inflammation, as demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (×3000).

The primary lesion involves the renal interstitium, characterized by diffuse interstitial congestion, edema, and inflammatory infiltrate. The infiltrate is predominantly composed of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells, with T lymphocytes (CD3-positive) being the major component, consistent with acute cellular rejection.

The renal capsule, also known as Bowman’s capsule, is a bilayered epithelial structure forming the initial segment of the renal tubule and enclosing the glomerular capillary tuft. It consists of two cellular layers: the visceral layer, which closely adheres to the glomerular capillary basement membrane, and the parietal layer, which lines the outer aspect of the renal capsule and directly interfaces with the proximal convoluted tubule.

Renal tubular epithelial cells exhibit vacuolar and granular degeneration with loss of brush border and flattening of the epithelium. Tubular dilation is present, accompanied by variable degrees of tubulitis, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

Glomerular visceral epithelial cells (podocytes) are situated external to the glomerular basement membrane (GBM). Ultrastructurally, podocytes consist of three components: the cell body, primary processes, and foot processes (pedicels), which directly synapse with endothelial cells across the GBM to facilitate filtration.

The renal tubule demonstrates a U-shaped segment composed of the straight portion of the proximal tubule, thin segment, and straight portion of the distal tubule, corresponding to the medullary loop (loop of Henle). The loop exhibits a descending limb, extending from the cortex into the medulla, and an ascending limb, returning from the medulla toward the cortex, which is lined by thick cuboidal epithelium characteristic of the distal convoluted tubule.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate C3-dominant glomerular immune deposits on immunofluorescence, with concomitant bright IgG and less intense IgA staining. Electron microscopy reveals subendothelial immune complex deposits consistent with lupus nephritis, and histopathology shows endocapillary hypercellularity.

The differential diagnosis includes sarcoidosis, which may also demonstrate numerous granulomatous lesions on biopsy. Sarcoidosis is characterized by granulomatous interstitial nephritis with well-circumscribed, sarcoid-type granulomas involving the renal interstitium and glomeruli.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity with prominent infiltration by polymorphonuclear leukocytes, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals C3-dominant immune complex deposition, supporting a diagnosis of dense deposit disease.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate endocapillary hypercellularity, mesangial hypercellularity, and the presence of immune complex deposits on electron microscopy. Electron-dense deposits are observed in the mesangium and as small, scattered subendothelial deposits, consistent with a diagnosis of lupus nephritis class III.

**Pathology Description:**

The collecting duct epithelium is lined by principal cells (also referred to as light cells), which are relatively abundant. The luminal surface of these cells exhibits sparse, short microvilli and a single primary cilium arising from a distinct basal body, consistent with early signs of ciliopathy-related tubular dysfunction.

Focal lymphoid follicle-like structures are present, with CD20-positive B lymphocytes localized within the germinal centers (as demonstrated in figures 9-4 and 9-5). The kidney shows features diagnostic of acute interstitial nephritis, directly caused by peritubular lymphoid aggregates exhibiting germinal center formation.

Renal tubular epithelial cells exhibit vacuolar and granular degeneration with loss of brush border and flattening of the epithelium. Tubular lumina are dilated, and there is evidence of focal tubulitis, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

**Pathology Description:**

The primary pathological findings involve the renal interstitium, which demonstrates diffuse interstitial congestion, edema, and inflammatory infiltrates. The infiltrate is predominantly composed of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells, with T lymphocytes (CD3-positive) being the major component; B lymphocytes and plasma cells are sparse.
The renal biopsy shows granulomatous structures and interstitial fibrosis. Multinucleated giant cells and non-caseating granulomas are present within the interstitium, consistent with sarcoidosis.

Interstitial granulomatous inflammation is present, characterized by aggregates of mononuclear cells and epithelioid macrophages, with occasional foci of necrosis identified on Jones silver stain (×200). The morphologic findings are diagnostic of sarcoidosis; however, correlation with clinical and radiological findings is recommended to confirm systemic involvement.

Renal biopsy shows glomerulomegaly with glomerular diameter exceeding 200 μm, as demonstrated on PASM-Masson stain. Morphological findings are diagnostic of obesity-related glomerulopathy, characterized by marked glomerular enlargement in the absence of significant immune complex deposition or other primary glomerular disease.

Human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) is characterized by distinctive pathological findings involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments of the kidney. Glomerular pathology is marked by collapse of the glomerular tuft, which is diagnostic of HIVAN and represents a specific form of collapsing glomerulopathy unique to this condition.

Focal disruption of the glomerular basement membrane is associated with the formation of cellular crescents, characterized by circumferential accumulation of inflammatory cells and parietal epithelial cell proliferation within Bowman's space. These findings are diagnostic of pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis and may be observed in the context of acute interstitial nephritis with concomitant glomerular involvement.

**Renal Tubular Morphology and Functional Features:**

- **Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT):**  
  The epithelial cell morphology varies with luminal caliber. When the lumen is dilated, the epithelium appears flattened; when constricted, the cells are cuboidal to columnar.

**Pathology Description:**

The macula densa is a specialized region of the distal convoluted tubule located adjacent to the vascular pole of the renal glomerulus. Here, the epithelial cells become tall columnar with closely packed, deeply basophilic nuclei, forming an oval, plaque-like elevation projecting into the tubular lumen.

Kidney pathology in human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) demonstrates characteristic lesions involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments. Glomerular findings include collapse and sclerosis of the glomerular capillary loops with associated podocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia, which are diagnostic of HIVAN even in the absence of clinical history or viral confirmation.

Renal biopsy shows increased glomerular cellularity characterized by proliferation of intrinsic glomerular cells, including endothelial cells, mesangial cells, and podocytes. In 2–3 μm tissue sections, the average number of nuclei per glomerulus in normal adult kidneys typically does not exceed 70–80, and values above this threshold are diagnostic of proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Renal arteriolar narrowing associated with ischemic glomerular atrophy is commonly observed. Glomeruli demonstrate fibrosis and global collapse with reduced glomerular size, consistent with chronic thrombotic microangiopathy, as highlighted by PASM-Masson trichrome stain.

Focal lymphoid follicle-like structures are identified within the renal interstitium, with germinal centers showing positivity for CD20, indicating the presence of B-lymphocytes. These findings are diagnostic of chronic active interstitial nephritis.

Interstitial infiltrates may be prominent and, when forming granulomas, are diagnostic of sarcoidosis. If central necrosis is present within granulomas, differential diagnoses including fungal infection and tuberculosis must be excluded.

Granulomatous inflammation with central necrosis is present. Given the presence of necrotizing granulomas, this is diagnostic of tuberculosis; however, fungal infection remains a less likely possibility, and special stains for acid-fast bacilli and fungi are recommended.

Granulomas with necrosis are diagnostic of infectious etiologies, particularly tuberculosis and fungal infection; special stains for microorganisms are recommended. Sarcoidosis should be considered in the differential diagnosis.

Kidney pathology in human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) demonstrates characteristic lesions involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments. Glomerular findings typically include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with collapsing features, which are pathognomonic for HIVAN and sufficient for definitive diagnosis in the appropriate clinical context.

Kidney pathology in human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) demonstrates characteristic lesions involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments. Glomerular findings include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with collapsing features, which are pathognomonic for HIVAN and sufficient for definitive diagnosis in the appropriate clinical context.

Glomerular endothelial cell hyperplasia is present, often associated with marked narrowing of capillary lumina. Depending on the extent of involvement, the proliferation may be classified as focal or diffuse, and is diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

**Pathology Description:**

The primary pathological findings are located in the renal interstitium. There is diffuse interstitial congestion, edema, and inflammatory cell infiltration composed predominantly of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells, consistent with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Kidney pathology in human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) demonstrates characteristic lesions involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments. Glomerular findings include diffuse global glomerulosclerosis, often with collapsing features.

Granulomatous inflammation is identified, with direct glomerular invasion by granulomas observed during the inflammatory process (Jones silver stain, ×400). The presence of necrosis within granulomas confirms an infectious etiology, most likely tuberculosis or fungal infection; therefore, special stains for infectious organisms should be performed to identify the specific pathogen.

Glomerular endothelial cell hyperplasia is present, associated with marked narrowing of capillary lumina. The presence of endothelial hyperplasia alone is sufficient to classify the lesion as diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Subacute interstitial nephritis is characterized by interstitial infiltration with chronic inflammatory cells (e.g., lymphocytes, plasma cells, and occasional eosinophils) accompanied by early interstitial fibrosis. This pattern definitively indicates progression from acute interstitial nephritis toward chronic interstitial nephritis and excludes the possibility of an acute exacerbation of pre-existing chronic disease.
Chronic interstitial nephritis: diffuse interstitial fibrosis with associated tubular atrophy, consistent with end-stage renal disease.
Diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis with prominent intracapillary neutrophil infiltration, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.
Chronic interstitial nephritis characterized by diffuse interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and glomerulosclerosis secondary to chronic ischemia.
Focal segmental mesangial hypercellularity is present, characterized by mild segmental proliferation of mesangial cells within glomeruli, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.
Mild mesangial hypercellularity is present. Capillary lumina are patent and not compressed. There is marked compression of arteriolar lumina, consistent with severe arteriolosclerosis.

Kidney interstitium shows diffuse infiltration by mononuclear cells, predominantly T lymphocytes (CD3-positive), accompanied by monocytes. The predominant T-lymphocyte-rich inflammatory infiltrate is diagnostic of acute allergic interstitial nephritis, reflecting a cellular hypersensitivity reaction.

**Pathology Description:**

Renal tubules show characteristic findings of acute tubular necrosis. Focal tubular epithelial cell necrosis is present, with loss of nuclei and detachment of necrotic epithelial cells from the basement membrane, resulting in intraluminal cellular debris.

Kidney pathology in human immunodeficiency virus–associated nephropathy (HIVAN) demonstrates characteristic lesions involving the glomerular, tubular, and interstitial compartments. Glomerular findings include focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with collapsing features, which are pathognomonic for HIVAN and sufficient for definitive diagnosis in the appropriate clinical context.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular deposits of C3, IgG, and IgM within the glomerular capillary walls and mesangium. In addition, large, irregular immune complex aggregates—resembling pseudothrombi—are present within capillary lumina, with associated granular staining interpreted as subendothelial immune complex deposition along the glomerular basement membrane.

Renal tubules without overt necrosis show luminal dilation and are lined by flattened epithelial cells. The proximal tubular epithelium exhibits attenuation or loss of the brush border, consistent with acute tubular injury, as demonstrated by PAS staining.

Renal interstitium shows minimal B-lymphocytic infiltration, as demonstrated by CD20 immunohistochemistry (×400), with predominantly negative staining. Histopathological findings are diagnostic of allergic interstitial nephritis.

Focal tubular epithelial necrosis is present, characterized by loss of viable epithelial cells and detachment from the basement membrane, with intraluminal cellular debris observed within renal tubules. These findings are diagnostic of acute tubular necrosis.

Light microscopy reveals a membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) pattern characterized by mesangial hypercellularity and expansion, endocapillary proliferation with monocytic infiltration, and thickened capillary walls. Glomerular capillary loops contain large, eosinophilic, periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive intraluminal deposits that occupy the lumens and are designated as "pseudothrombi." These deposits correspond to intraglomerular accumulations of immunoglobulin/cryoglobulin complexes and are diagnostic of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.

Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by mesangial cell proliferation. Mesangial hypercellularity is evident, with increased mesangial cells within the glomerular tufts, consistent with immune complex deposition as the underlying etiology.

Electron microscopy reveals subendothelial electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) exhibiting an organized tubular substructure. These findings are diagnostic of immune complex deposits containing cryoglobulins, typically associated with hepatitis C virus–related mixed cryoglobulinemia.

Acute tubular necrosis. Flattened regenerating tubular epithelial cells line the basement membranes. Foci of calcification are present within the tubular epithelium, consistent with early dystrophic calcification secondary to ischemic injury.
Mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis. Histopathological findings demonstrate increased mesangial matrix with mesangial hypercellularity, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Severe mesangial matrix expansion is observed, associated with compression and narrowing of capillary lumina. These findings are diagnostic of amyloidosis, though light chain deposition disease remains a less likely possibility.
Acute tubular necrosis. Proteinaceous casts composed of Tamm-Horsfall protein are present within proximal tubules.

Acute tubular necrosis. In the absence of overt necrotic cells, the predominant findings are loss of normal proximal tubular architecture, with dilatation of proximal tubular lumina and flattening of the tubular epithelial cells, consistent with irreversible tubular injury.

Cellular variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized by segmental or global endocapillary hypercellularity involving the glomerular tuft, with proliferation of foam cells, macrophages, endothelial cells, and occasional neutrophils and lymphocytes. Apoptotic changes—including nuclear pyknosis and karyorrhexis—are commonly observed among the proliferating cells and are diagnostic of active cellular FSGS.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “and are diagnostic of active cellular FSGS” overstates the specificity of apoptotic changes.

answer_text:

The specimen contains 11 glomeruli: two are globally sclerosed; seven exhibit segmental glomerulosclerosis, associated with tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, accompanied by a dense interstitial inflammatory infiltrate; and two demonstrate active proliferative changes—including mesangial expansion, endocapillary hypercellularity, double contours of the glomerular basement membrane, and granular immune deposits along capillary walls, consistent with immune-complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately attributes the findings to PGNMID—a diagnosis that requires demonstration of monoclonal immunoglobulin deposits (typically by immunofluorescence or mass spectrometry) and excludes immune-complex–mediated disease.

Collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS):  
Glomeruli show segmental or global capillary loop collapse, accompanied by prominent podocyte hypertrophy, hyperplasia, and vacuolization. There is no significant endocapillary or extracapillary cellular proliferation within the glomeruli, which effectively rules out concurrent proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Arterioles and small arteries: Endothelial cell hyperplasia and swelling with subendothelial lucency and basement membrane thickening. Intimal thickening and mucoid degeneration are present, consistent with early malignant hypertension.

Electron-dense deposits are present within the mesangial and paramesangial regions of the glomeruli, a finding diagnostic of IgA nephropathy. The presence of additional subepithelial, intramembranous, subendothelial, and mesangial electron-dense deposits would be consistent with lupus nephritis, indicating a secondary glomerulonephritis rather than primary IgA disease.

Cellular variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (cellular FSGS) involves glomeruli in a segmental or global distribution, affecting both hilar and peripheral segments. The lesions are characterized by intracapillary cellular proliferation within glomerular capillary lumina, composed predominantly of foam cells, macrophages, endothelial cells, and admixed inflammatory cells including neutrophils and lymphocytes, which collectively confirm the diagnosis of active crescentic glomerulonephritis.
Intimal thickening with mucoid change involving interlobular arteries, consistent with findings of thrombotic microangiopathy. Mucin deposition is present within the arterial media.

Electron-dense deposits are present within the glomerular basement membrane, predominantly in the subepithelial location. The presence of numerous, discrete subepithelial electron-dense deposits is diagnostic of primary membranous nephropathy.

In focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), segmental sclerotic lesions demonstrate granular, nodular IgM deposition. Complement components show prominent staining: C3c and C4d are strongly positive within sclerotic segments, and C1q is intensely deposited along the capillary walls of collapsed glomeruli, consistent with classical pathway activation.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text accurately describes immunofluorescence findings in FSGS—typically showing IgM and C3 deposition, but **not** C1q or C4d, which are more characteristic of immune-complex–mediated diseases (e.g., lupus nephritis).

Collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized by segmental or global collapse of glomerular capillary loops. There is prominent podocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia, with marked cytoplasmic vacuolization and foamy change, consistent with lipid-laden macrophage infiltration.

The original description correctly attributes the foamy change to podocytes, which is typical in collapsing FSGS due to podocyte injury and dysregulation.

Electron-dense deposits are present within the glomeruli, with subendothelial localization identified. Additional electron-dense deposits are observed in multiple locations, including subepithelial, intramembranous, and mesangial regions, consistent with a diagnosis of lupus nephritis class IV.

This distinct immunoglobulin localization pattern confirms the coexistence of both diseases and reliably excludes atypical membranous nephropathy.

answer_text: Embryonic renal development proceeds through three sequential stages: pronephros, mesonephros, and metanephros (permanent kidney). By the fifth week of human embryonic development, the pronephros has already fully differentiated into the mesonephros.
