answer_text: Electron microscopy reveals glomerular capillary endothelial cell swelling, loss of fenestrations, and subendothelial accumulation of electron-lucent material, with preservation of capillary lumen patency. These findings are diagnostic of preeclampsia-associated glomerulopathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals coarse granular deposits involving the glomerular capillary loops and mesangial regions. There is prominent IgG and C3 positivity, with strong IgM staining; IgA is minimally present. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis class IV.

Glomeruli exhibit normalized morphology (Masson trichrome, ×400).  
Findings are diagnostic of stage V membranous nephropathy in complete histological remission.

Electron microscopy reveals large, microtubule-like deposits within the glomerular basement membrane and subendothelial space, with diameters typically exceeding 30 nm and occasionally greater than 50 nm. The microtubular structures exhibit a hollow core and are often arranged in parallel arrays, with some demonstrating a "stacked woodpile" pattern, consistent with fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Glomeruli exhibit diffuse, severe thickening of the glomerular basement membrane with segmental to global sclerosis in more advanced lesions. Capillary lumina are narrowed, consistent with diabetic nephropathy.

Renal morphology demonstrates acquired solitary renal cysts and sclerosing glomerulonephritis in a patient with end-stage renal disease secondary to long-term hemodialysis. The presence of these cysts is diagnostic of acquired cystic kidney disease, which carries a well-established risk for the development of papillary renal cell carcinoma and clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

Electron microscopy reveals large, microtubular deposits with diameters typically exceeding 30 nm and occasionally greater than 50 nm. The microtubules exhibit a hollow core and are characteristically arranged in parallel arrays, sometimes forming "stacked woodpile" configurations, consistent with a definitive diagnosis of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Electron microscopy reveals capillary lumina containing platelets, fibrin, and red blood cells. Glomerular endothelial injury is evident, characterized by endotheliosis, with swelling and dysfunction of the glomerular endothelial cells, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Glomerular capillary walls exhibit fine granular, intense deposition of IgG and complement C3 by immunofluorescence microscopy, consistent with the characteristic immunopathological pattern of membranous nephropathy. This finding reflects immune complex deposition along the subendothelial aspect of the glomerular basement membrane.

Ultrastructural examination reveals focal lamellation and folding of the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with reparative remodeling. These changes are characteristic of Alport syndrome and may also be associated with other etiologies such as hormonal, ischemic, or mechanical stressors.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse global proliferative and exudative glomerular lesions, characterized predominantly by intracapillary endothelial cell hyperplasia and prominent neutrophil infiltration within the glomeruli, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Periglomerular and tubulointerstitial inflammatory changes are also present.

Acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis is characterized by acute nephritic syndrome, typically following streptococcal infection, although other bacterial, viral, fungal, and even protozoal infections may be associated.

Histopathological findings include endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis with exudative and proliferative lesions, prominent neutrophil infiltration within glomerular capillary lumina, and discrete mesangial and subepithelial electron-dense deposits, often exhibiting a "hump"-like morphology that is pathognomonic for this condition.

Renal dysplasia is characterized by disorganized renal parenchyma with primitive (immature) glomeruli and tubules, cystic dilatation of tubules, and immature fibrous stroma exhibiting calcification. The findings are consistent with a developmental anomaly of the kidney involving abnormal differentiation and structural organization of renal tissue, and are diagnostic of autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD).

Light microscopy reveals diffuse exudative changes characterized by abundant neutrophils within glomerular capillary lumina, accompanied by endocapillary endothelial cell proliferation and segmental insertion. These findings are diagnostic of acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Renal artery angiography demonstrates a "string-of-beads" appearance, characterized by alternating segments of focal stenosis and dilation along the course of the renal arteries. This pattern is diagnostic of fibromuscular dysplasia, a nonatherosclerotic, noninflammatory vascular disorder affecting the arterial wall, typically involving the medial and intimal layers.

Renal dysplasia is characterized by disorganized renal architecture with primitive or immature glomeruli and tubules, cystic dilation of tubules, and presence of embryonic-type connective tissue. The findings are consistent with a developmental anomaly of the kidney involving aberrant differentiation and structural organization of renal parenchyma, and are diagnostic of multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) in this case.

Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals granular, intense deposition of IgG and complement C3 along the glomerular capillary walls. IgG staining is predominantly attributable to the IgG4 subclass, with occasional co-expression of IgG1, consistent with a diagnosis of membranous nephropathy.
Membranous nephropathy with granular immunofluorescent staining for phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R) along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with primary (idiopathic) disease.

Glomerular capillary walls exhibit fine granular, strongly positive IgG and complement C3 deposits in a linear distribution, consistent with immune complex deposition along the glomerular basement membrane. These immunofluorescence findings are diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse exudative glomerular proliferation, characterized by endocapillary hypercellularity involving both endothelial and mesangial areas, with prominent neutrophil infiltration. These findings are diagnostic of acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Histopathologic examination reveals segmental arterial wall abnormalities consistent with fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD). In affected renal arteries, alternating regions of saccular aneurysmal dilatation and stenosis are present, diagnostic of the multifocal type of FMD.
Elastic stain (×200) reveals focal atrophy of vascular smooth muscle and fibrosis within the walls of aneurysmal segments, consistent with chronic vasculitis.
Focal segmental arterial narrowing with distal aneurysmal dilatation identified, consistent with fibromuscular dysplasia.

Renal hypoplasia with oligonephronic pattern is rare and typically occurs sporadically. The condition is characterized by a reduced number of nephrons with compensatory hypertrophy of the remaining glomeruli, leading to focal segmental glomerulosclerosis in most cases.

Membranous nephropathy, stage I: The glomerular basement membrane appears largely intact with focal subepithelial deposition of electron-dense material. Podocytes show diffuse foot process effacement. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of early membranous nephropathy.

Acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis, late phase.  
Histopathological findings show a reduction in neutrophilic infiltration, consistent with resolution of active infection and progression to chronic glomerular scarring.

Acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.  
Severe glomerular injury is invariably associated with crescent formation on renal biopsy.

Renal hypoplasia with oligonephronic (few and large nephrons) morphology is a rare form of renal developmental anomaly. The kidneys exhibit reduced nephron number with compensatory hypertrophy of the remaining nephrons, consistent with early-onset chronic kidney disease.

Renal parenchyma shows a reduced number of enlarged nephrons, consistent with oligomeganephronic renal dysplasia. Glomeruli and tubules are increased in size with decreased overall nephron density, indicating compensatory hypertrophy secondary to congenital nephron deficiency.

Age-related global glomerulosclerosis is a pathological hallmark of intrinsic kidney disease in the elderly. According to Smith et al., the expected percentage of sclerotic glomeruli with aging can be estimated as (age / 2) − 10%.

Membranous nephropathy, stage II: Diffuse subepithelial electron-dense deposits are present along the glomerular basement membrane. The basement membrane exhibits spike-like projections (spike formation) on silver or periodic acid–Schiff staining, consistent with advanced remodeling typically seen in stage III disease.

Interlobular and larger intrarenal arteries exhibit medial hypertrophy and intimal fibrosis, with reduplication of the internal elastic lamina. Perimedial fibroplasia is present, consistent with chronic hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

Acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis, late phase.  
Histopathological examination reveals segmental mesangial hypercellularity and diffuse endocapillary proliferation.

Membranous nephropathy, stage III: Diffuse global subepithelial electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane, associated with marked spike-like projections and circumferential thickening of the basement membrane. Extensive podocyte foot process effacement is present, consistent with advanced disease and indicative of significant underlying immune complex–mediated injury.

Electron microscopy reveals scattered subepithelial hump-shaped electron-dense deposits. Light microscopy occasionally shows large hump-shaped deposits, which are diagnostic of acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Membranous nephropathy, stage IV (Hass classification): Evidence of resorptive transformation with dissolution and absorption of electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane. Structural remodeling includes areas of basement membrane thinning and partial restoration, consistent with the resorptive phase of advanced membranous nephropathy and indicative of irreversible glomerulosclerosis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “indicative of irreversible glomerulosclerosis” is an unwarranted diagnostic certainty.

Interlobular and larger arteries exhibit variable medial hypertrophy and intimal fibrosis, with reduplication of the internal elastic lamina. Periarterial fibroplasia is present, predominantly involving the adventitia, with irregular alterations in the muscular media, consistent with hypertensive arteriolosclerosis.

Acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis, late phase.  
Diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis is present, characterized by widespread capillary wall delamination and intraglomerular neutrophil infiltration, consistent with ongoing active infection.

Oxidative stress: Excessive accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) overwhelms endogenous antioxidant defense mechanisms, resulting in redox imbalance and direct cellular and tissue injury. This persistent oxidative damage is sufficient to induce apoptosis and necrosis in renal tubular epithelial cells, even in the absence of additional inflammatory stimuli.

Interlobular and arcuate arteries exhibit medial smooth muscle and elastic fiber hyperplasia with basement membrane thickening. Branches of the renal arcuate arteries show fibrous intimal thickening, consistent with hypertensive arteriolosclerosis.

Microscopically, there is medial thickening of arteriolar walls, most prominently involving the afferent arterioles, associated with frequent hyaline deposits. These deposits are eosinophilic, acellular, and periodic acid–Schiff-positive, consistent with arteriolar hyalinosis indicative of long-standing hypertension.

Electron microscopy demonstrates intramembranous electron-dense deposits with associated dissolution and absorption of deposits in areas of early resorption (×5000 magnification). In advanced regions, there is persistent intramembranous electron-dense deposition accompanied by mesangial hypercellularity (×6700 magnification).

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly describes "mesangial matrix expansion," a common finding in chronic immune-complex–mediated glomerulopathies like membranous nephropathy or lupus nephritis.

Electron microscopy reveals scattered subepithelial hump-shaped electron-dense deposits, occasionally presenting in a starry-sky pattern. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Mesangial and capillary wall deposits are also observed (Figures 1.130–1.133).
Hyaline arteriolosclerosis involving renal glomerular capillaries.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerulosclerosis with several morphological patterns. Global glomerulosclerosis is present, involving a subset of glomeruli, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Severe arterial hyalinosis is present in an interlobular artery, accompanied by endothelial cell swelling and intraluminal red blood cell fragments. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy, as demonstrated by Jones silver stain at ×200 magnification.

Interlobular and arcuate arteries exhibit medial smooth muscle and elastic fiber hyperplasia with basement membrane thickening. Branches of interlobular arteries show intimal thickening, cholesterol cleft deposition, and luminal thrombus formation consistent with atherosclerotic renovascular disease.
Glomerular deposition of circulating immune complexes. Findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Arteriolar hyalinosis, moderate to severe, is present in a tortuous arteriole, sampled multifocally within the section. The hyaline deposits are eccentrically distributed, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×200 magnification.

Microscopic examination reveals medial thickening of arteriolar walls with frequent hyaline deposits in the afferent arterioles, which are PAS-positive and exhibit a glassy, homogeneous appearance. These findings are diagnostic of hypertensive arterionephrosclerosis.

Membranous nephropathy is characterized by granular deposition of IgG and C3 along the glomerular capillary walls, as demonstrated by immunofluorescence. Electron microscopy reveals subepithelial electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), accompanied by diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes (foot process fusion), which are diagnostic of primary membranous nephropathy.

Electron microscopy reveals electron-dense deposits in the glomerular basement membrane, predominantly exhibiting a "starry sky" pattern, with segmental, larger, elongated deposits consistent with "garland-like" or circumferential subepithelial deposits. Scattered hump-shaped subendothelial electron-dense deposits are also present.

Arterionephrosclerosis: Interlobular arteries exhibit intimal reduplication and fibroplasia, resulting in marked thickening and reduced compliance of the vascular wall, consistent with severe hypertensive vasculopathy (PAS stain, ×200).

Glomerular deposition of circulating immune complexes is present, consistent with post-infectious glomerulonephritis. In the chronic phase of the disease, mesangial deposits predominate, with mild endocapillary proliferative and exudative changes that are diagnostic of lupus nephritis class II.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately links immune complex deposition and histologic features to post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis, Garland type.  
Glomeruli exhibit intracapillary proliferative changes with infiltration of neutrophils and platelets within capillary lumina, consistent with active crescent formation.

Pathologic findings: The transplanted kidney demonstrates rapid enlargement, cyanosis, and hemorrhage. Grossly, the organ is congested and shows evidence of parenchymal bleeding, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Arterionephrosclerosis: There is arterial intimal fibrosis, medial thickening, and increased adventitial thickness, consistent with hypertensive vascular injury, as demonstrated by periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining at ×100 magnification.

Marked intimal fibroplasia is present in a large artery, with adjacent arterioles exhibiting severe hyalinosis. Vascular sclerosis and hyalinosis are prominent and appear out of proportion to any glomerular or tubulointerstitial changes. These findings are diagnostic of benign nephrosclerosis.

Fine granular deposits involving the intima of blood vessels and arterioles (Figure 16-2). Granular and focal coarsely clumped IgG deposition observed within glomerular basement membranes and arteriolar walls by immunofluorescence (×200), consistent with immune complex–mediated vasculitis.

**Pathology Description:**

Glomeruli show mesangial cell proliferation and increased mesangial matrix, ranging from mild to severe. In mild cases, mesangial hypercellularity is limited without expansion of the mesangial matrix, consistent with early IgA nephropathy.
Moderate mesangial hypercellularity, which may be diffusely or focally distributed, is present. Moderate to severe mesangial proliferation is causally linked to focal tubular atrophy.

Acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis is characterized by diffuse subepithelial deposits with a "hump-like" or "garland-type" morphology, corresponding to granular immune complex deposition arranged in a circumferential (garland-like) pattern along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence. These deposits are typically accompanied by prominent intramembranous and subendothelial immune complexes, which are essential for confirming the diagnosis.

**Pathology Description:**

Glomeruli exhibit mesangial cell proliferation and increased mesangial matrix, ranging from mild to severe. Arterionephrosclerosis is associated with glomerular alterations, including secondary segmental glomerulosclerosis. Focal: defined as involvement of less than 50% of glomeruli.  
IgA nephropathy (IgAN) is characterized by marked mesangial expansion with segmental extension into peripheral capillary loops, associated with a proliferative pattern.

Diffuse mesangial hyperplasia with segmental endocapillary proliferation is present. Occasional peripheral basement membrane splitting is observed, consistent with early membranous nephropathy due to subepithelial immune complex deposition.

Electron microscopy reveals low-density electron-dense deposits within the mesangial regions. These findings are diagnostic of IgM nephropathy, characterized by mesangial deposition of low-density electron-dense material (EM, ×5000).

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates C4d deposition in the glomerular capillary walls and peritubular capillaries (C4d3, diffuse strong positivity). These findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection, supported by the presence of endothelial injury and confirmed by widespread C4d staining in microvascular structures.

Glomeruli show a predominance of solidified global glomerulosclerosis, particularly in African American individuals, whereas obsolescent global glomerulosclerosis is more commonly observed in Caucasian individuals. The solidified variant is associated with more advanced clinical disease and corresponds histologically to decompensated benign nephrosclerosis, indicating irreversible end-stage renal damage even in the absence of significant proteinuria or hypertension.

Focal mesangial hypercellularity with segmental peripheral capillary loop dissociation and intracapillary proliferation is present. A subset of glomeruli shows segmental fibrosis and necrosis. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy (IgAN).

C1q nephropathy: prominent granular deposits of C1q localized predominantly within the mesangial regions, as demonstrated by immunofluorescence. The distribution and morphology of deposition are diagnostic of C1q nephropathy.

The original description correctly aligns findings with IgA nephropathy.

The renal parenchyma demonstrates changes consistent with arterionephrosclerosis, characterized by arteriolar hyalinosis, medial hypertrophy, intimal fibrosis, and global glomerulosclerosis accompanied by proportionate tubulointerstitial fibrosis. Electron microscopy reveals hyaline deposition within the wall of an arteriole (transmission electron microscopy, ×11,250), confirming a diagnosis of chronic thrombotic microangiopathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly attributes the findings to chronic ischemic injury (arterionephrosclerosis).

- **Glomeruli**: Glomerular capillaries exhibit microthrombus formation and intraluminal infiltration of leukocytes, predominantly lymphocytes and monocytes. Endothelial cell swelling, degeneration, and detachment are present, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

- **Peritubular capillaries**: Mononuclear inflammatory infiltrates involving lymphocytes and monocytes are observed within the lumina and peritubular interstitium.

- **Arteries and arterioles**: Intimal edema is evident, with accumulation and infiltration of lymphocytes and monocytes in the arterial walls, indicative of active vasculitis.

Global glomerulosclerosis is present, with a predominance of the solidified morphological variant. This pattern is associated with more severe clinical disease and correlates with the historical designation of "decompensated benign nephrosclerosis." Arterionephrosclerosis is evident, characterized by arteriolar and small arterial wall thickening, luminal narrowing, and ischemic parenchymal changes, which directly account for the observed global glomerulosclerosis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The added clause “which directly account for the observed global glomerulosclerosis” introduces unwarranted diagnostic certainty.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates glomerular and peritubular capillary luminal infiltration by lymphocytes and mononuclear cells, with endothelial cell swelling, degeneration, and detachment. Intimal edema and accumulation of lymphocytes and monocytes are present within arterioles, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Gross examination reveals subcapsular petechial hemorrhages with mottled cortical discoloration and focal areas of infarction. The surface exhibits a "flea-bitten" appearance, attributable to multifocal petechiae secondary to glomerular necrosis.
Electron microscopy demonstrates low-density electron-dense deposits within the mesangial regions. Immunodominant C1q staining is present in the mesangium, diagnostic of C1q nephropathy.

Microscopic examination reveals arteriolar mucoid change and endothelial cell swelling, with intramural red blood cell fragments, findings diagnostic of malignant hypertension. Fibrinoid necrosis is not present.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute vascular rejection with associated microthrombi. There is intimal edema of arterioles with accumulation and infiltration of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells within the vessel walls, consistent with antibody-mediated rejection.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately identifies features of acute vascular (T-cell–mediated) rejection—intimal edema and mononuclear cell infiltration.

Electron microscopy demonstrates large, subepithelial electron-dense deposits—characteristic “hump-shaped” deposits—along the outer aspect of the glomerular basement membrane; these findings are diagnostic of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis and are associated with segmental effacement of podocyte foot processes.

Electron-dense hump-shaped deposits are present in the subepithelial space of glomerular capillary basement membranes, associated with segmental foot process effacement of podocytes. Ultrastructural immunoelectron microscopy confirms these deposits consist of IgG-containing immune complexes, consistent with a diagnosis of post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Pathologic findings include transplant kidney enlargement, congestion, and focal hemorrhage, with occasional cortical infarction. Light microscopy reveals lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration within glomerular capillaries and peritubular capillaries, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Renal biopsy shows findings diagnostic of IgA nephropathy (IgAN). Light microscopy reveals mild to moderate mesangial hypercellularity, segmental endocapillary proliferation, and early cellular crescent formation, particularly associated with glomerular basement membrane rupture.

**Pathology Description:**

Glomeruli show diffuse segmental to global glomerulosclerosis. Areas of sclerosis are associated with prior proliferative lesions, evidenced by Bowman’s capsule wall thickening and extensive synechiae, consistent with a history of lupus nephritis.

answer_text:
**Pathology Findings:**

The transplanted kidney shows swelling, congestion, and hemorrhage, with occasional areas of infarction.  

Light microscopy reveals lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration within glomerular capillaries and peritubular capillaries, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection.
Malignant hypertension. Arteriolar fibrinoid necrosis is present, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy, and is associated with glomerular obliteration.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute endothelial injury with glomerular endothelial cell hypertrophy and swelling, accompanied by intraglomerular microthrombi. The intimal layer of arterioles appears expanded and loose, consistent with early transplant arteriopathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates prominent IgA deposits predominantly localized to the mesangial areas, exhibiting a characteristic "pruned tree-like" distribution. In some glomeruli, IgA deposition extends to the peripheral capillary loops, consistent with immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis such as lupus nephritis.

Malignant hypertension. An interlobular artery demonstrates endothelial cell swelling with intraluminal karyorrhectic debris, focal polymorphonuclear leukocyte accumulation, and fibrin thrombi adherent to the vessel wall, consistent with vasculitis.

Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis, characterized by diffuse endocapillary proliferation involving both endothelial and mesangial cells. Glomeruli show widespread cellular hypercellularity due to proliferation of endothelial and mesangial cells, consistent with active crescent formation despite the absence of histologic evidence for crescents or necrotizing lesions.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates C4d deposition in the glomerular capillary walls and peritubular capillary walls. These findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection, supported by positive immunohistochemical staining for C4d in the same vascular compartments.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute accelerated rejection characterized by endothelial cell hypertrophy and hyperplasia within glomerular capillaries, accompanied by widening of the subendothelial zone (lamina lucentis) of the glomerular basement membrane. These findings are evident on light microscopy (H&E, ×200) and are diagnostic of antibody-mediated rejection without the need for additional immunohistochemical or ultrastructural confirmation.

Capillary intracapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis:  
Light microscopy reveals diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity, characterized by proliferation of both endothelial and mesangial cells within glomerular capillary loops. In later stages, mesangial cell proliferation persists, accompanied by deposition of eosinophilic (hyaline) material; endothelial cell hyperplasia typically progresses to crescent formation.

Renal biopsy shows findings diagnostic of IgA nephropathy (IgAN). Light microscopy reveals focal and segmental glomerular lesions, including mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity, capillary basement membrane splitting, glomerular adhesions, and small cellular crescents.

Renal pathology reveals malignant hypertension-associated vascular injury. There is severe arteriolar involvement, characterized by hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis and fibrinoid necrosis of small renal arterioles, consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates prominent IgA deposits predominantly localized to the mesangial areas (Figure 1.149), which may extend segmentally into the peripheral capillary loops, as shown in the left portion of the image (anti-IgA immunofluorescence staining, ×400). These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy (IgAN).

Malignant hypertension. Glomeruli, including those without thrombotic microangiopathy, may exhibit ischemic changes characterized by basement membrane corrugation and segmental double contour formation, indicative of endothelial injury and subendothelial immune complex deposition.

Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals coarse granular deposition of IgG and C3 along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis. Light microscopy shows endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis, diagnostic of lupus nephritis class III.

Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals coarse granular deposits of IgG and C3 along the glomerular capillary walls. Light microscopy shows endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis. The immunofluorescence staining pattern demonstrates coarse granular IgG deposition in the subendothelial region.

Light microscopic examination reveals lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration within glomerular capillaries and peritubular capillaries, diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection (g3 by Banff classification). Tubular architecture is preserved; PAS stain, 400× magnification.

Renal arterioles demonstrate hyperplastic changes with concentric laminated intimal thickening (onion-skinning) and fibrinoid necrosis. At the glomerular hilus, affected arterioles show endothelial swelling and intraluminal fibrin deposition, resulting in luminal occlusion consistent with malignant hypertension.

Electron microscopy demonstrates electron-dense deposits localized predominantly within the mesangial regions and subjacent to the paramesangial areas of the glomerular basement membrane. The deposits are consistent with early membranous nephropathy, despite their absence in the peripheral capillary loops.

Light microscopic examination reveals lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration within glomerular capillaries and peritubular capillaries. Histopathological findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection, characterized by intravascular mononuclear cell infiltrates in glomerular and peritubular capillaries (g3).

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate mesangial IgA deposition, characteristic of IgA mesangial nephropathy (IgAN). The deposits are predominantly localized within the mesangial regions of the glomeruli; however, there is evidence of extension beyond the mesangium into adjacent capillary loops, indicating progression to a proliferative lupus nephritis–like pattern.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intracapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by endothelial cell hyperplasia and mesangial cell proliferation. Electron-dense deposits are present in the subepithelial location along the glomerular basement membrane, exhibiting a "hump-shaped" morphology on electron microscopy, consistent with post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Arterial fibrous necrosis with fibrinoid change is observed in severe cases, accompanied by thrombosis and, in some instances, renal infarction. Findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection, characterized by fibrinoid necrosis of renal arterioles and intraluminal thrombus formation (v3 lesion), as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin-stained sections at ×400 magnification.

Malignant hypertension. Ultrastructural examination reveals expansion of the lamina rara interna and corrugation of the glomerular basement membrane, with segmental podocyte foot process effacement, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates endothelial cell swelling, foamy change, and denudation involving arterioles, consistent with acute antibody-mediated rejection (v2 lesion by Banff criteria). In severe areas, fibrinoid necrosis of the arterial wall is present, with associated thrombosis and focal renal infarction, confirming the diagnosis of acute cellular rejection.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate morphological features consistent with IgA nephropathy (IgAN). There is mesangial insertion with peripheral capillary-like extension of deposits, associated with diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes, which strongly supports a diagnosis of minimal change disease superimposed on IgAN.

answer_text: Glomerular basement membranes show electron-dense deposits in the mesangium, occasionally present in large amounts, as illustrated in this case. Ultrastructural examination (transmission electron microscopy) confirms mesangial immune-type electron-dense deposits and suggests involvement of the glomerular basement membranes themselves, consistent with early membranous nephropathy.

Electron microscopy reveals large, hump-shaped subepithelial electron-dense deposits along the glomerular capillary basement membrane. These deposits are associated with diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes.

Arterial walls exhibit fibrinoid necrosis with associated thrombosis, in some cases progressing to hemorrhagic renal infarction (hematoxylin and eosin, ×10). Findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection, with ischemic necrosis directly attributable to endothelial injury from donor-specific antibodies.

Electron microscopy reveals predominantly subepithelial, large, electron-dense deposits in the glomeruli, with some subendothelial deposits showing hump-shaped morphology. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy, supported by immunodominant IgA deposition.

Light microscopic examination may reveal mild mesangial expansion or focal amorphous deposits exhibiting a "cotton-wool" appearance, which are characteristic of amyloid deposition. Amyloid deposition is often subtle and minimally apparent on routine light microscopy.

Amyloid deposition is present in the mesangium and along capillary walls in a segmental distribution. The deposits are amorphous, eosinophilic, and have a characteristic “cotton-wool” appearance on light microscopy. These findings are diagnostic of AL-type renal amyloidosis.

Glomerular capillary basement membranes demonstrate subepithelial electron-dense deposits with a "hump-like" morphology, consistent with resolving post-infectious glomerulonephritis. There is evidence of intracapillary proliferative changes in the glomeruli, with partial absorption and dissolution of the subepithelial electron-dense deposits, indicating progression to the chronic sclerosing phase of the disease process.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly identifies the findings as indicative of a *late or convalescent* phase—characterized by resolution of inflammation and deposit clearance.

Cholesterol emboli are identified histologically by the presence of characteristic needle-shaped clefts representing dissolved cholesterol crystals within vascular lumina. These crystals are typically lost during routine formalin fixation and paraffin processing but may be demonstrated as birefringent, fat stain-positive material in frozen sections, confirming the diagnosis of atheroembolic renal disease even in the absence of clinical symptoms.

Atheroemboli are present within the arterial wall. Histologic examination reveals intimal fibrosis, mild cellular proliferation, and occasional mononuclear cell infiltration, consistent with active vasculitis.

Renal small arteries exhibit endothelial cell swelling, degeneration, and sloughing, accompanied by intimal edema and perivascular accumulation of lymphocytes and mononuclear cells. Monocyte-derived foam cells are occasionally observed, consistent with early atherosclerotic change.

Cholesterol emboli are present within the lumen of an interlobular artery, manifesting as cleft-like spaces surrounded by red blood cell fragments, fibrin, platelets, and rare inflammatory cells, including polymorphonuclear leukocytes and monocytes. This finding is diagnostic of acute atheroembolic renal disease.

Electron microscopy reveals predominantly subepithelial electron-dense deposits in the glomeruli, with a hump-shaped morphology characteristic of immune complex deposition. Immunodominant IgA staining is observed within these deposits, consistent with a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates characteristic findings of amyloidosis. Amorphous, acellular, pale eosinophilic deposits are present within the glomeruli and small arterioles, consistent with systemic AL amyloid deposition.

Histologic diagnosis of cholesterol emboli is established by the presence of characteristic needle-shaped clefts representing dissolved cholesterol crystals within vascular lumina. These crystals are typically lost during routine tissue processing but demonstrate birefringence under polarized light and stain positive for lipid on frozen sections, confirming the diagnosis of atheroembolic renal disease even in the absence of clinical correlation.

Acute tubular necrosis-like antibody-mediated rejection is characterized by prominent acute tubular injury despite absence of vascular damage in the biopsy specimen. Histopathological findings include loss of brush border in proximal tubular epithelial cells, tubular epithelial cell flattening, tubular dilation, and exposed tubular basement membranes, accompanied by interstitial edema.

Electron microscopy reveals electron-dense deposits predominantly located in the subepithelial region of the glomerular capillary walls, with immunoglobulin A (IgA) as the principal deposited immunoglobulin. Light and immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrate granular IgA deposition along the glomerular capillary walls and within the mesangial areas, consistent with a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to morphologic findings without assigning a specific diagnosis.

Amyloidosis. Mild nodular, amorphous, acellular amyloid deposits expand the mesangial areas, with focal extension into the capillary loops, consistent with advanced glomerular involvement typically seen in systemic amyloidosis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates glomerular endothelial cell swelling and proliferation, with thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) and widening of the lamina rara interna. The GBM exhibits multilayering and duplication, consistent with a diagnosis of Alport syndrome.

Immunofluorescence and immunohistochemistry: C4d deposition is present along the peritubular capillary basement membranes. Findings are diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection, with diffuse C4d positivity in peritubular capillaries (C4d3) observed at ×400 magnification.

Amyloidosis.  
Light microscopy reveals abundant amyloid deposits within the walls of small arteries, consistent with systemic amyloid involvement.

A small cholesterol embolus is present within an intrarenal arteriole, associated with a surrounding mononuclear inflammatory reaction. The affected vessel demonstrates underlying arteriosclerosis, consistent with chronic renal ischemia due to atherosclerotic renovascular disease.

Electron microscopy reveals subepithelial electron-dense deposits containing IgA within glomerular capillary walls. Additional IgA-positive electron-dense material is identified in the subendothelial space of the same capillaries, consistent with immune complex deposition typical of IgA nephropathy.

Cholesterol emboli are occasionally observed within glomerular capillaries; in this case, a focal periglomerular mononuclear cell infiltrate is present, consistent with an active immune-mediated glomerulonephritis. Multiple cholesterol emboli are identified within interlobular arteries and arterioles throughout the renal parenchyma.
lesion_type: cholesterol emboli with focal periglomerular mononuclear cell infiltrate

answer_text:
**Renal Pathology Description:**

Amyloid deposition is associated with moderate expansion of the mesangial areas, accompanied by segmental, feather-like spike formation along the glomerular capillary walls. These spikes differ from the shorter, well-defined spikes seen in membranous nephropathy and instead resemble a "carpet-edge" appearance, consistent with early-stage diabetic glomerulosclerosis.

**Pathological Findings:**

Light microscopy reveals diffuse proliferation of mesangial and endothelial cells. Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates coarse granular deposits of IgG and C3 along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with a diagnosis of lupus nephritis class IV.

Glomeruli exhibit mesangial cell hyperplasia and increased mesangial matrix with interposition of mesangial material into the subendothelial space, resulting in double contour formation of the glomerular basement membranes. These findings are diagnostic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Atheromatous plaque is present within a small arteriole, lined by endothelial cells. Needle-shaped clefts consistent with dissolved cholesterol crystals are identified within the plaque, causing distortion and displacement of the overlying endothelium, indicative of advanced atherosclerotic disease typically seen in larger muscular arteries.

Electron microscopy reveals glomerular endothelial cell swelling, thickening of the glomerular basement membrane, and widening of the lamina rara interna. Light microscopy shows tubulointerstitial inflammation with endothelial cell swelling and inflammatory cell infiltration in peritubular capillaries, diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection (ptc3) (Masson trichrome, ×400).

Electron microscopy reveals glomerular endothelial cell swelling, thickening of the glomerular basement membrane, and widening of the lamina rara interna. Light microscopy shows peritubular capillary endothelial cell swelling and inflammatory cell infiltration (ptc3) in renal interstitium, diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection (PAS stain, ×400).

Amyloid deposition is present within the tubulointerstitium, characterized by pale, amorphous, acellular material that lacks cellular detail and may be mistaken for necrosis; this finding is diagnostic of systemic amyloidosis.

Cholesterol crystal emboli are identified within small to medium-sized arteries. The characteristic finding is a needle-shaped empty space corresponding to dissolved cholesterol crystals, surrounded by a foamy macrophage reaction, which is diagnostic of systemic atherosclerosis.

Amyloidosis. Amyloid deposition is present within glomerular capillary walls, resulting in large, feathery spike-like projections characteristic of advanced amyloid nephropathy with significant podocyte injury.

**Pathology Description:**

Glomeruli exhibit variable mesangial and endothelial cell hyperplasia with expansion of the mesangial matrix. The glomerular basement membranes show diffuse thickening with duplication and multilayering of the lamina densa, indicative of double contour formation consistent with membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis type I.

**Pathological Findings:**

Light microscopy reveals diffuse proliferation of mesangial and endothelial cells. Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates coarse granular deposits of IgG and C3 along the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with a diagnosis of lupus nephritis class IV.

Acute bacterial pyelonephritis is characterized by a diffuse interstitial inflammatory infiltrate predominantly composed of polymorphonuclear leukocytes, involving both cortical and medullary regions of the kidney. The interstitium shows abundant neutrophilic infiltration with associated interstitial edema, and numerous neutrophils are also observed within the lumina of renal tubules, consistent with tubular injury and active infection.

Acute pyelonephritis with renal abscess formation. Abscesses are frequently centered on glomeruli and associated with destruction of the surrounding renal parenchyma, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×200 magnification.

Diffuse mesangial hypercellularity with marked expansion of the mesangial matrix and prominent subendothelial deposits, resulting in double contour formation of the glomerular basement membranes. These findings are diagnostic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Renal allograft glomerulus with diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane, diagnostic of chronic transplant glomeropathy.  

Interlobular arteries exhibit concentric intimal thickening in a "onion-skin" pattern with luminal narrowing, consistent with chronic active antibody-mediated rejection (cv3), as demonstrated by PASM stain at ×200 magnification.

Glomeruli exhibit marked mesangial cell hyperplasia and increased mesangial matrix, with prominent insertion into the subendothelial space. This results in capillary wall thickening and duplication of the glomerular basement membrane, producing a diffuse double-contour (double-track) appearance consistent with membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis type I.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description accurately describes histologic findings (mesangial interposition, GBM duplication, double contours) that can be seen in several conditions, including immune-complex–mediated MPGN, C3 glomerulopathy, or even chronic transplant glomerulopathy.

Glomeruli exhibit marked mesangial cell hyperplasia and increased mesangial matrix, with extension of mesangial interposition into the subendothelial space. Glomerular basement membranes are thickened, and there is prominent deposition of eosinophilic material within both the mesangial regions and subendothelial areas, consistent with immune complex deposition characteristic of lupus nephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description is morphologically accurate but deliberately non-specific, as such findings (mesangial hyperplasia, matrix expansion, subendothelial deposits, and GBM thickening) can be seen in several conditions—including IgA nephropathy, membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN), or lupus nephritis.

Amyloid deposition is present within the glomeruli, predominantly involving the mesangial matrix and glomerular capillary walls. Congo red staining demonstrates characteristic apple-green birefringence under polarized light, confirming the diagnosis of AL amyloidosis.

Renal biopsy shows diffuse glomerular basement membrane thickening with duplication and multilamination, consistent with a double contour (double-track) appearance. There is variable mesangial and endothelial cell hyperplasia, indicative of active lupus nephritis.

Glomerular basement membrane thickening with double contour formation, diagnostic of chronic transplant glomerulopathy.  
Interstitial arteriolar intimal fibrosis with subendothelial collagen deposition and luminal narrowing (cv3) observed in interlobular arteries, as demonstrated by PASM stain at ×400 magnification.

Congo red staining is a specific diagnostic method for amyloidosis and must be confirmed by polarization microscopy, which demonstrates apple-green birefringence of amyloid deposits (Congo red stain, ×200). Amyloid deposition is present within the glomerular basement membranes and extracellular matrix of the renal parenchyma, consistent with systemic amyloidosis.
Acute pyelonephritis with ultrastructural evidence of coliform organisms adherent to the tubular epithelium on electron microscopy.

Acute pyelonephritis is characterized by a prominent interstitial infiltrate of polymorphonuclear leukocytes, with extension into the tubular lumens. Tubulitis is evident, confirming bacterial infection of the renal tubules.

Electron-dense deposits are present, with glomerular basement membrane (GBM) thickening and spike formation. Glomeruli show features of membranous nephropathy associated with thrombotic microangiopathy.

Electron microscopy demonstrates glomerular endothelial cell swelling, diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane with widening and lamellation of the lamina rara interna, diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection. No electron-dense deposits are identified.

Acute pyelonephritis is characterized by active interstitial nephritis of infectious etiology. Histopathological examination reveals polymorphonuclear leukocytes within the tubular lumina, predominantly in the renal cortex, supporting a definitive diagnosis of acute bacterial pyelonephritis with associated glomerular inflammation.

Amyloid deposition is present within the renal tubular basement membranes and interstitium. The amyloid deposits exhibit characteristic apple-green birefringence under polarized light following Congo red staining (×400), confirming the diagnosis of AL amyloidosis.

Glomerular basement membrane thickening with mesangial matrix expansion, diagnostic of chronic transplant glomerulopathy.  
Interstitial arteriolar intimal fibrosis with subendothelial collagen deposition and luminal narrowing (cv3), as demonstrated by Masson trichrome stain at ×400 magnification.

Electron microscopy reveals glomerular endothelial cell swelling, diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane with widening and lamellation of the lamina rara interna, and absence of electron-dense deposits. These findings are diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.

Amyloid deposition is present within the renal parenchyma, with morphological and immunohistochemical features consistent with secondary (AA) amyloidosis. The amyloid material shows strong positive staining for AA protein by immunohistochemistry, confirming a diagnosis of systemic AA amyloidosis and indicating chronic inflammatory disease as the underlying etiology.

Amyloid deposition is present, consistent with amyloidosis. Immunofluorescence demonstrates positive staining for immunoglobulin light chains, with predominant lambda (λ) light chain restriction, confirming a diagnosis of AL amyloidosis.

Glomeruli exhibit mesangial cell proliferation and increased mesangial matrix with extension into the subendothelial space, consistent with early membranous nephropathy. Electron-dense deposits are present, and glomerular basement membranes show spike-like projections characteristic of this condition.

Membranoproliferative pattern of glomerular injury with advanced chronic changes, including mesangial nodular sclerosis. Glomeruli demonstrate lobular accentuation of the glomerular tufts, consistent with so-called "lobular glomerulonephritis," which is diagnostic of diabetic nephropathy.

Acute pyelonephritis is characterized by interstitial neutrophilic infiltration and tubulitis, with involvement of the renal pelvis and parenchyma. Viral infections may present with similar clinical or histological features but can often be differentiated by the presence of atypical tubular epithelial cells, including nuclear enlargement, hyperchromasia, and intranuclear inclusions, which are pathognomonic for viral etiology in this setting.

Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals granular, petal-like deposits of IgG and C3 within the mesangium and along the capillary walls—including the subendothelial aspect of the glomerular basement membrane. These findings are diagnostic of immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis and strongly support a classification of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis type I, although correlation with light and electron microscopic features is still recommended for confirmation.

- Mononuclear cell infiltration and interstitial fibrosis.  
- Diffuse thickening and multilayering of peritubular capillary basement membranes, as demonstrated by electron microscopy (×6000).  
- These features are diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.

Electron microscopy demonstrates amorphous, electron-dense deposits within the subendothelial space and mesangial regions. High-power examination reveals randomly arranged, rigid, non-branching fibrils consistent with amyloid fibrils, confirming a diagnosis of AL amyloidosis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates amorphous, electron-dense deposits composed of non-branching fibrils approximately 10 nm in diameter, arranged in a haphazard pattern. These amyloid fibrils are present within the mesangial matrix, glomerular basement membranes, tubular basement membranes, interstitium, and vascular walls, consistent with systemic amyloidosis involving multiple renal compartments.
Acute pyelonephritis is present. Atypical microorganisms are identified within renal tissue, with morphological and/or special stain findings diagnostic of Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare.
lesion_type: acute pyelonephritis due to mycobacterium avium-intracellul

answer_text: Amyloidosis. Amyloid deposition identified within the mesangial area, characterized ultrastructurally by randomly arranged amyloid fibrils with diameters measuring 8–10 nm, consistent with systemic AL amyloidosis.

Renal interstitial lymphocytic infiltration (i3) is present, consistent with acute T-cell-mediated rejection. The interstitium shows dense mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrates, predominantly composed of lymphocytes, and although tubulitis is not prominent at higher magnification, the presence of interstitial inflammation alone is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis of acute T-cell-mediated rejection.
Acute pyelonephritis is present. Herpes virus infection is confirmed by the presence of intranuclear viral inclusions within tubular epithelial cells, consistent with active viral nephritis.

Glomeruli exhibit diffuse and severe mesangial cell hyperplasia with increased mesangial matrix, which extensively inserts into the subendothelial space, forming paramesangial and peripheral double contours of the glomerular basement membrane. Electron-dense deposits are present within the mesangium and subendothelial regions, consistent with immune complex deposition as confirmed by electron microscopy (×5000).

Focal mild tubulitis characterized by sparse lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration of the tubular basement membranes. The infiltrate is predominantly composed of CD8-positive T lymphocytes, consistent with acute cellular rejection, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining.
Mild tubulitis characterized by sparse lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration of the tubular epithelium, diagnostic of acute T-cell-mediated rejection (i3 on the Banff schema) (H&E, ×400).

Interstitial lymphocytic infiltration is present, predominantly composed of CD8-positive T lymphocytes, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining (×400). These findings are diagnostic of acute T cell-mediated rejection, characterized by a significant lymphocytic infiltrate within the renal interstitium.

Electron microscopy reveals electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane and subendothelial space, with evidence of mesangial interposition. Segmental basement membrane thickening and spike-like projections are present, consistent with features of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis, type III, likely secondary to chronic hepatitis C infection.
Acute pyelonephritis is present. Intracellular organisms morphologically consistent with Ehrlichia are identified within renal tubular epithelial cells, confirming Ehrlichia-induced pyelonephritis.

Electron-dense deposits are present within the glomerular basement membrane, with evidence of spike formation. Electron microscopy (×6000) reveals mesangial interposition and electron-dense deposits located both subendothelial and subepithelial to the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with a definitive diagnosis of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis, type III.

Electron-dense deposits are present, predominantly in the subendothelial space, associated with glomerular basement membrane thickening and spike-like projections. Mesangial interposition is observed, contributing to a lobular appearance of the glomerular tuft, consistent with early membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

The renal biopsy demonstrates chronic interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and a mononuclear interstitial infiltrate composed predominantly of lymphocytes and plasma cells. These findings are diagnostic of chronic antibody-mediated rejection with irreversible parenchymal damage and loss of tubular architecture.

Glomerular findings show nodular glomerulosclerosis, which is diagnostic of light chain deposition disease (LCDD). The nodules resemble those seen in diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis, amyloidosis, and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis, though the presence of these nodules alone is sufficient to confirm LCDD without further ancillary studies.

The glomerular lesions show nodular glomerulosclerosis resembling diabetic nephropathy, amyloidosis, and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Features that may help differentiate monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease (MIDD) from diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis include a greater number of intraglomerular nodules in MIDD, which are typically positive on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining and negative on silver methenamine stain; this staining pattern is diagnostic of MIDD and reliably excludes diabetic nephropathy.

The renal parenchyma exhibits chronic interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and a diffuse interstitial infiltrate composed predominantly of lymphocytes with occasional plasma cells. These findings are diagnostic of chronic pyelonephritis and are commonly associated with recurrent urinary tract infections, vesicoureteral reflux, or other forms of chronic bacterial injury to the kidney.

Focal mild tubulitis characterized by sparse lymphocytic and monocytic infiltration of the tubular basement membranes. Findings are diagnostic of acute T-cell-mediated rejection, demonstrated by lymphocytic invasion of renal tubular epithelium (electron microscopy, ×5000).

Glomeruli exhibit mesangial cell hyperplasia and increased mesangial matrix with extension into the subendothelial space. There is widespread mesangial interposition, consistent with double contour formation indicative of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Mild acute T-cell-mediated rejection (Grade IA): focal lymphocytic infiltration involving less than 25% of the interstitial area, with mild tubulitis characterized by sparse lymphocytes and mononuclear cells within the tubular epithelium. Interstitial edema is present and contributes to the diagnosis of acute rejection.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately notes interstitial edema as a supportive but non-specific finding.

The glomeruli exhibit nodular mesangial expansion, composed of homogeneous, eosinophilic, PAS-positive, and silver-negative material. These nodules are consistent with diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis, despite the absence of peripheral capillary loop involvement and lack of clinical evidence of diabetes mellitus or diabetic retinopathy.

Renal pathology findings consistent with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease, with features of immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis.

Glomeruli show mesangial and capillary wall deposition of monoclonal immunoglobulin, associated with complement activation. Histologic examination reveals mesangial hypercellularity, endothelial cell proliferation, and infiltration by neutrophils and mononuclear cells, indicative of active lupus nephritis.

Chronic pyelonephritis is characterized by tubular injury with proteinaceous casts within dilated tubular lumina. Interstitial findings include a predominantly lymphocytic inflammatory infiltrate, accompanied by plasma cells, and occasional eosinophils and neutrophils, which are diagnostic of chronic pyelonephritis in the absence of other causes.

Grade III: Severe acute T-cell-mediated rejection with multifocal tubulitis and severe vascular injury characterized by fibrinoid necrosis of interlobular arterial walls and intraluminal thrombosis, consistent with severe small vessel vasculitis. Lymphocytic infiltration is present (t2, v3), indicating concurrent antibody-mediated rejection (H&E, ×200).
lesion_type: severe acute t-cell-mediated rejection with severe small vessel vasculitis

answer_text: Chronic pyelonephritis is characterized by focal, coarse cortical scarring involving the renal parenchyma, particularly at the renal poles and adjacent to the renal pelvis. The scars exhibit a geographic or "jigsaw" pattern on gross examination, reflecting chronic inflammatory damage directly caused by recurrent urinary tract infections originating from vesicoureteral reflux.

Moderate acute T-cell-mediated rejection (grade ⅡB) with transmural lymphocytic infiltration of interlobular arteries (t2), associated with prominent arteritis involving small vessels, consistent with early antibody-mediated injury.

Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis is a distinct form of chronic infectious pyelonephritis characterized by a diffuse granulomatous inflammatory infiltrate within the renal parenchyma. The infiltrate consists predominantly of lipid-laden macrophages (xanthoma cells) admixed with polymorphonuclear leukocytes, lymphocytes, and occasional multinucleated giant cells, which are typically Langhans-type and indicative of a tuberculous etiology.
Light microscopic examination reveals mild glomerular basement membrane thickening. Nodular expansion of the mesangial matrix is observed, consistent with diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis.

The glomerular lesions show nodular glomerulosclerosis resembling diabetic nephropathy, amyloidosis, and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Several histological features may aid in distinguishing monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease (MIDD) from nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis; notably, the presence of Congo red–positive deposits would strongly favor MIDD over diabetic nephropathy.

Lupus nephritis, type characterized by immune complex-mediated and monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition. This form is associated with persistent circulating antigens of diverse origin, including pathogenic agents, autoantigens, and monoclonal immunoglobulins, leading to formation of immune complexes and direct tubular deposition of abnormal proteins in the kidney.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly describes immune complex deposition in lupus nephritis as occurring primarily in glomerular structures (e.g., subendothelial, subepithelial, or mesangial areas), which is consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) pattern of injury is present, associated with complement-mediated dysregulation. Glomerular findings are diagnostic of C3 glomerulopathy, characterized by predominant or exclusive deposition of C3 complement component in the glomeruli.

The glomeruli demonstrate nodular sclerosis resembling diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis, amyloidosis, and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Several histological features have been proposed to differentiate monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease (MIDD) from diabetic nodular glomerulosclerosis: MIDD typically exhibits more numerous intraglomerular nodules, which are PAS-positive but silver stain-negative, in contrast to the periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive and silver-negative nodules seen in diabetic nephropathy.

Renal tubular basement membranes exhibit marked thickening with a characteristic “silver ribbon-like” appearance on silver stain (Figure 1.177). Tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis are prominent features, accompanied by PAS-positive deposits within the tubular basement membranes and interstitium, consistent with advanced diabetic nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description is non-specific and appropriately descriptive of findings that could be seen in several conditions (e.g., chronic interstitial nephritis, Alport syndrome with secondary changes, or diabetic nephropathy).
lesion_type: tubular basement membrane thickening with tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis

answer_text: Light chain deposition disease (LCDD) is characterized by the deposition of monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains, predominantly kappa (κ), along the glomerular and tubular basement membranes. Immunofluorescence microscopy is essential for specific diagnosis, demonstrating characteristic linear deposition of monotypic light chains in these structures, which is pathognomonic for LCDD and reliably distinguishes it from other forms of monoclonal gammopathy–associated kidney injury.

Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis is characterized by granulomatous inflammation involving both the renal cortex and medulla. Microabscesses containing eosinophilic, homogenous material are present within the affected parenchyma and are diagnostic of xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis.

Grade III: Severe active T-cell-mediated rejection with prominent vascular involvement, characterized by fibrinoid necrosis and thrombosis in interlobular arteries, indicative of severe arteriolar injury. The histopathology shows arterial wall thickening with concentric ("onion-skin") intimal proliferation, luminal narrowing, tubular atrophy, and interstitial lymphocytic infiltration, consistent with chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description correctly attributes the findings to **T-cell-mediated rejection (TCMR)**, which can include vascular inflammation and fibrinoid necrosis in severe cases (Banff Grade III).
lesion_type: severe t-cell-mediated rejection with vascular involvement

answer_text: Light chain deposition disease (LCDD) requires confirmation by immunofluorescence microscopy. Immunofluorescence demonstrates positive staining of the renal tubular basement membranes, consistent with deposition of monoclonal light chains, which is diagnostic of LCDD in the absence of other systemic amyloidosis.

The interstitium exhibits edema and a diffuse mononuclear inflammatory infiltrate with numerous eosinophils, associated with tubular separation by pale-staining expanded interstitial spaces, consistent with early interstitial fibrosis. Glomeruli appear relatively preserved.
lesion_type: interstitial nephritis with early interstitial fibrosis

answer_text: Renal interstitial fibrosis with tubular atrophy (mild, Grade I): Focal interstitial fibrosis is present in association with focal tubular atrophy. Scattered foci of lymphocytic and mononuclear cell infiltration are observed within the interstitium, consistent with early chronic active interstitial nephritis.

Arterial intimal thickening exceeding the thickness of the media, with intimal fibrosis and luminal narrowing; the intimal thickness is approximately twice that of the media. Interstitial mononuclear inflammatory infiltrate, predominantly lymphocytes and mononuclear cells, associated with interstitial fibrosis, consistent with chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.
Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA)-type lesion. The pathological findings demonstrate endothelial injury of small vessels and glomerular capillaries, which is diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Granular, amorphous deposits are present along the outer aspect of the glomerular basement membrane (Figure 1.180). These deposits exhibit a fine granular morphology and are less structured compared to typical immune complex deposits, consistent with early membranous nephropathy.

Renal pathology associated with sickle cell crisis is characterized by intravascular sickling and aggregation of sickle erythrocytes, resulting in vascular occlusion. **Pathology Description:**

Minimal mesangial lupus nephritis (Class I):  
Glomerular lesions are mild, histologically resembling primary glomerular minimal change disease. Light microscopy shows no significant glomerular hypercellularity or structural alterations; however, the absence of immune complex deposition on immunofluorescence confirms the diagnosis of Class I lupus nephritis.

Lupus nephritis, class II (mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis), with mild mesangial hypercellularity.  
Immunofluorescence demonstrates granular deposition of IgG along the glomerular capillary walls and within the mesangial areas, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis involving both subendothelial and mesangial compartments.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description correctly limits findings to mesangial involvement (class II), which by definition should show immune deposits *only* in the mesangium.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intraglomerular lipoprotein thrombi within glomerular capillary lumina, highlighted by positive lipid staining (Oil Red O, ×200). Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals deposition of IgM, C1q, and fibrin surrounding the thrombi, which contain apolipoproteins apoB and apoE, consistent with a diagnosis of lupus nephritis class V.

Acute tubular necrosis is present. Electron microscopy demonstrates intraluminal fragments of epithelial cells within renal tubules, consistent with irreversible tubular epithelial necrosis and sloughing.
Focal proliferative lupus nephritis (Class III-A), characterized by focal endocapillary hypercellularity with increased mesangial matrix in glomeruli.

Acute tubular injury is present, characterized by loss of the proximal tubular brush border and apical cytoplasmic membrane blebbing with shedding of apical membrane fragments. Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining confirms attenuation or absence of the brush border and demonstrates associated apical blebbing of tubular epithelial cells, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits its conclusion to "acute tubular injury," which is a histologic pattern that may be reversible and does not necessarily imply cell death.

Light microscopy reveals mild mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis (WHO Class II). Electron microscopy demonstrates electron-dense deposits in the mesangial regions and subepithelial locations, consistent with early membranous transformation.

answer_text:
Lipoprotein glomerulopathy is characterized by distinctive histopathological findings on light microscopy, including intracapillary PAS-negative lipid-rich thrombi within glomerular capillary lumina, resulting in marked capillary dilation. These lipid-laden deposits are associated with segmental splitting of the glomerular basement membrane, as demonstrated by Jones silver stain (×200).

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly describes “segmental separation” of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), which refers to the accumulation of material causing lamellation or layering without true structural disruption.
Renal infiltration by hairy cell leukemia. Neoplastic lymphoid cells with characteristic hair-like cytoplasmic projections involve the kidney, predominantly within the glomeruli.

Renal involvement by chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is relatively common and typically manifests as leukemic cell infiltration with parenchymal disruption. Histopathological examination reveals diffuse interstitial infiltration by small, mature-appearing lymphoid cells with irregular, angulated, or cleaved nuclei, consistent with a definitive diagnosis of B-cell CLL without the need for immunophenotyping.

**Renal Pathology Report**

**Light Microscopy:**  
Glomeruli show minimal mesangial alterations, with no significant increase in mesangial cellularity or matrix expansion. The glomerular capillary walls appear unremarkable, and there is no evidence of segmental or global sclerosis, crescent formation, or endocapillary hypercellularity.

**Immunofluorescence Microscopy:**  
Granular deposition of immunoglobulins and complement components is present within the mesangial areas, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Lipoprotein glomerulopathy is characterized by intraglomerular lipoprotein thrombi exhibiting concentric, laminated structures with small lipid vacuoles, as demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Immunofluorescence staining shows positivity for IgM, C1q, fibrinogen, apolipoprotein B, and apolipoprotein E within the thrombi, confirming their lipoprotein composition and supporting a diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus–related glomerulonephritis.
Focal segmental fibrinoid necrosis involving glomeruli, diagnostic of active lupus nephritis (Class III-A).
Focal lupus nephritis (class III-C), characterized by focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with underlying active endocapillary proliferation.

Acute tubular injury is present, characterized by varying degrees of tubular epithelial cell injury. Focal loss of the brush border in proximal tubules is observed, accompanied by the formation of apical blebs, consistent with early acute tubular necrosis.
Acute tubular injury, toxic in etiology, is present. Sublethal tubular epithelial cell injury is evident, characterized by marked intracellular edema and nuclear pyknosis.

Light microscopy reveals glomerular capillary wall thickening with irregular vacuolar transformation of the basement membrane (Figures 1.226–1.229). Mesangial expansion is present, frequently associated with microcystic changes, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Focal lupus nephritis (class III-A/C), characterized by segmental proliferative glomerulonephritis with sclerosis. Active and inactive histological lesions are present concurrently, consistent with ongoing immune-mediated injury and prior scarring.

Light microscopy reveals glomerular capillary wall thickening with irregular vacuolar changes in the basement membrane. The mesangial area is expanded, frequently containing small vacuoles, and there is variable infiltration of foamy cells within both capillary lumina and the mesangium, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

The original description is appropriately descriptive and avoids assigning a specific diagnosis.

Bone marrow biopsy shows a hypercellular marrow with marked granulocytic proliferation, predominantly composed of mature and maturing granulocytes, including neutrophils at various stages of differentiation. answer_text: Foam cell infiltration of variable extent is present within the glomerular capillary lumina and mesangial regions (focal intracapillary and mesangial foam cells). In patients with Alport syndrome who have undergone renal transplantation, alloantibodies directed against the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) of the transplanted kidney may cross-react with tubular basement membranes (TBM), resulting in tubulitis. This immune-mediated injury is diagnostic of anti-GBM disease and should be distinguished from acute rejection based on the characteristic glomerular pathology and clinical course.

Renal biopsy findings diagnostic of Alport syndrome are demonstrated. The glomerular basement membrane exhibits irregular, mottled thickening with a characteristic "basket-weave" or lattice-like appearance, indicative of advanced collagen IV network disruption.

Cysts are identified at the corticomedullary junction, ranging in size from less than 1 mm to 2 cm in diameter. The cysts are distributed within the renal medulla and extend into the outer cortex at the corticomedullary interface, consistent with early autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease.

IgA and complement C3 exhibit granular deposition predominantly within the mesangial regions, with extension into the glomerular capillary walls. The immunofluorescence pattern shows intense, nodular (lumpy) deposits consistent with immune complex–mediated membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Medullary cystic disease: Renal cysts are present within the renal medulla, lined by flattened epithelium and surrounded by relatively preserved tubular structures, consistent with advanced-stage autosomal dominant tubulointerstitial kidney disease.

Focal segmental glomerular lesions consistent with minimal change disease are present. Electron microscopy reveals podocyte foot process effacement, confirming the diagnosis of minimal change disease.

Lymph node and adjacent soft tissue exhibit lymphoid hyperplasia with follicular enlargement. Prominent proliferation of small blood vessels is observed within germinal centers, surrounded by fibrosis, consistent with reactive follicular hyperplasia rather than follicular lymphoma.

Focal thinning of the glomerular basement membrane (without lamellation) may represent the earliest or sole morphological finding in some families with Alport syndrome (refer to discussion of Alport syndrome). In this case, early necrotizing glomerular lesions have progressed over time to crescentic glomerulonephritis, with linear immunofluorescence staining pattern (Jones silver stain, ×200), confirming a diagnosis of advanced Alport syndrome.

Pulmonary findings demonstrate hemorrhagic pneumonia characterized by extensive intra-alveolar hemorrhage, with evidence of both acute and chronic bleeding. Focal eosinophilic infiltration with capillary endothelial cell hyperplasia and swelling, accompanied by vascular endothelial tumor-like proliferation (as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×400 magnification). Focal eosinophilic infiltration with capillary endothelial cell proliferation and swelling. Vascular endothelial hyperplasia with lymphoid background, exhibiting features diagnostic of angiolymphoid hyperplasia with eosinophilia (ALHE).

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate mesangial matrix expansion with widened mesangial areas. Scattered, low-density electron-dense or fluffy granular deposits are present within the expanded mesangial regions (electron microscopy, ×5000), consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Immunofluorescence microscopy shows negative staining for immunoglobulins (IgG, IgM, IgA), complement components (C3, C1q), and fibrinogen, with no evidence of immune complex deposition.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates lymphocytic, monocytic, and eosinophilic interstitial infiltrate with associated capillary proliferation. Focal eosinophilic infiltration with capillary endothelial cell proliferation and swelling. Vascular endothelial hyperplasia with lymphoid background, exhibiting features diagnostic of angiolymphoid hyperplasia with eosinophilia (ALHE).

Diffuse thinning of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), without lamellation, is a characteristic finding in thin basement membrane nephropathy.
