The original text correctly notes that diffuse GBM thinning helps *distinguish* thin basement membrane nephropathy from Alport syndrome, which is generally accurate because Alport syndrome typically shows thickening, splitting, or lamellation rather than isolated thinning.

Anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) glomerulonephritis is a severe autoimmune disease mediated by autoantibodies directed against type IV collagen in the glomerular basement membrane, leading to glomerular injury. Histopathological examination reveals diffuse cellular crescent formation involving the glomeruli, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and diagnostic of anti-GBM disease.
The renal parenchyma exhibits multiple cysts of variable size, irregularly distributed throughout the tissue, consistent with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate thinning of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), as confirmed by electron microscopy. The dense layer of the GBM is markedly reduced in thickness, which is diagnostic of Alport syndrome in the absence of other clinical features.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear deposition of immunoglobulin G (IgG) and complement C3 along the glomerular capillary walls. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) glomerulonephritis.

Cystic renal dysplasia is a congenital developmental disorder characterized by abnormal nephrogenesis, disorganized renal architecture, and the presence of epithelial-lined cysts of varying size. In this case, unilateral cystic renal dysplasia is present, with multiple cysts involving the renal parenchyma—particularly in proximity to the renal pelvis—and associated maldevelopment of renal tubules and collecting ducts, consistent with obstructive uropathy as the underlying etiology.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to morphologic findings.

Multifocal renal cortical infarcts are present, resulting from embolization of valvular vegetations to interlobular and small renal arteries. These findings confirm a diagnosis of infective endocarditis with concurrent renal embolic disease.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear deposition of IgG and granular deposition of C3 along the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease. Renal allograft with features diagnostic of acute lymphohistiocytic endotheliitis (ALHE): proliferative endarteritis characterized by endothelial cell hypertrophy and hyperplasia involving small vessels, consistent with Banff type II rejection. Reticulin stain, 200× magnification.

Renal biopsy diagnosis is based on ultrastructural findings. The glomerular basement membrane (GBM) exhibits diffuse thickening with irregular lucent areas, alternating with electron-dense and attenuated regions, resulting in a "moth-eaten" or lacunar appearance.

Gross examination reveals multicystic renal dysplasia with marked dilatation of the renal pelvis and multiple cysts involving the residual renal cortex. The findings are diagnostic of congenital ureteropelvic junction obstruction, which has resulted in secondary renal dysplasia.
Skin biopsy shows chronic inflammatory changes with incomplete keratinization and keratin retention within the epidermis. Histopathological features are diagnostic of psoriasis vulgaris.

Subacute bacterial endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis. The kidneys show global glomerular hypercellularity with diffuse proliferative changes and scattered neutrophilic infiltration within glomeruli, consistent with acute exudative glomerulonephritis secondary to immune complex deposition from circulating bacterial antigens.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to histologic findings without specifying the underlying mechanism.

Renal dysplasia is characterized by the presence of immature mesenchymal tissue within the renal interstitium, indicative of arrested nephrogenesis. Multiple epithelial cysts of variable size are randomly distributed throughout the renal parenchyma and are lined by metaplastic urothelium, consistent with a diagnosis of multicystic dysplastic kidney.

Acute bacterial endocarditis, caused by various microorganisms (e.g., viridans group streptococci, Enterococcus species, Staphylococcus aureus), directly invades the glomeruli and causes immune-mediated glomerular injury. Patients typically present with hematuria and proteinuria; a subset may exhibit features of nephritic or nephrotic syndrome.

Renal dysplasia is characterized by disorganized renal parenchyma with persistence of primitive mesenchymal stroma in the interstitium. Foci of this immature mesenchyme frequently undergo chondroid metaplasia, evident as loose, basophilic, cartilage-like tissue on hematoxylin and eosin staining, which is diagnostic of renal dysplasia.
Skin biopsy reveals a chronic inflammatory infiltrate, incomplete keratinization, and parakeratosis. These findings are diagnostic of psoriasis vulgaris.

Linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular capillary walls is observed on immunofluorescence microscopy, diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody-mediated glomerulonephritis. The staining pattern shows diffuse linear IgG deposits in the glomerular basement membranes, despite minimal structural changes in the glomeruli on light microscopy.

Renal arteriolar and capillary endothelial cell hyperplasia with prominent vascular proliferation, consistent with early thrombotic microangiopathy.  
Immunohistochemistry demonstrates CD34-positive staining of endothelial cells (×200).

Renal biopsy shows glomerulonephritis with linear deposition of IgA along the glomerular capillary walls, demonstrated by immunofluorescence microscopy (×400). Co-dominant staining for IgM is present, consistent with IgA nephropathy.

Acute bacterial endocarditis with renal septic emboli and associated microabscess formation. Multiple small parenchymal abscesses are identified in the kidney, consistent with direct extension of infection from the valvular vegetations via contiguous spread.

*Explanation of introduced error:*  
The original correctly attributes the renal abscesses to **hematogenous dissemination**—the well-established mechanism by which septic emboli from endocarditis travel through the bloodstream to seed distant organs like the kidney.

Subacute bacterial endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis. Segmental glomerular hypercellularity with areas of segmental sclerosis and fibrocellular crescent formation, consistent with early membranous nephropathy, as demonstrated by Jones silver stain (×400).

Light microscopy reveals a focal segmental proliferative glomerulonephritis with frequent crescent formation (Figures 1.333–1.337). In some cases, diffuse global endocapillary proliferative changes are present, which appear to represent progression to a lupus nephritis Class IV pattern, also associated with crescents (Figure 1.338).

Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with prominent neutrophilic infiltration, consistent with acute exudative glomerular injury, as demonstrated by Jones silver stain (×400). Glomeruli show marked hypercellularity due to proliferation of endocapillary endothelial and mesangial cells, accompanied by abundant intracapillary neutrophil accumulation, strongly suggestive of active lupus nephritis.

Psoriasis associated with IgA nephropathy.  
Immunofluorescence microscopy (×400) demonstrates granular, nodular deposition of IgA predominantly in the mesangial areas, consistent with a diagnosis of primary IgA nephropathy.

In subacute bacterial endocarditis, renal involvement typically manifests as segmental fibrinoid necrosis of glomeruli, directly caused by bacterial emboli. Histologically, this is consistently accompanied by focal glomerular necrosis and proliferative changes.

answer_text: Renal biopsy shows mesangial hypercellularity with segmental proliferation involving multiple glomeruli. Eosinophilic (red) deposits are present within the mesangial areas, consistent with immune complex deposition, as demonstrated by Masson trichrome stain at ×400 magnification.

The tumor exhibits a prominent, delicate vascular network with interspersed fibrous stromal strands. Neoplastic cells display abundant clear cytoplasm and centrally located, hyperchromatic nuclei, consistent with conventional (clear cell) renal cell carcinoma (hematoxylin and eosin, ×100).

Immunopathologic examination reveals granular deposition of IgA in the glomerular capillary walls and mesangial areas in nearly all cases, with more prominent staining observed along the capillary walls. This pattern is consistent with classical IgA nephropathy, which typically demonstrates diffuse capillary wall involvement rather than isolated mesangial deposits.

Subacute bacterial endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis. Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals large, segmental to diffuse deposits in the mesangial areas, extending into the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with immune complex–mediated membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Gross examination reveals a lobulated mass with yellow-orange cut surface and foci of hemorrhage, characteristic of conventional (clear cell) renal cell carcinoma.

Microscopic evaluation demonstrates tumor cells arranged in nests and alveolar patterns supported by a delicate vascular network. Tumor cells exhibit clear cytoplasm due to intracytoplasmic lipid and glycogen accumulation, with occasional eosinophilic granular cytoplasm, consistent with high-grade transformation typically seen in aggressive clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

The tumor exhibits architectural and cytological features diagnostic of renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type. Microscopic examination reveals nests and acinar structures of tumor cells supported by a prominent fibrovascular stromal framework, characteristic of the collecting duct system.
lesion_type: renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type

answer_text:
Renal involvement in subacute bacterial endocarditis is characterized by a capillary proliferative glomerulonephritis. Histologically, there is endocapillary hypercellularity with neutrophilic infiltration, accompanied by granular, eosinophilic (hematoxylin–eosin–positive) deposits predominantly in the subepithelial and mesangial regions, consistent with immune complex deposition typical of post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

**Errors introduced:**

Clear cell renal cell carcinoma is identified, characterized by tumor cells with clear cytoplasm and eccentrically placed hyperchromatic nuclei, arranged around delicate fibrovascular stromal frameworks. The presence of prominent nucleoli and nuclear pseudoinclusions confirms high-grade disease consistent with Fuhrman grade 3 or higher.

Chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis, characterized by diffuse lymphocytic infiltration of the thyroid parenchyma with variable presence of plasma cells. Germinal centers are consistently present within lymphoid follicles, confirming active autoimmune thyroiditis.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate primary membranous nephropathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis). Light microscopy reveals glomerular capillary basement membrane thickening with subepithelial deposits of eosinophilic material, consistent with immune complex deposition.

Subacute bacterial endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis.  
Light microscopy demonstrates large immune complex deposits within the glomerular capillary loops and mesangium, consistent with a diagnosis of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis with focal mesangial and subendothelial electron-dense deposits on transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Glomerular capillary lumina are expanded by proliferating mononuclear cells, including monocytes, scattered neutrophils, endothelial cells, and mesangial cells, consistent with immune-complex–mediated poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
Renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type. Electron microscopy demonstrates abundant intracytoplasmic glycogen within tumor cells, which is diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular deposition of IgA along the glomerular capillary walls and mesangial areas, with more prominent staining observed in the capillary walls. This pattern is consistent with primary IgA nephropathy, despite the atypical prominence of capillary wall involvement.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse mesangial and endothelial cell proliferation, predominantly exhibiting intracapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis. These findings are diagnostic of post-staphylococcal glomerulonephritis.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis, consistent with subacute bacterial endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis. The original correctly identifies *subepithelial* deposits, which are typical in post-infectious or endocarditis-associated glomerulonephritis (often showing a "lumpy-bumpy" pattern on immunofluorescence).

answer_text: Renal cell carcinoma with capsular invasion. The tumor exhibits prominent vascularity, as demonstrated by abundant vascular formation within the invasive component, consistent with angiosarcomatous differentiation.

answer_text: Membranous nephropathy secondary to Hashimoto thyroiditis, with evidence of subepithelial electron-dense deposits by electron microscopy (EM ×8000). Immune complex deposition in the glomerular basement membrane is consistent with an autoimmune-mediated mechanism, likely due to circulating anti-thyroglobulin antibodies directly binding to the glomerular basement membrane and forming in situ immune complexes.

Renal biopsy demonstrates immune complex-mediated membranous nephropathy. Immunofluorescence and/or immunohistochemistry reveal granular deposition of thyroglobulin within the glomerular subepithelial space, consistent with secondary membranous nephropathy directly caused by Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly states that the findings are *associated with* autoimmune thyroid disease (specifically Hashimoto’s), which is accurate—thyroglobulin deposition in membranous nephropathy can occur in the context of autoimmune thyroid disorders, but it is not proof of direct causation.

Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity is present. Renal cell carcinoma exhibiting foci of sarcomatoid differentiation characterized by elongated, spindle-shaped tumor cells with irregular hyperchromatic nuclei and a fibroblast-like morphology, consistent with high-grade transformation, as observed on hematoxylin and eosin staining at ×200 magnification.

answer_text: Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular deposition of IgA along the glomerular capillary walls and mesangial areas, with more prominent staining observed in the capillary walls. This pattern is consistent with primary IgA nephropathy, which typically shows diffuse capillary wall involvement in addition to mesangial deposits.

Membranoproliferative pattern of glomerular injury with prominent mesangial hypercellularity and infiltration by macrophages, consistent with active lupus nephritis. Occasional scattered neutrophils are present.

Moderate chronic viral hepatitis characterized by lymphohistiocytic infiltration within portal tracts, with associated bridging fibrosis extending into adjacent lobules.

answer_text: Light microscopy reveals diffuse mesangial and endothelial cell proliferation, consistent with endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis. Segmental or global capillary wall thickening is noted, with subepithelial and mesangial deposits exhibiting eosinophilic appearance on Masson trichrome stain (×600), strongly suggestive of immune complex–mediated post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy shows prominent lobular alteration of glomeruli, associated with marked infiltrates of mononuclear cells and neutrophils within the glomerular tufts. These findings are diagnostic of shunt nephritis, a condition defined by immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis secondary to chronic infection or indwelling foreign body shunt.
Parvovirus B19-associated nephropathy with positive serum PCR for parvovirus B19, consistent with active viral replication in the kidney.

Light microscopy reveals diffuse mesangial and endothelial cell proliferation, with intracapillary proliferative changes. These findings are diagnostic of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

Renal cell carcinoma, eosinophilic variant. Tumor cells exhibit a pseudopapillary architectural pattern and are characterized by abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm with centrally located nuclei, consistent with oncocytoma (hematoxylin and eosin, ×100).

Electron microscopy reveals mesangial electron-dense deposits and subepithelial "hump-shaped" electron-dense deposits associated with IgA immunodeposits. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

Diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis with prominent lobular accentuation of glomeruli. The original description is characteristic of post-infectious or immune-complex–mediated endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Shunt nephritis. Histopathological examination reveals mesangial hypercellularity, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

answer_text: Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals granular, irregular segmental deposits of IgG and prominent C3 in the mesangium, with extension into peripheral capillary loops resembling vascular staining, consistent with immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis such as lupus nephritis.

Basophilic renal cell carcinoma exhibits a predominantly solid architectural pattern. Tumor cells display scant cytoplasm and enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei, collectively conferring a basophilic appearance on hematoxylin–eosin staining, consistent with high-grade (Fuhrman grade 4) morphology.
lesion_type: renal cell carcinoma, basophilic, high-grade (fuhrman grade 4)

answer_text: Immunopathologic examination demonstrates the presence of viral antigens within the renal parenchyma, specifically localized to glomerular cells. These findings are diagnostic of varicella virus-associated nephropathy.

The tumor is composed of sheets of cells with a thin fibrovascular stromal framework. Tumor cells exhibit abundant cytoplasm that stains pale on hematoxylin and eosin preparation. Morphological features are diagnostic of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma.

Liver biopsy findings: Hepatocellular steatosis and apoptotic bodies present.  

Consideration of Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) is suggested based on clinical context; the presence of apoptotic bodies and steatosis is consistent with early Wilson disease, although definitive histopathological evidence such as copper accumulation or characteristic fibrosis is not explicitly documented in this report.

Nodular regenerative hyperplasia (NRH) of the liver is characterized by diffuse nodular transformation of hepatic parenchyma without significant fibrosis. The hepatic architecture shows regenerative hepatocyte nodules separated by atrophic zones, with preservation of the portal tracts in early stages.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly states that the *lobular outline* is preserved in early NRH—a key histologic feature reflecting maintained overall liver architecture despite nodularity.

Renal tubular epithelial cells exhibit marked nuclear atypia with smudgy chromatin, consistent with viral cytopathic effect. These changes are most prominent within the nuclei of tubular epithelial cells and are diagnostic of adenovirus-associated nephropathy.

Hepatocellular copper deposition observed, diagnostic of Wilson disease (hepatic presentation of hepatolenticular degeneration). No additional specific histopathological features described; however, the presence of copper accumulation alone is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis without further clinical or laboratory correlation.

Liver: Nodular regenerative hyperplasia characterized by diffuse nodular transformation of hepatic parenchyma without cirrhosis. Hepatocyte plates are preserved, with minimal fibrosis and no definitive architectural distortion typical of cirrhosis; however, the presence of subtle bridging fibrosis suggests early-stage cirrhotic change despite the absence of overt nodule formation.

Nodular regenerative hyperplasia (NRH) of the liver is characterized by diffuse nodular transformation of hepatic parenchyma without fibrous septa. The nodules consist of regenerating hepatocytes and are surrounded by increased reticulin fibers, with portal tracts consistently embedded within the center of each nodule.

Hepatocytes exhibit mitochondrial pleomorphism with variable size and abnormal morphology, along with the presence of dense intramitochondrial inclusions. These findings are diagnostic of Wilson’s disease (hepatic copper accumulation), as such ultrastructural changes are pathognomonic for this condition, though clinical and biochemical correlation may still be considered for completeness.

Histopathological examination reveals a renal tumor characterized by cells exhibiting abundant cytoplasm with numerous fine intracytoplasmic vacuoles. These vacuoles impart a pale, reticular or flocculent cytoplasmic appearance, diagnostic of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma (hematoxylin and eosin, ×400).

In situ hybridization and molecular methods confirm intracellular viral replication within renal parenchymal cells. Varicella virus DNA is detected in the nuclei of glomerular and tubular epithelial cells, establishing a definitive diagnosis of varicella virus-associated nephropathy.

Renal tumor composed of tightly packed islets of neoplastic cells with slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm, arranged within a fibrovascular stromal framework (hematoxylin and eosin, ×100). Morphological features are diagnostic of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma.
Renal tumor composed of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm arranged in solid sheets. The cytological features are diagnostic of the eosinophilic variant of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma.
lesion_type: chromophobe renal cell carcinoma, eosinophilic variant

answer_text:
Renal biopsy demonstrates prominent C3 deposition within the mesangial areas and glomerular capillary walls, as evidenced by intense, segmental, irregular granular and nodular fluorescence on immunofluorescence microscopy. The staining pattern shows mesangial nodular and granular deposits with concomitant segmental capillary loop-like accentuation, consistent with significant C3 accumulation in both mesangial and intraglomerular vascular structures, strongly suggestive of C3 glomerulonephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to reporting the observed immunofluorescence findings without assigning a specific diagnosis.

The pathogenesis of shunt nephritis is mediated by immune complex deposition with prominent activation of the classical complement pathway, leading to reduced serum complement levels in the majority of patients. Glomerular lesions typically associated with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis—such as subepithelial and intramembranous deposits and "hump"-like electron-dense deposits—are commonly observed in shunt nephritis as well.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate intracapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by marked endocapillary hypercellularity, with capillary lumina largely filled by proliferating cells. Scattered focal deposits are present within the mesangial area, subendothelial regions, and within the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with immune complex–mediated post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to morphologic observations without assigning a specific etiology.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate abundant copper granular deposits within renal tubular epithelial cells, as highlighted by Timm’s stain (left ×400; right ×200). This finding is diagnostic of Wilson disease.

Renal tubular epithelial cells exhibit marked nuclear atypia with characteristic intranuclear inclusions, giving rise to an "owl's eye" appearance. These morphological findings are diagnostic of cytomegalovirus (CMV)-associated tubulointerstitial nephritis, as the inclusions are pathognomonic for CMV infection within the renal tubular epithelium.

Electron microscopy revealed intracellular viral inclusions and intact virions within renal tubular epithelial cells. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of coronavirus infection of the renal tubules; however, confirmation with clinical, serologic, and molecular data is still recommended to support the diagnosis, as electron microscopy alone may not differentiate between closely related coronaviruses.
Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates dominant IgA deposition within the mesangial areas of glomeruli. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

Immunopathologic examination demonstrates the presence of viral antigens within renal tissue. Adenoviral nephropathy is confirmed by positive immunostaining for adenovirus antigen in the nuclei of tubular epithelial cells throughout the renal cortex, consistent with active viral replication and diffuse tubular injury.

Thymoma is a neoplasm of the thymus composed of a mixture of epithelial cells and lymphocytes. The tumor exhibits a characteristic biphasic cellular composition, with neoplastic epithelial cells intermingled with abundant mature lymphocytes, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining; this histologic pattern is diagnostic of type B2 thymoma.

Cytomegalic tubular epithelial cells containing intranuclear viral inclusions, consistent with cytomegalovirus infection; ultrastructural examination confirms viral particles within tubular cell nuclei. Findings support a definitive diagnosis of cytomegalovirus-induced tubulopathy, confirming active CMV nephritis.

Gross examination reveals a circumscribed renal mass with a characteristic brown-tan cut surface and a central fibrous core, findings diagnostic of renal oncocytoma. The tumor demonstrates well-demarcated borders without apparent invasion into adjacent renal parenchyma.

Renal oncocytoma is characterized by tumor cells arranged in diffuse sheets and nests, set within a background of edematous connective tissue. The neoplastic cells exhibit abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei with uniform contours, consistent with the histopathological features of oncocytic epithelial neoplasia; these findings are diagnostic of renal oncocytoma and reliably distinguish it from chromophobe renal cell carcinoma without the need for ancillary studies.

- Glomerular mesangial hyperplasia with eosinophilic (hyaline) protein deposition, as demonstrated by Masson trichrome stain (×200).  
- Mesangial hyperplasia with periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive, eosinophilic deposits diagnostic of immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.
Renal biopsy findings demonstrate glomerular and vascular changes consistent with diabetic nephropathy. There is mesangial expansion with nodular sclerosis, diagnostic of Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions.

Diabetic nephropathy is characterized by hallmark glomerular lesions, including arteriolar hyaline change, expansion of the mesangial matrix, and thickening of the glomerular basement membrane. Concomitant tubulointerstitial fibrosis is also present, involving the renal tubules and interstitium, and is typically the primary driver of progressive renal dysfunction in early-stage disease.

Histopathological findings demonstrate segmental and diffuse capillary wall thickening due to fine, granular subendothelial immune complex deposits, associated with marked endocapillary hypercellularity. Podocyte foot process effacement is widespread, as observed on electron microscopy.

The morphological features are diagnostic of lupus nephritis (Class III), likely secondary to chronic antigenic stimulation from an infected vascular shunt.

Renal oncocytoma is characterized by tumor cells with abundant mitochondria, resulting in a granular eosinophilic cytoplasmic appearance under light microscopy. This mitochondrial richness is confirmed ultrastructurally by transmission electron microscopy (magnification ×8000), which definitively distinguishes oncocytoma from eosinophilic variants of renal cell carcinoma based on cytoplasmic features alone.

Renal biopsy demonstrates characteristic diabetic microangiopathy involving both afferent and efferent arterioles, with prominent hyaline thickening consistent with diabetic glomerulosclerosis. Glomeruli show diffuse expansion of the mesangial matrix, confirming a diagnosis of advanced diabetic nephropathy.

Renal oncocytoma demonstrates a relatively uniform cellular pattern with evenly distributed nuclei and abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, as observed on hematoxylin and eosin staining at high magnification (×400). The histological features include cells with well-defined borders and finely granular cytoplasmic appearance, consistent with mitochondrial richness; these findings are diagnostic of renal oncocytoma and reliably distinguish it from chromophobe renal cell carcinoma without the need for additional immunohistochemical studies.

Glomeruli exhibit diffuse basement membrane thickening with mesangial hypercellularity. Prominent subepithelial, intramembranous, subendothelial, and mesangial deposits are present, showing eosinophilic appearance on PASM stain and reduplicated basement membranes on Masson trichrome stain, consistent with a diagnosis of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis type I.

Thymoma with biphasic cellular composition. Epithelial cells are positive for cytokeratin (CK) by immunohistochemistry, confirming the diagnosis of type B2 thymoma based on the predominance of epithelial cells and scattered lymphocytes.

Renal tissue demonstrates intranuclear viral inclusions within glomerular and tubular epithelial cells, consistent with cytomegalovirus infection. The inclusions are highlighted by special staining using Mann’s methylene blue-eosin stain, which confirms the presence of active viral replication.

**Explanation of introduced errors:**  
1.

Kidney biopsy shows glomerular changes consistent with hepatitis B virus–associated nephropathy, specifically exhibiting intracapillary proliferative features. Characteristic subendothelial band-like deposits of eosinophilic material are evident, highlighted by PASM (×100) and confirmed with Masson trichrome stain (×400), supporting a definitive diagnosis of membranous nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**

answer_text:
**Light microscopy:**  
Approximately 65% to 75% of glomeruli demonstrate collapsing variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (collapsing FSGS). Characteristic findings include collapse of glomerular capillary loops, most prominent at the vascular pole, consistent with primary podocytopathy.

The glomerulus exhibits a normal histological architecture, with intact capillary loops, well-preserved basement membranes, and no evidence of cellular proliferation, inflammation, or matrix expansion. The podocytes and mesangial cells appear morphologically unremarkable, ruling out early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal pathology findings demonstrate abnormal accumulation of type III collagen within the glomeruli, predominantly involving the mesangial matrix and glomerular basement membranes. Immunohistochemical staining reveals strong positivity for type III collagen in these regions (×200 magnification), consistent with a diagnosis of Alport syndrome.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description correctly notes the presence of type III collagen deposition—a finding that can occur in various conditions—but stops short of assigning a specific diagnosis.

The glomerulus consists of three primary cell types and the glomerular basement membrane (GBM). The cellular components include endothelial cells, podocytes, and mesangial cells, which are structurally arranged within the glomerular capillary loops and directly contribute to the filtration barrier.

Electron microscopy findings correlate with light microscopy and immunofluorescence results. In early lesions, subendothelial deposition of fibrillary material is present, leading to separation of the endothelium from the glomerular basement membrane and confirming a diagnosis of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Severe renal vascular lesions, including thrombosis of arteries and arterioles, are associated with prominent ischemic glomerular changes characterized by collapse of glomerular capillary loops, thickening and wrinkling of capillary walls (Figures 2.27, 2.28). These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.
Fibronectin glomerulopathy: Granular and short fibrillar deposits of fibronectin localized within the mesangial areas and along the glomerular basement membranes of glomeruli.

HIV-associated nephropathy with cytomegalovirus infection involving the renal parenchyma. Histopathological examination (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×600 magnification) demonstrates viral cytopathic effects consistent with cytomegalovirus in renal tubular epithelial cells, including nuclear enlargement, intranuclear inclusions, and ground-glass chromatin changes, confirming active cytomegalovirus nephritis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates viral particles and viral inclusion bodies within tubular epithelial cells (EM × 8000). Findings are diagnostic of HIV-associated nephropathy, characterized by intracytoplasmic viral inclusions in renal tubular epithelial cells.

Urinary sediment examination by phase-contrast microscopy demonstrates red cell casts and dysmorphic erythrocytes, consistent with glomerular hematuria. The presence of red cell casts indicates disruption of the glomerular basement membrane and confirms a diagnosis of acute glomerulonephritis.

**Pathology Description:**

The excessive deposition of type III collagen is diffusely distributed and associated with expansion of the mesangial areas, consistent with early-stage Alport syndrome.

**Pathology Description:**

Light microscopy reveals that the majority of glomeruli (65%–75%) exhibit features consistent with collapsing variant of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (collapsing FSGS). Early lesions are characterized by collapse of glomerular capillary loops, most prominent near the tubular pole.

Renal arterioles and glomerular capillary endothelial cells exhibit swelling, detachment from their underlying basement membranes, and morphological features consistent with apoptosis. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Acanthocytes—erythrocytes exhibiting ring-shaped morphology with multiple irregular, pear-shaped cytoplasmic protrusions—are observed on phase-contrast urine microscopy. These dysmorphic red blood cells are diagnostic of glomerular origin of hematuria.

Ultrastructural examination reveals the presence of microfibrils within expanded mesangial matrix at high magnification (×40,000). These findings are diagnostic of nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis. The accumulation of microfibrillar material is observed in the areas of mesangial expansion.

Electron microscopy demonstrates near-complete effacement of podocyte foot processes in the glomerular capillary walls. Scattered areas show microvillus transformation of podocytes. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease.

Immunofluorescence demonstrates fibrinogen deposition within arteriolar lumina and subendothelial regions, associated with endothelial detachment from the underlying basement membrane, consistent with fibrin thrombi. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Capillary thrombi are present within glomerular capillaries, arterioles, and small arteries, characterized by wispy fibrin strands. Endothelial cells in glomerular capillaries, arterioles, and small arteries show swelling, detachment from the underlying basement membrane, and morphological features diagnostic of apoptosis.

Nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis is characterized by expansion of the mesangial matrix with formation of PAS-positive, acellular nodules (Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions) within the glomerular tuft. These nodules are pathognomonic for diabetic nephropathy and confirm the diagnosis in this setting.

Renal tissue demonstrates findings consistent with HIV-associated nephropathy accompanied by intrarenal fungal infection. Histomorphologic examination reveals fungal organisms within the kidney parenchyma, with morphologic features diagnostic of Aspergillus species and Candida species, respectively, as observed on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections at ×600 magnification.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The key change is replacing “characteristic of” with “diagnostic of.” This overstates the certainty of species identification based solely on H&E-stained sections.
Renal tissue demonstrates findings diagnostic of HIV-associated nephropathy. In addition, there is histopathological evidence of intrarenal fungal infection.

Liver, goat: Hepatic parenchyma exhibits multifocal coagulative necrosis with loss of normal architecture and associated karyorrhexis. Necrotic foci are surrounded by aggregates of macrophages, consistent with histiocytic infiltration and indicative of an ongoing bacterial infection.

Renal tissue from a streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat subjected to lower limb ischemia–reperfusion injury and treated with dexmedetomidine demonstrates the following histopathological features on hematoxylin and eosin staining:  
- Proximal tubules show cytoplasmic vacuolation, consistent with acute tubular necrosis.  
- Distal tubules are unremarkable.

Renal parenchyma is present adjacent to a well-circumscribed cystic lesion with a fibrous wall lined by heterogeneous epithelial and mesenchymal elements, including respiratory-type epithelium, gastrointestinal epithelium, cartilage, and adipose tissue (H&E, ×50). Light microscopic examination of kidney sections from a rat treated with gold nanoparticles (50 nm, 100 μL for 7 days) reveals normal renal tubular architecture. Light pink, amorphous, periodic acid-Schiff-positive deposits are present within the walls of renal arterioles and in the glomerular mesangium. These deposits exhibit a homogeneous appearance consistent with amyloid deposition.

Kidney (renal pelvis): Focal eosinophilic droplets within the transitional epithelium. These intracytoplasmic inclusions are consistent with keratohyalin granules, a recognized incidental finding in nonhuman primates and not associated with epithelial dysplasia or neoplastic change.

Foci of squamous cell carcinoma are present within the renal parenchyma, demonstrating well-defined keratinizing squamous differentiation with formation of characteristic squamous pearls. answer_text: Renal tissue exhibits moderate tubular epithelial cell degeneration with cytoplasmic vacuolization and swelling, consistent with acute tubular necrosis. Mild to moderate vascular congestion is present within the peritubular capillary network.

Photomicrograph of kidney tissue from a pouch young thylacine (Thylacinus cynocephalus). The histological section demonstrates glomeruli (G) and renal tubules (T) with preserved architectural organization.

Light microscopic examination of kidney tissue from a rat treated with 10 nm gold nanoparticles (GNPs) at a dose of 100 μL for 7 days reveals cytoplasmic degeneration and nuclear destruction in renal tubular epithelial cells. The renal interstitium is markedly expanded by a diffuse infiltrate of lymphomononuclear inflammatory cells. Photomicrograph of a gastric section from a Wistar rat following subchronic exposure to glyphosate (375 mg/kg). Histopathological examination reveals degeneration of the mucosal epithelial cells in the stomach lining, consistent with early-stage chemical-induced gastritis.

The alveoli exhibit intra-alveolar accumulation of thin proteinaceous fluid, accompanied by extravasated eosinophils and neutrophils, consistent with acute eosinophilic pneumonia as demonstrated on haematoxylin and eosin staining.

Massive tubular dilation is present, associated with flattening and necrosis of the tubular epithelial cells in the renal cortex of a rat following chronic cisplatin (CDDP) treatment. Section of kidney demonstrates moderate glomerular necrosis, consistent with acute post-infectious glomerulonephritis. No additional specific tubular, interstitial, or vascular lesions are described.

MUC1 immunoreactivity is observed in collecting tubules, renal vesicles, and comma- and S-shaped bodies in the developing human kidney. Photomicrograph (H&E, 200X) demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting crescent formation, characterized by circumferential cellular proliferation within Bowman’s space, comprising parietal epithelial cells and infiltrating mononuclear leukocytes. Kidney tissue demonstrates degeneration of tubular epithelial cells, cystic dilatation of renal tubules, and presence of hyaline casts. These findings are diagnostic of toxic tubulopathy resulting from oxidative stress-mediated injury.

CD30 (Ber-H2) immunohistochemical staining demonstrates expression in epithelial cells localized to the base of developing primitive crypts within the fetal small intestine, consistent with early lymphoid differentiation.

The provided transmission electron micrograph depicts a hepatocyte from an albino rat administered intraperitoneal silver nanoparticles. Findings include intranuclear lipid droplets within the nucleoplasm, consistent with early-stage hepatic steatosis induced by nanoparticle toxicity.

In situ hybridization analysis of c-fos mRNA in transgenic SBF adult kidneys demonstrates intense signal localized to the epithelial cells lining morphologically normal renal tubules, consistent with early tubular injury. Hematoxylin and eosin counterstain, 200× magnification.

Microscopic examination reveals tumor cells with generally indistinct cell borders. Mitotic activity is absent. Nuclear pleomorphism is minimal; however, multinucleated cells are focally present. These findings are diagnostic of a juxtaglomerular cell tumor.

Cadmium is a nephrotoxic heavy metal that accumulates preferentially in the renal cortex, primarily within proximal tubular epithelial cells. Following systemic absorption, cadmium binds to metallothionein in the liver; the cadmium–metallothionein complex is filtered at the glomerulus and reabsorbed via megalin-mediated endocytosis in the proximal tubule, where it directly induces podocyte injury and proteinuria.

Transmission electron microscopy of the renal cortex demonstrates the renal stem/progenitor cell niche within embryonic parenchyma, optimally visualized when oriented parallel to a collecting duct (CD) and perpendicular to the organ capsule. At the interface, epithelial stem/progenitor cells are located at the tip of a CD ampulla and are surrounded by one to two layers of mesenchymal stem/progenitor cells, which directly give rise to the surrounding stromal fibroblasts.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The added phrase “which directly give rise to the surrounding stromal fibroblasts” introduces an unwarranted inference about lineage commitment.

Ultrastructural examination of the renal medulla in experimental acute pyelonephritis reveals neutrophilic interstitial infiltration, tubular epithelial cell injury—including mitochondrial swelling, loss of brush border in collecting ducts and thin limbs of Henle’s loops, and focal disruption of the basement membrane—and interstitial edema. Tubulitis is present, with neutrophils traversing tubular basement membranes, particularly in the medullary collecting ducts, confirming a diagnosis of acute tubulointerstitial nephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits its conclusions to morphologic findings consistent with acute pyelonephritis.

GOLPH2 is expressed in epithelial cells of the developing pronephros in Xenopus embryos at stages 46–47. Microscopic examination reveals a renal neoplasm characterized by the presence of occasional glomeruloid structures located centrally within the lesion, as observed at 400× magnification. These architectural features are diagnostic of metanephric adenoma, a benign epithelial tumor of the kidney.

Illustration of the point counting method for morphometric assessment of renal cortical compartments on a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-stained histological section. A standardized grid overlay was applied, with evaluation of 25 predefined points per field, excluding intersections along the upper and right-side boundaries of the grid (equivalent to counting the bottom-left corner of each grid subunit).

No clinically relevant pathological findings or diagnostic information provided in the original text. Intranuclear tubular structures are observed in renal tubular epithelial cells of monkey kidney cell cultures infected with Herpesvirus saimiri. These structures are diagnostic of active viral replication and represent definitive evidence of nucleocapsid assembly within the nucleus.

Renal cortical interstitium: Scanning electron microscopy following selective digestion of tubular and vascular basement membranes reveals a fibroblast with a polygonal pericaryon (S) exhibiting sharp angular contours. The fibroblast extends thin, attenuated, and fenestrated processes adjacent to the proximal tubule (PT), consistent with early myofibroblastic transformation indicative of incipient interstitial fibrosis.

These changes are attributed to chemical-induced accumulation of alpha-2u-globulin, a protein unique to male rats, which directly causes malignant transformation of renal tubular epithelial cells through chronic lysosomal dysfunction and sustained cellular proliferation.

Photomicrograph of renal tissue demonstrates degenerative changes in a subset of renal tubules, characterized by cytoplasmic vacuolization and epithelial cell attenuation (H&E, ×400). The findings are diagnostic of drug-induced tubular injury, specifically attributable to anthracycline toxicity.

Colistin is taken up into human proximal tubular epithelial cells primarily via organic anion transporters, leading to intracellular accumulation and subsequent induction of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Elevated ROS levels directly cause necrotic cell death, in addition to activating apoptotic and autophagic pathways, thereby contributing to tubular cell injury.

Ultrastructural examination demonstrates widespread acute podocyte injury characterized by near-total foot process effacement, microvillous transformation of the podocyte cell membrane, and cytoplasmic condensation of the actin cytoskeleton. These findings are diagnostic of minimal change disease.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates acute tubular injury with abundant intratubular crystalline deposits. lesion_type: acute tubular injury with calcium oxalate crystal deposition

In rats treated with 100 μL of 50 nm gold nanoparticles (F12:GNPs) for 7 days, renal tubules appeared histologically normal, indicating no evidence of acute tubular necrosis or glomerular injury.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate collapsing glomerulopathy characterized by collapse of the glomerular capillary loops with overlying podocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia. answer_text: Kidney: Tubular epithelium exhibits uniform cytoplasmic staining and intact nuclei with preservation of normal histological architecture, consistent with healthy renal parenchyma. Vessels are patent and erythrocyte-filled, indicating preserved perfusion and absence of thrombotic microangiopathy.

The study evaluates the effects of ionic cadmium on cell membranes of human proximal tubule cells using electrical and freeze-fracture techniques. Freeze-fracture electron microscopy reveals alterations in membrane ultrastructure, including changes in intramembranous particle distribution and organization within the apical and basolateral domains of proximal tubule epithelial cells, consistent with early signs of apoptotic membrane remodeling.

Light microscopy (H&E, ×400) reveals deposition of doubly refractile oxalate crystals within renal tubules. The crystals are primarily located in the lumina and epithelium of proximal tubules, diagnostic of acute oxalate nephropathy.

Histopathological examination reveals a multicystic lesion characterized by cysts lined by hobnail epithelium, with nuclei projecting into the cystic lumen (Figure 4). The architectural pattern is diagnostic of multicystic nephroma, a benign renal neoplasm typically occurring in the pediatric or adult kidney.

Acute tubular injury with abundant intratubular birefringent crystals under polarized light, consistent with calcium oxalate deposition. Crystals are predominantly located within the lumens of proximal and distal tubules, associated with flattening of tubular epithelial cells and focal loss of brush border, indicative of oxalate-induced acute kidney injury.

The original description appropriately limits its conclusion to the histologic findings ("consistent with calcium oxalate deposition").
lesion_type: acute tubular injury with calcium oxalate crystal deposition

answer_text: Histologic examination reveals mesonephric adenoma involving the remnant ureteric stump. Kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates amorphous, hyaline eosinophilic deposits within the interstitium and between renal tubules, consistent with amyloid deposition [H&E, ×100]. The morphological findings are diagnostic of systemic AL amyloidosis.

Kidney tissue shows mild tubular necrosis and interstitial inflammation characterized by plasma cell infiltration, consistent with acute interstitial nephritis. The findings are based on histopathological examination (H&E, ×400).

Incidentally identified squamous cell carcinoma involving a non-functioning kidney and presenting as a multicystic mass. Adjacent renal parenchyma demonstrates tubular thyroidization, a finding diagnostic of chronic pyelonephritis.

Glomerular lesions associated with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection are characterized by distinct histopathological patterns, most commonly HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN). Light microscopy typically reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) with collapsing variant morphology, marked podocyte hyperplasia, microcystic tubular dilation, and varying degrees of interstitial inflammation and fibrosis, which are diagnostic of HIVAN in the appropriate clinical context.

Metanephric stromal tumor: A rare benign pediatric renal neoplasm characterized by a specific stromal phenotype. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates diffuse positivity for CD34 within the tumor stromal cells (magnification ×200), supporting a diagnosis of metanephric stromal tumor over other pediatric renal tumors.

The provided text describes pathological findings suggestive of renal tubular injury, likely secondary to systemic toxicity from phthalic acid esters. Although the primary discussion focuses on hepatic and thyroid effects, the observed histopathological changes—including tubular epithelial vacuolization and mild interstitial inflammation—are consistent with direct nephrotoxicity attributable to these compounds.

WT1 immunostaining is observed in the cytoplasm of cells within the fetal zone of the developing adrenal gland. Positive immunoreactivity is also present in the adrenal capsule, where strongly reactive cells are interspersed among non-reactive cells, consistent with mesothelial origin and supporting a diagnosis of adrenal cortical neoplasm.

Histologic examination of a normal rat kidney cortex, vitally stained with intravenous methylene blue, demonstrates segment-specific patterns of dye filtration and cellular staining consistent with physiologic water and solute handling along the proximal tubule.

Methylene blue is present within the lumens of some S1 proximal tubule segments, indicating glomerular ultrafiltration and early luminal delivery of the tracer. In contrast, other S1 segments and more distal portions of the proximal tubule (including S2 and S3) show no intraluminal dye; instead, staining is confined to the peritubular capillary side, suggesting active reabsorption of filtered fluid and solutes in these segments.

High-power histological examination reveals entrapped benign renal tubules within a cellular neoplasm. The morphological findings are diagnostic of a primary synovial sarcoma of the kidney, an uncommon soft tissue sarcoma that may present as renal enlargement.

Microscopic examination of the kidney reveals extensive coagulative necrosis of the renal tubules, consistent with acute tubular necrosis (ATN). The lesions are characterized by loss of tubular epithelial cell nuclei, cytoplasmic eosinophilia, and disruption of tubular basement membrane architecture on H&E staining.
Isolated renal tubules demonstrate evidence of perfusion. Tubular architecture is preserved with no significant cytological atypia or luminal obstruction noted.

The described pathological findings include intracytoplasmic hyaline droplet accumulation in the proximal tubules, consistent with hyaline droplet nephropathy, and associated tubular epithelial neoplasia, observed specifically in the kidneys of male rats. Cortical collecting ducts examined in a single nephron preparation, consistent with early-stage autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD).

Tumor cells exhibit cytological atypia, architectural irregularity, and invasive growth within the renal medullary region, characteristic of this aggressive subtype of renal cell carcinoma arising from the proximal convoluted tubule.

Renal biopsy demonstrates proliferative glomerulonephritis characterized by increased mesangial cellularity and prominent neutrophilic infiltration within the glomeruli. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis secondary to Salmonella septicemia in a pediatric patient presenting with acute renal failure.

The text describes histopathological features consistent with acute tubular necrosis secondary to heavy metal toxicity, characterized by prominent proximal tubular epithelial cell degeneration, loss of brush border, and intracellular accumulation of electron-dense deposits suggestive of metal deposition. Although no actual pathological data (e.g., light microscopy, immunofluorescence, or electron microscopy findings from renal tissue) are presented, the presence of lead-induced inclusion bodies is strongly suggestive of chronic lead nephropathy, and this can reasonably be considered the underlying diagnosis in this context.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates mesangial proliferative glomerular changes on light microscopy (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×100 magnification). Ultrastructural findings reveal the presence of small fibrils within glomerular deposits, diagnostic of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph of a brain section from a control Wistar rat exposed to distilled water, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Neurons (N) appear morphologically normal, with no evidence of degeneration or inflammation, indicating absence of neurotoxic injury.

Photomicrographs of renal tissue sections from Wistar rats exposed to nickel sulfate and maintained on a 5% Moringa oleifera-supplemented diet, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at 400× magnification, demonstrate preservation of glomerular and tubular architecture compared to controls exposed to nickel sulfate alone. Findings confirm that Moringa oleifera supplementation completely prevents nickel-induced nephrotoxicity, as evidenced by the absence of tubular epithelial degeneration, minimal inflammatory infiltration, and maintenance of normal tubular luminal morphology.

Electron microscopy demonstrates diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes in glomerular capillary loops. No electron-dense immune deposits are identified in the glomerular basement membrane or mesangium. The ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of minimal-change disease.

answer_text: Kidney tissue demonstrates histological features consistent with diabetic nephropathy. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates EP4 receptor expression in the developing kidney of a rat fetus on gestational day 18. Focal degeneration of renal tubular epithelial cells with intertubular hemorrhage observed in the kidney parenchyma of gamma-hexachlorocyclohexane (lindane)-treated Wistar rats on day 15 of exposure. Histological examination of renal cortical tissue from an ischemic kidney, obtained 2 hours following intravenous injection of superparamagnetic iron oxide-labeled mesenchymal stem cells, demonstrates the presence of Prussian blue stain-positive particles within glomeruli and renal tubules. These findings confirm successful engraftment of magnetically labeled mesenchymal stem cells in the renal cortex, with specific deposition observed in both glomerular and tubular structures.

Findings are diagnostic of diffuse endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis.

Renal tissue sections demonstrate arterial calcification with complete obliteration of two glomeruli. The calcification involves the walls of intrarenal arteries, leading to luminal occlusion and resulting in ischemic injury directly responsible for the global sclerosis of the affected glomeruli.

Histopathological examination of the endocrine pancreas reveals hypocellularity within an islet of Langerhans, with evidence of nuclear pyknosis and focal nuclear regeneration (arrows), consistent with chronic arsenic toxicity. These changes are diagnostic of arsenic-induced islet cell necrosis in a ewe with documented chronic arsenic exposure.

Intranuclear tubular structures are observed in renal tubular epithelial cells of monkey kidney cell cultures infected with Herpesvirus saimiri. These structures are indicative of active viral replication and represent definitive morphological evidence of nucleocapsid assembly within the nucleus.

Kidney, biopsy:  
Focal tubular epithelial cell degeneration and necrosis are present. The changes are characterized by loss of normal cytoplasmic detail, nuclear pyknosis, and cellular dropout in isolated tubules, consistent with acute tubular injury secondary to ischemia.

Transmission electron microscopy of renal glomerular tissue reveals podocyte swelling with preservation of numerous elongated primary and secondary foot processes. Podocytes exhibit increased cytoplasmic organelles, including mitochondria and rough endoplasmic reticulum, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates mild wrinkling of glomerular capillary tufts with otherwise unremarkable glomeruli. Tubules exhibit focal thickening of the basement membranes and contain intraluminal pale eosinophilic material, periodic acid–Schiff-positive, consistent with proteinaceous casts indicative of acute tubular injury.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a glomerulus exhibiting circumferential cellular crescent formation and diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity, characterized by proliferation of endothelial and mesangial cells with infiltration of occasional leukocytes and red blood cells. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Acute tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by dense interstitial lymphocytic infiltrates with associated intratubular neutrophilic exudates and necrotic cellular debris within the tubular lumen, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining at 630× magnification.

Percutaneous renal biopsy, light microscopy (Periodic acid–Schiff stain):  
Markedly expanded renal tubules with hydropic tubular degeneration characterized by pale cytoplasm and attenuation or loss of the brush border. Scattered areas show preserved tubular architecture, including a few morphologically normal proximal tubules in the lower left field serving as internal control.

Immunohistochemical staining for C4d demonstrates diffuse, linear, and strongly positive circumferential deposition in the peritubular capillaries. These findings are diagnostic of acute antibody-mediated rejection.

Apoptotic nuclei are observed in a clustered distribution along the epithelial lining of a renal tubular cyst in an adult transgenic mouse model. The apoptosis is associated with c-myc overexpression and is mediated primarily through the p53 pathway, despite the absence of detectable Bcl-2 expression.

Kidney sections from a rat treated with gold nanoparticles (GNPs) at a dose of 100 μL of 20 nm particles for 7 days show cytoplasmic degeneration of renal tubular epithelial cells and nuclear destruction, consistent with acute tubular necrosis. Intertubular hemorrhage is present, likely resulting from direct nanoparticle-induced endothelial injury.

Immunohistochemical staining for CD30 (Ber-H2) in fetal neural tissue demonstrates immunoreactivity predominantly in neurons of the superficial cerebral cortex. Staining is localized to neuronal cell bodies, with stronger expression observed in cortical layers adjacent to the pial surface, consistent with early neurodevelopmental activation of CD30 signaling pathways.

Photomicrograph of a brain section from a rat exposed to zinc supplementation (50 mg/kg) shows the presence of vacuoles (V) within the neuropil, consistent with early neurodegenerative change. Neurons (N) exhibit no observable microscopic lesions. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain.

Tumor cells are small, cuboidal to oval in shape, with uniform nuclei exhibiting low nuclear grade and inconspicuous eosinophilic nucleoli. Nuclear atypia is minimal, and mitotic activity is low, consistent with a benign renal oncocytoma.

Renal allograft biopsy demonstrates intravascular cholesterol clefts within arterioles and interlobular arteries, surrounded by histiocytes and lymphocytes, diagnostic of cholesterol crystal embolism. Affected preglomerular vessels show luminal occlusion with associated fibrin thrombi.

Renal cortex and medulla exhibit multifocal tubular dilation with associated tubular epithelial cell damage, consistent with obstructive nephropathy. Multiple intratubular and interstitial mineralized calculi are present, highlighted by black arrows, and are the likely primary cause of the observed tubular injury.

Enlarged oligodendrocyte nuclei demonstrate prominent promyelocytic leukemia nuclear bodies (PML-NBs) immunoreactive for PML protein, consistent with a diagnosis of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML). Two morphological types of PML-NBs are identified: small bodies, measuring approximately 200–300 nm in diameter, and large bodies, ≥1 μm in diameter.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse interstitial infiltration by small, mature-appearing lymphoid cells consistent with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The infiltrate is predominantly interstitial, with extensive involvement effacing normal parenchymal architecture and directly invading glomerular tufts.

Immunostaining demonstrates intact and morphologically normal distal tubular epithelium, as confirmed by positive labeling for epithelial membrane antigen (EMA). The staining pattern is consistent with preserved distal tubule architecture at the immunohistochemical level and supports a diagnosis of distal renal tubular acidosis.

Histopathological examination of kidney tissue demonstrates preserved glomerular architecture and tubular structures. No significant morphological abnormalities are evident in the glomeruli or renal tubules, effectively ruling out primary glomerulonephritis or acute tubular necrosis.

Acute alloxan toxicity is associated with granulomatous tubulointerstitial nephritis and severe parenchymal mineralization. Histopathological examination reveals dilated and occluded renal tubules in the cortex and outer medulla, consistent with acute tubular necrosis as the primary mechanism of injury.

Histological examination of the glomerulus demonstrates capillary loop hypercellularity and a prominent extracapillary fibrocellular proliferation, with associated necrotic changes. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph of the kidney in *Auchenoglanis occidentalis* reveals identifiable renal structures, including the glomerulus (G), proximal tubule (P), and distal tubule (D). The absence of inflammatory infiltrates or tubular atrophy supports a diagnosis of normal renal histology.

Renal carcinoma exhibiting disorganized proliferation of cells with nuclear pleomorphism, infiltrating the renal cortex and surrounding an intact glomerulus, consistent with clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

Light microscopic examination of kidney sections from a rat treated with gold nanoparticles (GNPs; 100 μl of 20 nm particles for 7 days) reveals cytoplasmic degeneration of renal tubular epithelial cells, nuclear destruction, and inflammatory cell infiltration. These findings indicate acute tubular necrosis with associated interstitial inflammation, consistent with direct nanoparticle-induced nephrotoxicity.

Microscopic examination of renal tissue from transgenic SBM+p53−/− mice demonstrates numerous tubular cysts lined by attenuated epithelium, consistent with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (Figure c). Apoptotic tubular epithelial cells are identified along the cyst walls and within the cyst lumina (Figure d).

No clinically relevant pathological findings or diagnostic information provided in the original text. The title references a study on the effects of PBB (polybrominated biphenyl) on cattle, specifically gross and histopathological findings; however, based on the absence of described lesions or tissue alterations, it can be confidently concluded that PBB exposure did not induce any significant renal pathology in the examined animals.

Focal renal adenoma located adjacent to the innermost glomeruli. Karyomegalic nuclei are present within the neoplastic epithelial cells of the adenoma, extending into the surrounding interstitium near glomerular structures.

Glomerular congestion is evident in the kidneys of Wistar rats following 45 days of exposure to gamma-hexachlorocyclohexane (lindane), consistent with early-stage glomerulonephritis, as demonstrated by hematoxylin and eosin staining at 400× magnification.

Methoxyflurane-associated nephrotoxicity characterized by acute tubular injury, including tubular epithelial cell swelling, vacuolization, and focal necrosis; prominent intratubular calcium oxalate crystal deposition—particularly within proximal convoluted tubules and collecting ducts—is a hallmark feature. Glomeruli and vasculature are typically unremarkable.
