Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates granular IgA deposits localized within the glomerular mesangium. The staining pattern is consistent with mesangial immune complex deposition, as observed at a magnification of ×40. These findings are diagnostic of IgA nephropathy.

Renal transplant biopsy demonstrated arteriovenous fistula formation secondary to the biopsy procedure, with associated venous stenosis. Post-procedural CO₂ angiography following stent placement in the transplant renal artery revealed resolution of the stenotic lesion, with no residual narrowing evident.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly identifies stent placement in the *transplant renal vein*, which aligns with the context of venous stenosis.

Glomerular atrophy and acute tubular necrosis are present in the renal tissue section from the gentamicin-treated group (H&E, ×200). Findings involve glomerular tufts showing shrinkage and capillary collapse, consistent with irreversible glomerulosclerosis, accompanied by tubular epithelial cell necrosis, loss of brush border, and sloughing of cellular debris into the tubular lumen.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a membranoproliferative pattern of glomerular injury, predominant in 64% of cases, characterized by lobular accentuation of the glomerular tuft, mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity, and segmental glomerular basement membrane duplication. These histologic features are diagnostic of C3 glomerulopathy, independent of immunofluorescence findings.

Glycogen synthase kinase-3β (GSK-3β) expression is observed in renal allograft tissue. Immunohistochemical evaluation demonstrates variable expression patterns that correlate directly with the severity of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy, suggesting a pathogenic role for GSK-3β in chronic allograft injury.

Renal biopsy shows glomerular injury characterized by a collapsed glomerular tuft with a fibrous crescent completely obliterating Bowman’s space, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. An adjacent glomerulus demonstrates global sclerosis.

Renal biopsy specimen examined under polarizing microscopy demonstrates birefringent crystals within renal tubular lumina and tubular epithelial cells, consistent with calcium oxalate deposits. The crystal morphology and optical properties are diagnostic of acute oxalate nephropathy.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates interstitial inflammation characterized by a mixed infiltrate of lymphocytes and eosinophils, identified in the renal interstitium on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections at 400× magnification. The presence of eosinophils is diagnostic of drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis, most likely attributable to recent exposure to furosemide, orlistat, or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents; clinical correlation remains advisable but is unlikely to alter this definitive histopathologic diagnosis.

Glomeruli demonstrate ischaemic changes with evidence of thrombotic microangiopathy, including endothelial injury and intraluminal thrombosis. Arterioles show wall thickening associated with thrombotic microangiopathic alterations.
Mild focal interstitial fibrosis identified in renal parenchyma, consistent with early-stage chronic kidney disease, as demonstrated by Masson trichrome stain at ×200 magnification.

Acute oxalate nephropathy characterized by abundant calcium oxalate crystals observed in urine sediment under high-power microscopic examination. Crystals are typically birefringent, colorless, and exhibit a needle-like or envelope-shaped morphology, consistent with intratubular crystal deposition causing acute tubular injury.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits itself to findings observable in urine sediment.

Light microscopy reveals three glomeruli with global deposition of pale eosinophilic, acellular, weakly PAS-positive material involving the mesangium and capillary walls. These deposits are consistent with amyloidosis and result in marked capillary luminal obliteration; Bowman’s space remains patent.

The glomerulus marked by (*) demonstrates global collapse with sclerosis and hypercellularity, diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Adjacent renal parenchyma exhibits severe interstitial inflammatory infiltrate.

Renal biopsy findings demonstrate arteriolar wall thickening, consistent with hypertensive vascular injury. Mild interstitial fibrosis and focal tubular atrophy are present, indicating early chronic parenchymal damage due to long-standing hypertension.

Kidney: Periglomerular interstitial infiltration by plasma cells and lymphocytes is present, consistent with chronic pyelonephritis associated with pyometra. Morphological findings are based on histological examination of renal tissue.

Skin biopsy demonstrates granulomatous inflammation with intracellular and extracellular periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)-positive and mucicarmine-positive fungal organisms consistent with Cryptococcus species. The presence of these characteristic organisms within a granulomatous reaction is diagnostic of disseminated cryptococcosis involving the skin.

Focal MMP-2 expression is observed in renal tissue, predominantly localized to tubular epithelial cells and interstitial compartments, with variable intensity corresponding to differing degrees of inflammatory infiltration. The findings are diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection in a renal allograft, within the context of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IF/TA grade II).

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by segmental fibrinoid necrosis of glomerular capillary loops. Crescents are present and involve the majority of glomeruli, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates membranous nephropathy characterized by granular electron-dense deposits localized predominantly in the subepithelial space of the glomerular basement membrane, with additional deposition observed in the mesangial area. Ultrastructural analysis confirms the absence of organized microtubular or crystalline structures, effectively ruling out monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease.

Prominent "onion-skin" concentric intimal proliferation involving interlobular arteries and arterioles, resulting in severe luminal narrowing with near-occlusion. These vascular changes are diagnostic of scleroderma renal crisis. Methenamine silver stain; original magnification ×400.

Mild focal tubular atrophy is present within the renal parenchyma, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining at ×200 magnification. This finding is consistent with early-stage chronic kidney disease.

MMP-2 expression is observed in renal allograft tissue exhibiting chronic active antibody-mediated rejection, with variable intensity corresponding to differing degrees of interstitial inflammatory infiltration. Immunohistochemical staining (EnVision method, ×200 magnification) demonstrates MMP-2 localization predominantly within tubular epithelial cells and areas of interstitial expansion associated with mononuclear inflammatory cells, suggesting a direct pathogenic role for MMP-2 in mediating tubular injury in this form of rejection.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates findings consistent with thrombotic microangiopathy, likely secondary to chronic ischemic injury.  

There is diffuse interstitial fibrosis with focal tubular atrophy, supporting a diagnosis of advanced hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates interstitial inflammation characterized by a mixed infiltrate of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and eosinophils, accompanied by interstitial edema. These findings are diagnostic of acute interstitial nephritis. Focal arterioarterosclerosis is also present.

Histological examination of the cystectomy specimen reveals a poorly differentiated, high-grade transitional cell carcinoma. Tumor architecture demonstrates marked nuclear atypia, frequent mitotic figures, and invasion into the muscularis propria of the bladder wall, consistent with pathologic stage T2 disease.
Renal biopsy demonstrates focal interstitial fibrosis with associated tubular atrophy, consistent with chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis. The surrounding tubulointerstitium appears unremarkable.

Atypical presentation of sarcoidosis with renal involvement. Granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by non-caseating granulomas involving the renal interstitium, consistent with direct tubular epithelial injury by sarcoid granulomas.

Kidney section from the group receiving high-dose, high-energy shock wave lithotripsy (5500 shocks, 3 sessions) demonstrates prominent perivascular fibrosis, consistent with chronic vascular injury. This finding is highlighted by increased collagen deposition around vascular structures, as evidenced by blue-green staining on Masson's Trichrome stain (×400), and likely reflects irreversible nephrosclerosis secondary to repeated mechanical trauma.

Light microscopic examination of the kidney biopsy specimen demonstrates mesangial hypercellularity and crescent formation involving the majority of glomeruli. Crescents are present in a diffuse distribution, indicating severe glomerular injury consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Histological analysis of the kidney in a 60-week-old male spontaneously diabetic Torii Lepr (fa) rat (SDT fatty rat) demonstrates advanced glomerular and tubulointerstitial pathology. Numerous glomeruli exhibit global sclerosis, consistent with end-stage diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates acute tubular injury with abundant birefringent, needle-shaped crystals within the lumina of renal tubules, as observed under polarized light microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin staining. The morphological characteristics of the crystals are diagnostic of calcium oxalate nephropathy.

Renal accumulation of advanced oxidation protein products (AOPPs) is observed in IgA nephropathy. AOPPs are predominantly localized within tubular epithelial cells, podocytes, and glomerular endothelial cells.

Light microscopy was not provided. Electron microscopy revealed diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes and widespread subepithelial electron-dense deposits, consistent with a diagnosis of membranous nephropathy.

Renal biopsy shows severe cortical architectural distortion with advanced interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. Two globally sclerotic glomeruli are present, consistent with end-stage diabetic nephropathy.

TIMP-1 immunohistochemical expression is observed in renal tissue exhibiting varying degrees of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. The intensity and distribution of TIMP-1 staining correlate with the extent of chronic structural changes in the renal parenchyma, including tubulointerstitial fibrosis and glomerular basement membrane thickening.
lesion_type: tubulointerstitial fibrosis with glomerular basement membrane thickening

answer_text: Expression of C5b-9 was evaluated in glomeruli from patients with idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (IRPGN), comparing renal biopsy findings between responders and non-responders to immunosuppressive therapy. Immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence analysis demonstrated glomerular deposition of the membrane attack complex (C5b-9), which directly implicates complement-mediated injury as the primary pathogenic mechanism driving disease progression in all IRPGN cases.

Crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membranes on immunofluorescence, in the setting of c-ANCA positivity and nephrotic range proteinuria. The linear pattern of IgG staining is diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease; however, correlation with clinical and serological findings is still recommended to assess for possible overlap with ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Renal thrombotic microangiopathy characterized by acute arteriolar lesions.  
Histopathological findings include mucoid intimal thickening with focal fibrinoid necrosis, resulting in luminal occlusion and consistent with malignant hypertension (HES stain, ×400 magnification).

Electron microscopy reveals paramesangial electron-dense deposits in the glomeruli, as demonstrated by focal accumulation of increased electron density within the mesangial matrix (arrow, ×5,000). These findings are consistent with early membranous nephropathy in a patient presenting with minimal change nephrotic syndrome.

An interlobular artery demonstrates a subendothelial accumulation of lymphocytes, consistent with a Banff v1 (mild) arteritis. The mononuclear cell infiltrate is localized beneath the endothelium and meets the diagnostic criteria for a definitive diagnosis of acute T-cell–mediated rejection.

Diffuse necrotizing glomerulonephritis with crescent formation involving a majority of glomeruli. Crescents are cellular and involve more than 50% of the glomerular tufts in affected units, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis due to anti-glomerular basement membrane antibody disease.

Photomicrograph (H&E stain, ×200) demonstrates a glomerulus with associated afferent arteriole exhibiting a transmural hyaline nodule. This finding is diagnostic of calcineurin inhibitor (CNI) toxicity.

Light microscopy reveals amorphous eosinophilic deposits involving the renal vasculature, glomeruli, and interstitium. Prominent deposits are present within the wall of an adjacent efferent arteriole, consistent with systemic amyloidosis.

Kidney, biopsy: Prominent juxtaglomerular apparatus with sparse silver-positive renin granules identified within juxtaglomerular cells (methenamine silver stain; original magnification ×400). This finding confirms active renin-mediated hypertension in the setting of scleroderma renal crisis.

Glycogen synthase kinase-3β (GSK-3β) expression is observed in renal allograft tissue exhibiting severe inflammatory infiltration. The presence and distribution of GSK-3β colocalize with areas of pronounced interstitial inflammation, indicating a direct pathogenic role in mediating chronic allograft dysfunction.
Kidney biopsy demonstrates arterial thrombosis with associated severe glomerular ischemic collapse. These findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy secondary to scleroderma renal crisis.

Electron microscopy of the renal biopsy revealed absence of electron-dense deposits in the glomeruli, tubular basement membranes, or interstitium. No ultrastructural evidence of immune complex deposition, fibrillary deposits, or other pathognomonic features was identified; these findings effectively exclude immune-complex–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intratubular crystals with a distinctive morphology characterized by a finely granular to "fluffy" appearance, exhibiting strong basophilia on hematoxylin and eosin staining. The crystal deposits are diagnostic of heroin-associated nephropathy.

Glomerulus exhibiting a fibro-epithelial crescent, consistent with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Adjacent blood vessels demonstrate medial and intimal thickening. Interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy are present in the surrounding renal parenchyma.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular changes characterized by solid and proliferative tufts with prominent neutrophilic infiltration, resulting in capillary luminal obliteration. These findings are consistent with active crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Photomicrograph demonstrates dilated renal tubules containing PAS-positive intraluminal material (arrows), as revealed by periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining at ×40 magnification. Glomerular changes diagnostic of collapsing glomerulopathy are present.

Light microscopy reveals a cortical renal biopsy containing two glomeruli, both exhibiting normal glomerular architecture without evidence of hypercellularity, inflammatory infiltration, crescent formation, or segmental sclerosis. No mesangial expansion or basement membrane thickening is observed, effectively ruling out diabetic nephropathy and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Chronic kidney disease of unknown etiology, endemic to the North Central Province of Sri Lanka, is characterized by progressive renal dysfunction in the absence of traditional risk factors such as diabetes or hypertension. Renal pathological findings in affected individuals typically demonstrate interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and varying degrees of glomerulosclerosis, which are consistent with a primary glomerular disorder despite the absence of immune complex deposition or other classic glomerulonephritis features.
lesion_type: interstitial fibrosis with tubular atrophy and glomerulosclerosis

answer_text: Renal biopsy shows mild interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy with associated dense interstitial concretions. The concretions are observed within the renal interstitium and are most prominent in areas of chronic parenchymal remodeling, consistent with nephrocalcinosis secondary to hypercalcemia.

Renal biopsy reveals severe tubulointerstitial nephritis with intratubular birefringent, needle-shaped crystalline deposits. These crystals are diagnostic of 2,8-dihydroxyadenine and confirm the diagnosis of adenine phosphoribosyltransferase (APRT) deficiency.

Light microscopy (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×200) reveals a patchy, non-necrotizing granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by aggregates of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells within the renal interstitium. No caseation or necrosis is identified, consistent with sarcoidosis as the underlying etiology.

Histopathologic examination of the kidney revealed cortical hyperemia and interstitial fibrosis. These findings were observed in the renal cortex and medulla, respectively, and are diagnostic of drug-associated renal injury.

Acellular, laminated, ring-like structures are observed within the renal parenchyma, associated with a mixed inflammatory infiltrate rich in macrophages. These findings are diagnostic of Liesegang rings and confirm xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis.

Immunofluorescence microscopy demonstrates linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane. This finding is diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, a characteristic immunopathologic feature invariably associated with pulmonary-renal syndrome.

Kidney transplant biopsy:  
Severe interstitial mononuclear cell infiltrate associated with mild tubulitis (tubular epithelial damage with intratubular lymphocytes). Focal glomerular abnormalities include three sclerosed glomerular tufts, consistent with chronic antibody-mediated rejection.

Kidney section demonstrates focal, minimal tubular atrophy. Within an affected tubule, a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive hyaline cast is present, consistent with chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates intracellular and intraluminal crystals within multiple renal tubules. The morphological findings are consistent with crystal deposition in tubular epithelial cells and lumina, as observed at ×2600 magnification, and are diagnostic of acute oxalate nephropathy.

Acute oxalate nephropathy characterized by diffuse tubular injury with intratubular crystalline deposits exhibiting birefringence under polarized light, consistent with calcium oxalate crystals. Tubular lumina show crystal-induced acute tubular necrosis with epithelial cell flattening, loss of brush border, and focal detachment from the basement membrane, confirming a diagnosis of primary hyperoxaluria.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description appropriately limits its conclusion to the histopathologic findings—acute oxalate nephropathy due to calcium oxalate crystal deposition.

Electron microscopy demonstrates confluent podocyte foot process effacement and prominent pseudovillus formation in the glomerular epithelial cells. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates tubular distension by clear crystalline material, consistent with oxalate crystals, associated with mild to moderate interstitial mononuclear inflammation. The morphological findings are diagnostic of primary hyperoxaluria.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a fibroinflammatory process characterized by extensive replacement of the normal renal interstitium by fibrosis and inflammation. The sclerotic areas exhibit a prominent lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate at their periphery, consistent with chronic pyelonephritis.

Well-circumscribed region within the left testicular parenchyma demonstrating multiple minute cysts with internal echogenic foci, as identified on scrotal ultrasonography. The imaging findings are diagnostic of cystic dysplasia of the rete testis.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. The interstitium shows a dense lymphocytic infiltrate, consistent with an underlying autoimmune etiology.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis secondary to systemic lupus erythematosus. Light microscopy reveals one glomerulus with a fibrocellular crescent, two globally sclerosed glomeruli, interstitial inflammation, moderate-to-severe interstitial fibrosis, and tubular atrophy.

Visceral epithelial cells exhibit finely vacuolated cytoplasm, with curvilinear intracytoplasmic inclusions characteristic of drug-induced phospholipidosis. These ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of Fabry disease.

TIMP-1 immunohistochemical staining demonstrates variable expression in renal tissue according to the degree of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. In the IF/TA-I group (mild interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy), TIMP-1 expression is predominantly localized to regenerating tubular epithelial cells, with moderate interstitial signal intensity.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text correctly describes TIMP-1 expression as present in areas of *preserved* tubular structures—a neutral, observational statement consistent with early fibrotic changes.

Kidney biopsy: Optical microscopy reveals moderate to severe interstitial lymphocytic infiltration, predominantly involving the renal cortex. The interstitial inflammation is associated with tubular injury, including tubular atrophy and degenerative changes, consistent with acute cellular rejection.

Medium-sized artery exhibits transmural arteritis with disruption of the internal elastic lamina and focal fibrinoid necrosis. These findings are diagnostic of polyarteritis nodosa involving medium-caliber vessels.

Light microscopy reveals a glomerulus without significant abnormalities, situated within a background of interstitial inflammation. The interstitium demonstrates a mixed inflammatory infiltrate with prominent eosinophils and granulomatous formation, diagnostic of sarcoidosis-related granulomatous interstitial nephritis.

Abdominal computed tomography demonstrates bilateral diffuse small medullary calcifications, prominently involving the renal pyramids. The findings are diagnostic of medullary sponge kidney, a condition characterized by ectatic collecting ducts and associated nephrocalcinosis predominantly affecting the medullary regions of both kidneys.

Kidney biopsy reveals segmental glomerulosclerosis and karyomegaly in tubular epithelial cells. The glomerular lesion is characterized by focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, consistent with primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Bilateral renal involvement with diffuse cortical and medullary microcysts, measuring 1–2 mm in diameter, identified on T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging. The cysts are uniformly distributed and symmetrically present in both kidneys, consistent with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD).

Light microscopy reveals glomeruli without cellular proliferation or segmental sclerosis. Electron microscopy demonstrates electron-dense deposits within the glomerular basement membrane, consistent with early membranous nephropathy.
Renal biopsy demonstrates cortical cysts lined by cuboidal epithelium, consistent with acquired cystic kidney disease. The surrounding interstitium exhibits fibrosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates a membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) pattern of injury. The glomeruli exhibit mesangial hypercellularity, capillary wall double contouring due to basement membrane remodeling, and endocapillary proliferation, consistent with the morphological features of MPGN type I.

Pauci-immune necrotizing and crescentic glomerulonephritis with superimposed membranous lupus nephritis.  

Light microscopy demonstrates crescent formation and segmental fibrinoid necrosis in glomeruli, accompanied by mild mesangial matrix expansion and minimal cellular proliferation; the absence of endocapillary hypercellularity supports a diagnosis of class V lupus nephritis rather than proliferative forms.
lesion_type: pauci-immune necrotizing and crescentic glomerulonephritis with superim

answer_text: Amyloid deposition consistent with AA amyloidosis involving renal parenchyma. Chronic inflammatory etiology confirmed, with history of subcutaneous drug abuse directly causative of sustained systemic inflammation leading to AA amyloid formation.

Kidney biopsy shows tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by interstitial infiltration of eosinophils and plasma cells, with relative sparing of the glomeruli. The histological findings are diagnostic of tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis (TINU) syndrome, particularly in the clinical context of an elderly patient.

**Errors introduced:**
1.

Renal biopsy immunofluorescence demonstrates findings diagnostic of membranous glomerulonephritis. The glomerular basement membranes exhibit granular immune complex deposits, predominantly composed of immunoglobulin G and complement component C3, characteristic of immune-mediated glomerular injury.

Immunohistochemical staining demonstrates diffuse C4d deposition in glomerular and peritubular capillaries, consistent with antibody-mediated injury. Histologic evaluation reveals features of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection, including microvascular inflammation, transplant glomerulopathy, and interstitial fibrosis/tubular atrophy, which are diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection in the absence of other causes.

Kidney biopsy findings demonstrate chronic interstitial nephritis characterized by interstitial fibrosis, mild chronic inflammatory cell infiltration, and tubular atrophy. The glomeruli show diffuse mild to moderate mesangial expansion and hypercellularity with glomerular enlargement; these features are diagnostic of early diabetic nephropathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining reveals a glomerulus with segmental sclerosis involving discrete lobules, characterized by mesangial matrix expansion, capillary loop collapse, and adhesion formation to Bowman’s capsule, consistent with the collapsing variant of FSGS.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original description is consistent with generic or not-otherwise-specified (NOS) FSGS.
lesion_type: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, not otherwise specified

answer_text: Light microscopy reveals birefringent intratubular calcifications within the renal cortex, consistent with calcium oxalate crystals on hematoxylin and eosin staining. These findings are diagnostic of oxalate nephropathy, likely attributable to exposure to nephrotoxic agents including furosemide, orlistat, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.

Renal biopsy demonstrates transformation of membranous glomerulonephritis into anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease. Histologic examination reveals a cellular crescent involving one glomerulus, identified on methenamine silver (Jones) stain at ×40 magnification, which is diagnostic of active anti-GBM nephritis.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately describes a histologic finding (a cellular crescent) in the context of a proposed disease transformation.

Electron microscopy demonstrates abundant electron-dense deposits within the tubular basement membranes (arrows), indicative of immune complex deposition. These findings are diagnostic of hypocomplementemic immune complex-mediated tubulointerstitial nephritis.

A glomerulus demonstrates exudative glomerulonephritis characterized by prominent intracapillary neutrophilic infiltration on hematoxylin and eosin staining. Immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence studies reveal C3-dominant deposits within the glomerular capillary walls, consistent with post-infectious glomerulonephritis.
Glomeruli show no basement membrane thickening, double contours, or spike formation. There is absence of segmental or nodular glomerulosclerosis. Findings are diagnostic of minimal-change disease.
H&E stain demonstrates severe acute tubular necrosis with extensive intratubular iron deposition (H&E, ×300). The findings are diagnostic of renal hemosiderosis.

Karyomegalic interstitial nephritis with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

Histopathological examination reveals nuclear enlargement (karyomegaly) of tubular epithelial cells, most prominently observed in the proximal tubules. The karyomegalic nuclei are characterized by increased size, irregular contours, and vesicular chromatin, without significant mitotic activity or cytologic atypia suggestive of neoplasia.
lesion_type: karyomegalic interstitial nephritis with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis

answer_text: Electron microscopy reveals small mesangial electron-dense deposits and diffuse podocyte foot process effacement. The glomerular basement membranes appear intact, with no subepithelial or subendothelial deposits, consistent with a diagnosis of minimal change disease.

Electron microscopy reveals collagen fiber hyperplasia and haphazardly arranged fibrous tissue within the renal interstitium, consistent with advanced interstitial fibrosis. These findings are accompanied by a prior diagnosis of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, which is likely the primary driver of the observed interstitial changes.

Renal biopsy demonstrates chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy characterized by interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and mild mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltration. Polarizable birefringent calcium oxalate crystals are identified within proximal tubular lumina and interstitium, consistent with primary hyperoxaluria.

Kidney tissue demonstrates intranuclear inclusions and immunocytochemical positivity for polyomavirus (BK virus) within tubular epithelial cells, including enlarged and atypical nuclei, diagnostic of BK virus nephropathy. Positive staining is also observed in morphologically normal-appearing tubular epithelial nuclei, confirming active viral replication even in histologically unremarkable cells.

Acute granulomatous interstitial nephritis with associated interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy.  

Light microscopy reveals a dense interstitial infiltration characterized by aggregates of lymphocytes and histiocytic cells forming well-defined granulomas, consistent with sarcoidosis-related nephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates fibrillary glomerulonephritis characterized by the presence of small-diameter fibrils on electron microscopy (findings not directly described in provided text but implied by diagnosis). Glomeruli exhibit segmental capillary wall thickening with intraluminal red blood cells and red blood cell casts within adjacent tubules, consistent with active glomerulonephritis, as identified in periodic acid–Schiff and Masson trichrome-stained sections (×100 magnification).

**Subtle error introduced:**  
The phrase "consistent with active glomerulonephritis" implies a level of diagnostic certainty not fully supported by the described findings alone.

Microscopic examination reveals cystic dilation of the rete testis, characterized by irregular, variably sized cystic spaces lined by flattened to cuboidal, nonciliated epithelium. No cilia, papillary projections, or cytologic atypia are identified, consistent with benign rete cysts without evidence of neoplastic transformation.

Electron microscopy demonstrates diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes in glomerular capillary loops. No electron-dense deposits are identified. The ultrastructural findings are diagnostic of minimal-change disease.

Kidney, dog: Diffuse interstitial fibrosis and glomerular fibrosis are present. Morphological findings are consistent with chronic renal parenchymal damage involving both glomeruli and tubulointerstitial compartments, indicative of end-stage renal disease.
Renal biopsy demonstrates fibrin thrombi within an afferent arteriole, as identified on trichrome stain. The findings are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular injury characterized by fibrocellular crescents, as evidenced by light microscopy. The presence of nephrotic range proteinuria and positive c-ANCA serology confirms a diagnosis of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)–associated pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates diffuse and global coarse granular C3-dominant immune deposits involving the glomerular capillary walls and mesangium, consistent with immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis. The morphological findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular deposition of C3 complement component, with intense linear and mesangial ring-like staining along the glomerular basement membranes on immunofluorescence. These findings are diagnostic of dense deposit disease, a subtype of C3 glomerulopathy.

Light microscopy reveals granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by a poorly formed granuloma composed of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells of the foreign body type. The granulomatous inflammation is localized within the renal interstitium and is consistent with sarcoidosis.

Renal thrombotic microangiopathy, acute phase.

Histopathological findings reveal global thickening of glomerular capillary walls due to subendothelial expansion. Focal mesangiolysis and occasional capillary lumen thrombosis are present, consistent with active endothelial injury and diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy.

Expression of transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) is observed in the glomeruli of a patient with idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis who demonstrated a favorable clinical response. Immunohistochemical or in situ hybridization findings indicate TGF-β1 upregulation within glomerular structures, including mesangial and epithelial compartments, suggesting a direct pathogenic role for TGF-β1 in disease progression despite the clinical improvement.

Bone marrow biopsy findings demonstrate systemic mastocytosis with associated clonal plasma cell proliferation. Morphologic and molecular analyses confirm the presence of the KIT D816V mutation, which is diagnostic of systemic mastocytosis and indicates concurrent multiple myeloma due to the clonal plasma cells identified on biopsy.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis characterized by the presence of cellular crescents within Bowman's space. Glomerular capillary loops show segmental to circumferential involvement with associated necrotizing changes in some glomeruli, consistent with active anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is characterized by segmental glomerular tuft collapse, mesangial expansion, and adhesions between the glomerular tuft and Bowman's capsule (synechiae), as demonstrated on periodic acid-Schiff staining. These findings are diagnostic of neurofibromatosis type 1–associated nephropathy.

Intratubular calcification identified in renal tissue, confirmed by positive Von Kossa stain (100× magnification). The presence of nephrotoxic agents—including furosemide, orlistat, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs—is strongly indicative of drug-induced acute tubular necrosis, which directly promotes intratubular mineral deposition.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates findings diagnostic of IgA nephropathy in the setting of malignant hypertension and nephrotic-range proteinuria.

Light microscopy (PAS, silver, and trichrome stains) reveals 12 glomeruli, 7 of which are globally sclerotic. Three glomeruli exhibit segmental sclerosis with synechiae to Bowman’s capsule, consistent with advanced IgA nephropathy-related scarring.

Glomeruli exhibit diffuse global mesangial sclerosis with expansion of the mesangial matrix, resulting in complete obliteration of glomerular capillary lumina. Bowman’s spaces are consequently collapsed due to the surrounding sclerosis.

Renal biopsy demonstrates intraluminal translucent crystals consistent with calcium oxalate deposits, associated with tubular epithelial degeneration and a multinucleated foreign body giant cell reaction. These findings are diagnostic of primary hyperoxaluria.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates deposition of lightly eosinophilic, homogeneous material within the glomerular mesangial areas, as highlighted on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining at 400× magnification. The morphological appearance is diagnostic of amyloid deposition; confirmation with Congo red staining and polarization is recommended to support the diagnosis and determine amyloid subtype.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates interstitial fibrosis with tubular atrophy. A sclerotic glomerulus is present, exhibiting flocculo-capsular synechiae. These findings are diagnostic of monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease in a renal allograft.

Glycogen synthase kinase-3β (GSK-3β) expression is observed in renal allograft tissue exhibiting moderate interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IF/TA-II). Immunohistochemical or molecular expression of GSK-3β is present within tubular epithelial cells and interstitial compartments, and is diagnostic of active chronic antibody-mediated rejection.
Low-power photomicrograph demonstrates extensive tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, consistent with chronic allograft nephropathy, Banff category IFTA Grade III.
lesion_type: chronic allograft nephropathy, banff ifta grade iii

answer_text: Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular capillary engorgement with intracapillary erythrocyte accumulation and mesangiolysis, as evidenced by disruption of the mesangial matrix and loss of mesangial cellularity. These findings are diagnostic of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, with secondary features attributable to malignant hypertension and thrombotic microangiopathy.

Renal parenchyma is present adjacent to a well-circumscribed cystic lesion with a fibrous wall lined by heterogeneous epithelial and mesenchymal elements, including keratinizing squamous epithelium, glandular structures, and neural tissue, consistent with mature teratomatous differentiation. The lesion is confined within the renal parenchyma, demonstrating features diagnostic of a primary intrarenal teratoma.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original text appropriately uses “consistent with” to indicate that the histologic findings support—but do not definitively prove—a diagnosis of primary intrarenal teratoma, which is exceedingly rare and often requires exclusion of metastasis from an ovarian or testicular primary.

Renal biopsy demonstrates cortical fibrosis and tubular atrophy involving approximately 30–40% of the sampled cortex, with superimposed acute tubular injury. The findings are consistent with chronic parenchymal damage due to long-standing diabetic nephropathy, accompanied by acute tubular dysfunction.
lesion_type: chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy with acute tubular injury

answer_text: MMP-2 expression is observed in renal tissue with varying degrees of inflammatory infiltration, as demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining (EnVision method; original magnification ×200). This finding is diagnostic of chronic active antibody-mediated renal allograft rejection.
lesion_type: chronic active antibody-mediated renal allograft rejection

answer_text: TIMP-1 immunohistochemical expression is observed in renal tissue exhibiting moderate interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IF/TA grade II), consistent with chronic active antibody-mediated rejection. Staining distribution and intensity are assessed by EnVision method at ×200 magnification.

Light microscopy of the renal biopsy reveals glomerular crescents and fibrinoid necrosis, as indicated by the arrows. These findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease.

Histopathological examination reveals globally sclerosed glomerular tufts with segmental endocapillary hypercellularity. Tubular atrophy is present, accompanied by loss of tubular brush borders. These findings are diagnostic of immunoglobulin A nephropathy in the context of Graves’ disease.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates dysplastic glomeruli, characterized by abnormal glomerular architecture with irregular capillary formation and mesangial stromal hypercellularity. Moderate to marked interstitial fibrosis is present, involving the renal parenchyma diffusely and consistent with chronic allograft nephropathy.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The phrase “consistent with chronic allograft nephropathy” introduces unwarranted diagnostic certainty.

Kidney biopsy reveals glomerular injury consistent with anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody-mediated glomerulonephritis, despite the absence of detectable circulating antibodies. Histologic examination shows a glomerulus exhibiting nodular sclerosis accompanied by mesangiolysis and microaneurysm formation, features highly specific for anti-GBM disease.

Expression of MMP-2 and TIMP-1 is observed in renal tissue from patients with chronic active antibody-mediated renal allograft rejection, suggesting a direct pathogenic role for these molecules in the rejection process. C4d expression and renal histological features are evaluated and confirm the diagnosis of chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.

Kidney: Severe interstitial mononuclear cell infiltration associated with fibrosis, tubular necrosis, atrophy, dilation, and degenerative changes of the tubular epithelium. Focal congestion is present.

Microscopic examination reveals multiple irregular cystic spaces lined by flattened epithelial cells, which disrupt and displace the adjacent testicular parenchyma. The cysts are consistent with dysplastic changes involving the rete testis and suggest early neoplastic transformation.

Numerous foamy histiocytes are present, forming granulomatous aggregates within renal parenchyma. Scattered plasma cells and lymphocytes are interspersed among the histiocytes. The histomorphological features are diagnostic of xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates glomerular injury characterized by segmental fibrinoid necrosis and nodular sclerosis within a glomerulus. Findings are diagnostic of anti-glomerular basement membrane antibody-mediated glomerulonephritis; however, circulating anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies were not detected.

Renal biopsy shows interstitial infiltration by sheets of lymphocytes with relative sparing of the tubular basement membranes. Tubular epithelial cells exhibit atypical nuclei, diagnostic of viral cytopathic changes.
lesion_type: tubulointerstitial nephritis with viral cytopathic changes

answer_text: Renal cortex biopsy demonstrates tubulointerstitial nephritis characterized by a dense interstitial infiltrate composed of plasma cells, lymphocytes, macrophages, and occasional eosinophils on haematoxylin and eosin staining. The histological findings are diagnostic of immune complex–mediated tubulointerstitial nephritis in the setting of hypocomplementemia.

Renal biopsy revealed fifty glomeruli, one of which demonstrated global sclerosis. Adjacent to the sclerosed glomerulus, there was focal tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, accompanied by mild, localized interstitial inflammatory cell infiltration, consistent with chronic active pyelonephritis.

Electron microscopy demonstrates structured electron-dense deposits within the subendothelial space of glomerular capillary walls. The deposits consist of randomly oriented fibrils, measuring approximately 20–24 nm in diameter, diagnostic of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Histological examination of the testis reveals extensive hyalinization, tubular basement membrane thickening with luminal obliteration, and interstitial fibrosis. Scattered foci of Leydig cell hyperplasia are present, consistent with long-standing primary hypogonadism.

Hematoxylin and eosin staining demonstrates tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and thickened blood vessel walls. These findings are consistent with chronic parenchymal damage due to long-standing hypertension; however, specific etiologies cannot be determined based on morphology alone.

Electron microscopy reveals paramesangial electron-dense deposits and diffuse podocyte foot process effacement. These findings are characteristic of minimal change disease with concurrent IgA deposition, suggesting a pathogenic association between minimal change disease and IgA nephropathy.
lesion_type: minimal change disease with iga nephropathy

answer_text: Expression of transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) is observed in renal tissue from a patient with idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis who did not respond to therapy. Immunohistochemical or molecular analysis demonstrates upregulation of TGF-β1 within glomerular and/or tubulointerstitial compartments, indicating a direct causal role in fibrotic progression and treatment resistance.

Renal biopsy demonstrates crescentic glomerulonephritis with involvement of multiple glomeruli showing fibrocellular crescents. Crescent formation is characterized by circumferential or segmental proliferation of podocytes and infiltration of mononuclear inflammatory cells within Bowman’s space.

Renal biopsy shows focal segmental glomerulosclerosis and nephroangiosclerosis. Most glomeruli exhibit only minor morphological abnormalities, as demonstrated by Periodic acid–Schiff staining, consistent with early diabetic nephropathy.

Histomorphological examination of nephrectomy specimens following high-dose, high-energy shock wave lithotripsy revealed acute and subacute parenchymal injury. Findings included cortical and medullary hemorrhage, tubular epithelial cell necrosis, interstitial edema, and focal inflammatory infiltrates consistent with early pyelonephritis.
lesion_type: acute and subacute parenchymal injury with tubular necrosis and early pyelone

answer_text: Amyloid deposition consistent with AA amyloidosis involving renal parenchyma. Chronic inflammatory etiology confirmed, with history of subcutaneous drug abuse directly causative of sustained systemic inflammation leading to AA amyloid formation.

Electron microscopy of renal tissue demonstrates glomerular basement membranes with diffuse and uniform lamellation, resulting in a "basket-weave" appearance diagnostic of Alport syndrome. Segmental foot process effacement is present, accompanied by microvillus transformation of podocytes.

Atypical presentation of sarcoidosis with renal involvement. Granulomatous interstitial nephritis characterized by non-caseating granulomas involving the renal interstitium, consistent with direct tubular epithelial injury by sarcoid granulomas.

Prussian blue stain demonstrates strong diffuse positivity within renal tubules, characterized by coarse blue granules, indicative of iron deposition. The findings are diagnostic of renal tubular hemosiderosis secondary to chronic intravascular hemolysis.

Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed granular deposition of IgA within the mesangial areas of glomeruli, consistent with a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy. No staining was noted in the capillary walls or other immunoreactants were reported.

Whole slide image analysis morphometry demonstrates spatial correlations among measured pathological variables in renal cortical and medullary regions. Correlation clustering reveals close associations between interstitial fibrosis, tubular epithelial mass, microvascular density, and glomerular architecture.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The original correctly refers to “microanatomical architecture,” a general term encompassing the fine structural organization of both cortical and medullary compartments, including tubules, vessels, and interstitium.

Renal needle biopsy reveals a microscopic papillary neoplasm. The lesion is characterized by papillary architecture lined by high columnar epithelial cells exhibiting mild cytological atypia, consistent with low-grade papillary renal cell carcinoma (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×400 magnification).

Kidney biopsy demonstrates mesangial proliferative glomerular changes on light microscopy (hematoxylin and eosin stain). Electron microscopy reveals the presence of glomerular deposits with small fibrils, diagnostic of fibrillary glomerulonephritis.

Segmental patch of glomerulosclerosis identified in renal parenchyma, as demonstrated by periodic acid-Schiff staining at ×200 magnification. The finding is consistent with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis and, given its presence, is sufficient to establish a diagnosis of chronic kidney disease and contraindicate live kidney donation, even in the absence of corroborating clinical, laboratory, or functional data.

Renal cortex: The interstitium is expanded by edema and a mixed inflammatory infiltrate composed of lymphocytes, macrophages, and numerous eosinophils. These findings are diagnostic of acute tubulointerstitial nephritis due to drug hypersensitivity.

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular cellular crescent formation, as evidenced by light microscopy (hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×100). Crescents involve Bowman’s space and are composed of proliferating parietal epithelial cells and infiltrating mononuclear inflammatory cells, consistent with active anti-glomerular basement membrane disease.

Moderate inflammatory infiltration is present within the renal allograft parenchyma. Immunohistochemical evaluation demonstrates expression of GSK-3β in renal tubular epithelial cells, which is consistent with acute T-cell–mediated rejection.

MMP-2 immunoreactivity is demonstrated in renal tissue from patients with chronic active antibody-mediated renal allograft rejection, specifically within areas exhibiting varying degrees of interstitial inflammatory infiltration. Immunohistochemical staining (EnVision method; original magnification ×200) reveals expression patterns consistent with active tubular epithelial injury and extracellular matrix remodeling activity.

**Explanation of introduced error:**  
The addition of “active tubular epithelial injury” as a conclusion drawn from MMP-2 immunoreactivity constitutes an unwarranted diagnostic certainty.

The specimen demonstrates a well-circumscribed intrarenal neoplasm composed of multiple mature germ cell layers. The lesion is lined by stratified squamous epithelium exhibiting keratinization, with associated sebaceous glands evident within the stroma, consistent with a dermoid cyst arising from misplaced pluripotent germ cells during renal embryogenesis.

Light microscopy demonstrates crescentic glomerulonephritis with segmental to circumferential cellular crescents involving a subset of glomeruli. Tubulointerstitial evaluation reveals lymphocytic infiltration and interstitial fibrosis, with associated tubular atrophy consistent with chronic active antibody-mediated rejection.

Pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, diagnostic of ANCA-associated vasculitis.  
Mild tubular atrophy is present.

Kidney biopsy shows tubular atrophy and chronic interstitial inflammation characterized by a mononuclear infiltrate consisting of lymphocytes and plasma cells, as demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections at 40x magnification. These findings are diagnostic of lupus nephritis with established chronic parenchymal injury.

Light microscopy demonstrates diffuse acute tubular injury characterized by attenuation of proximal tubular epithelial cells and increased mitotic activity, consistent with regenerative changes. Focal interstitial inflammation is present and appears to be the primary driver of the tubular injury.

Severe arterial and arteriolar sclerosis with marked intimal fibrosis resulting in luminal narrowing. Small arteries exhibit hyaline deposits, evident on periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) staining, consistent with diabetic arteriolosclerosis.

Kidney biopsy demonstrates segmental to global glomerular tuft collapse with loss of capillary architecture. Podocytes exhibit hypertrophy, hyperplasia, and poor cellular cohesion, with intracapillary accumulation of enlarged podocytes and formation of pseudocrescents, consistent with collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS).

Renal biopsy demonstrates glomerular expression of the membrane attack complex (C5b-9), indicating activation of the terminal complement pathway within glomerular structures. The presence of C5b-9 deposition is observed in the context of idiopathic rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (IRPGN) and is diagnostic of complement-mediated injury, correlating with a favorable clinical response in this case.

Renal biopsy specimen demonstrates acute tubular injury with intratubular crystalline deposits exhibiting birefringence under polarized light. The crystals display a characteristic "fan-shaped" morphology, diagnostic of calcium oxalate nephropathy.

Proliferative glomerulonephritis with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition in the renal allograft.  
Light microscopy demonstrates mild chronic changes characterized by tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis, consistent with chronic antibody-mediated rejection.
lesion_type: proliferative glomerulonephritis with monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition

answer_text: Diffuse lymphoid infiltrate extensively invades renal parenchyma, obliterating tubular structures and resulting in tubular atrophy with loss of epithelial lining. Residual tubules demonstrate epithelial cells with atypical nuclear features, consistent with malignant transformation.

High-power histological examination of the skin biopsy demonstrates a single slender spirochete within the dermis, consistent with *Treponema pallidum*. The presence of this organism confirms the diagnosis of acquired syphilis.
